You can also edit automation and controller data in lanes below the track’s main playlist view. For details on editing automation data for audio tracks, see Chapter 53, “Automation.” Setting Track Views in the Track View set to Clips for MIDI track Edit Window Notes View To set the Track View:
(MIDI Only) Click the Track View selector for the track and  With the Track View set to Notes, you can insert select the view from the pop-up menu.
and edit MIDI notes on MIDI and Instrument tracks.
The track displays the new view. If the track is part of an active Edit Group, all tracks in the group are set to the new view.
Automation and Controller Views When an audio, Auxiliary Input, Routing Folder, Master Fader, or Instrument track is displayed as Volume, Pan, or another automated control, or when a MIDI or Instrument track is set to one of the continuous controller types (such as or Volume Bend), the data for that track appears in the Pitch form of a line graph with a series of editable break- Audio Track View selector points. The breakpoints can be dragged to modify the automation data, and new breakpoints can be inserted with the Pencil tool or a Grabber tool.
MIDI and Instrument tracks also provide Velocity view for editing MIDI velocities.
Auxiliary Track View selector Track View set to Pan for audio track 254 Pro Tools Reference Guide Master Fader Track View selector Routing Folder Track View selector Changing Track Views For audio, Auxiliary Input, MIDI, and Instrument tracks, you can change to the next or previous VCA Master Track View selector Track View, or toggle between pre-defined com- mon views.
Changing to Previous or Next Track View When changing to the next or previous Track View, Track View list ordering is maintained as shown in the Track View selector.
Track Views at the beginning of the list (such as Blocks for audio or MIDI tracks) cannot be changed to the previous Track View.
Tracks Views at the end of the list (such as a MIDI Track View selector MIDI controllers option) cannot be changed to the next Track View.
To change to the previous or next Track View:
1 Click in the track you want to change. To change views on multiple tracks, Shift-click or drag the Selector tool to select additional tracks, or select a group.
2 Do one of the following:
• To change to the previous or next Track View on all selected tracks, press Control+Com- mand (Mac) or Control+Start (Windows) and the Left or Right Arrow key.
• To change to the previous or next Track View on all tracks, press Command+Option+Con- Instrument Track View selector trol (Mac) or Control+Alt+Start (Windows) and the Left or Right Arrow key.
Toggling Common Track Views Master Views for Tracks The most common editing view for audio tracks Audio, MIDI, and Instrument tracks have Track are Waveform and Volume view. For more information, Tracks List) provides commands that allow you to see “Tracks List” on page 841.
show or hide all tracks, tracks currently selected on-screen, or specific types of tracks (Folders, au- Showing and Hiding Tracks dio, Auxiliary Input, Master Fader, MIDI, Instru- ment, VCA Master, or Inactive or Frozen tracks).
The Mix and Edit windows are linked in terms of which tracks are shown or hidden. However, the Score Editor window and MIDI Editor windows are all unique in terms of which tracks are shown or hidden. For example, hiding a specific track in the Edit window also hides it in the Mix window, but not in the Score Editor or MIDI Editor win- dows. Also, hiding a specific track in a MIDI Edi- tor window hides it only in that MIDI Editor win- Show Only option dow.

Create and Assign New Track from Track Output Pro Tools lets you create a new Auxiliary Input, Audio, or Instrument track from the Output selec- tor and automatically assign the Output of the orig- inal track to the Input of the new track using an available internal mix bus.
To create a new track from a track output:
1 On an existing track, click the Output selector for the track and select New Track.
2 In the New Track dialog, select the Width, Type, and Time Base for the new track.
3 Type a Name for the new track.
4 Select (or deselect) whether you want the new track to be created next to the current track.
New Auxiliary Input track with audio track output 5 Click OK.

Searching Track Input and Track Output Path Selectors To search for a path in an Input or Output selector:
Click the path selector for the input or output you want to assign and do one of the following:
• Type the first character of the path name you want.
• Click Search.
• Press Tab.
Searching a path selector—single path found by typing the first letters of its name 4 Do one of the following:

• Click anywhere outside the menu.

Assigning MIDI Track Input Pro Tools lets you assign specific MIDI ports and channels to a MIDI track input. The default selec- No free voice available for a lower priority audio track tion of All receives all incoming MIDI data from all ports on all channels. Use the MIDI Input selec- tor to specify a MIDI port and channel for input.
Tracks do not play back when they are inac- tive or their voice assignment is set to Off.
For information on assigning MIDI input to Instrument tracks, see “Assigning MIDI In- When working with more than the maximum num- put and Output for Instrument Tracks” on ber of voiced audio tracks allowed by your system, page 272.
you can only play back audio from higher-num- bered (lower priority) tracks by changing the track priority by doing any of the following:
• Make a lower-numbered track inactive (click the Track Name and select Track > Make Inactive).
• Set the voice assignment in a lower-numbered track to Off (click the Voice selector and select Off).

Color Coding for Tracks, Clips, Markers, and Groups Separate colors can be assigned to audio and MIDI clips, tracks, markers, and groups. Clips shown in Waveform and Block Views in the Edit window are drawn in color. Tracks shown in the Tracks List, Groups List, and Mix and Edit windows have associated color bars.
Color Bars Color coding at the track level is displayed using color bars, as follows:
Mix Window Track colors are displayed in hori- zontal color bars that appear above each channel strip, and below the track name.
Edit Window Track colors are displayed in vertical color bars that appear to the left of each track.
Tracks List Track colors are displayed in vertical color bars that appear to the left of each track name.
Groups List Track colors are displayed in vertical color bars that appear to the left of each Group Name.

To use the Hold button to assign the same color to multiple items:
Click the button to enable it. The but- 1 Hold Hold ton becomes white, and the currently selected color is now highlighted with a wider white out- line.
Select additional tracks or clips to which you want to assign the same color. Because the Hold button is enabled, the Color Palette does not fol- low item selection; it remains (or “holds”) at the last currently assigned color.
Click the assigned color again to assign it to the new selection of tracks or clips. Use the Apply to pop-up menu to determine which se- Selected lected elements are affected.

Chapter 14: Folder Tracks Pro Tools provides Folder tracks to help you orga- Routing Folder Tracks Let you organize and man- nize both track layout and signal routing in your age tracks in your sessions, and they also provide projects and sessions. Folder tracks essentially audio routing and processing. You can route audio function as containers for other tracks of any type. from member tracks through its input, apply plug- A Folder track can be closed to hide all of the ins, use sends, assign track automation, and output tracks it contains, or opened to show all contained audio much like an Auxiliary Input track.
tracks (member tracks). Thus, Folder tracks are a Tracks do not need to be contained by a powerful tool for organizing tracks (by stems, in- Routing Folder track in order to route audio struments, and so on) so that you can more easily through it. You can bus audio from any track navigate complex sessions from a high level over- to the audio input of a Routing Folder track view.
as desired.
Folder tracks can hold other Folder tracks up to nine layers deep. A Folder track that contains an- Basic Folder Tracks other Folder track is a parent Folder, and the Folder it contains is a child Folder. Folder tracks that are Basic Folder tracks can contain multiple tracks of in the same layer are sibling Folders.
any type (including other Folder tracks). Use Basic There are two types of Folder tracks: Basic and Folder tracks to organize tracks in your session. In Routing. the Edit window, Basic Folder tracks display an Overview of clips on all member tracks. Any edits Basic Folder Tracks Let you organize and manage you make on Folder tracks Overview apply to all tracks in your sessions, but without any of the spe- member tracks (see “Editing Folder Tracks in cial routing capabilities that Routing Folder tracks Overview” on page 292).
provide. A Basic Folder track is like a container for other tracks (of any type). It is especially useful in large sessions with many tracks. For example, if you have sixteen drum tracks in a session, you can add them to a Basic Folder track which you can then close to free up screen space so you can view other tracks in the Edit and Mix windows without scrolling. The solo and mute buttons on Basic Folder tracks affect all member tracks, but other- wise Basic Folder tracks do not provide any audio or MIDI routing for member tracks.
To create a Basic Folder track:
1 Do one of the following:
• Choose Track > New.
• Press Command+Shift+N (Mac) or Control+Shift+N (Windows).
• Right-click the Track name of any track in the Tracks List, or Edit or Mix windows, and choose New.
2 In the New Tracks dialog, select Basic Folder from the Track Type selector.
New Tracks dialog, Basic Folder track selected 3 Type a descriptive Name for the Folder track, if desired.
4 Click Create.
Basic Folder Track Controls Basic Folder tracks provide Solo and Mute controls that apply to all member tracks. They also provide Au- dio and MIDI signal indicators, which light when any member track is playing back audio or MIDI respec- tively.
Audio signal indicator MIDI signal indicator Mute button Solo button Open/Close Folder track Basic Folder track, Edit window controls and indicators 282 Pro Tools Reference Guide Lights green when any Audio Signal Indicator member track plays back audio (even if the track is muted). If audio from any member track clips, the border of the indicator turns either amber or red.
Solo button MIDI Signal Indicator Lights amber when any Mute button member track plays back MIDI.
Audio signal indicator Mute When selected (lit), all member tracks are muted.
MIDI signal indicator Solo When selected (lit), all member tracks remain unmuted (except for any member tracks that were explicitly muted separately) while all other tracks that are not also soloed are muted.
Open/Close Folder Click to open or close the Folder track. When closed, all member tracks are hidden. When open, all member tracks are shown.
In the Edit window, member tracks are indented to Open/Close Folder track show that they are contained by the Folder track. In the Mix window, member tracks are bracketed by Basic Folder track, Mix window controls and indicators the Folder track’s track color.
To open or close any Folder tracks that con- tain the Edit cursor or are included in the Edit selection, press Shift+F.
Indentation indicating member tracks of the open Open/Close Folder track folder track Edit window, Basic Folder track open Outline indicating member tracks of the open folder track Open/Close Folder track Mix window, Basic Folder track open 284 Pro Tools Reference Guide Routing Folder Tracks Routing Folder tracks function just like Basic Folder tracks and can contain multiple tracks of any type (in- cluding other Folder tracks). They also let you route and process audio in the same way as Auxiliary Input tracks. Routing Folder tracks are especially useful for summing and stem-mixing workflows. In the Edit window, Routing Folder tracks provide Track Automation views in addition to Overview. Any edits you make on Folder tracks Overview apply to all member tracks (see “Editing Folder Tracks in Overview” on page 292).
To create a Routing Folder track:
Do one of the following:
• Choose Track > New.
• Press Command+Shift+N (Mac) or Control+Shift+N (Windows).
• Right-click the Track name of any track in the Tracks List, or Edit or Mix windows, and choose New.
In the New Tracks dialog, select Routing Folder from the Track Type selector.
New Tracks dialog, stereo Routing Folder track selected 3 Select the desired channel width from the Track Format selector.
4 Select the desired Track Time Base.
5 Type a descriptive Name for the Folder track.
6 Click Create.
Routing Folder Track Controls Routing Folder tracks provide the same controls and indicators as Basic Folder tracks as well as the same controls as Auxiliary Input tracks. However, be sure to note the difference in Mute behavior between Basic and Routing Folder tracks.

Folder tracks can be opened or closed to show or hide all member tracks. When opened, tracks con- Click the reveal triangle in the Tracks List.

To convert an Auxiliary Input track to a Routing Click a Folder icon or reveal triangle in the Tracks Folder track:
List using the following key modifiers:
1 Select the Auxiliary Input track that you want to • Command-click (Mac) or Control-click convert in the Edit or Mix window, or in the (Windows) to open or close the entire Folder Tracks List.
hierarchy.
2 Do one of the following:
• Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) • Choose Track > Convert Aux to Routing to open or close all Folder tracks in the same Folder.
layer (sibling Folders).
• Right-click the name of the Auxiliary Input • Command-Option-click (Mac) or Control- track in the Edit or Mix window, or in the Alt-click (Windows) to open or close all Tracks List and choose Convert Aux to Rout- Folder tracks in the same layer (sibling Fold- ing Folder.
ers) and below (nested, or child Folders).
• Option-Shift-click (Mac) or Alt-Shift-click (Windows) to open or close all selected Folder tracks.
In order to avoid confusion when a large number of tracks are being shown or hidden, Pro Tools does its best to keep the tracks that are currently on-screen visible while Folders are being opened or closed.
Adding Tracks to and Removing Tracks from Folder Tracks You can add tracks to and remove tracks from Folder tracks in several different ways.
To add one or more tracks of any type to a new Folder track:
Select one or more tracks in the Edit or Mix window, or in the Tracks List that you want to add to a new Folder track.
Do one of the following:
• Choose Track > Move To New Folder.
• Press Command+Option+Shift+N (Mac) or Control+Alt+Shift+N (Windows).
• Right-click the Track name of a selected track and choose Move To > New Folder.
In the resulting Move To New Folder dialog, do the following:
• Select Basic Folder or Routing Folder for the Type selector.
• If you selected Routing Folder, select the Format, Time Base, and, if desired, select Route Tracks to New Folder.
• Name the new Folder track.
• Click Create.
Move To New Folder dialog A new Basic or Routing Folder track is created and the selected tracks are moved to the new Folder track.
If you created a new Routing Folder track, a new internal bus named after the new folder track is created and assigned to its track input. If you selected the Route Tracks to New Folder option, the outputs of any tracks you are moving to the new Routing Folder track are also assigned to that bus.
A new bus is always created for a new Routing Folder and assigned to its input. The outputs of new mem- ber tracks are only automatically routed to that bus if the Route Tracks to New Folder option is enabled.
290 Pro Tools Reference Guide To add one or more tracks of any type to an exiting Folder track:
1 Select one or more tracks in the Edit or Mix window, or in the Tracks List that you want to add to an existing Folder track.
2 Do one of the following:
• Click and drag any of the selected tracks to the Folder track in either the Edit or Mix win- dow, or in the Tracks list. Orange lines indi- cate the target location and level.
• Right-click the Track name of any selected track and choose Move To > <Folder track name>.
Moving a Folder track also moves all of its member tracks with it.
Dragged track added to Folder To remove one or more tracks from a Folder track:
If necessary, open the Folder track to view all contained tracks.
Select one or more tracks in the Edit or Mix window, or in the Tracks List that you want to remove from the Folder track.
Do one of the following:
• Click and drag any of the selected tracks out of the Folder to blank space or to another track location in either the Edit or Mix win- dow, or in the Tracks list. Orange lines indi- cate the target location of dragged tracks.
• Right-click the Track name of any selected track and choose Level.
Move To > Top Dragging a track to add it to a Folder track Dragged track removed from Folder Dragging a track to remove it from a Folder track Editing Folder Tracks in Overview In the Edit window, Folder tracks provide the unique Overview track view that displays blocks represent- ing clips on member tracks. This is the only track view available for Basic Folder tracks. Routing Folder tracks also let you view and edit in Track Automation views in addition to Overview.
Overview of member tracks Open Folder track in Overview 292 Pro Tools Reference Guide Any Edit selection you make on a Folder track in Of course you can make explicit Edit selections in Overview includes any track material within the member tracks and apply edits only to specific range of the selection on all member tracks. Edit- member tracks.
ing in Overview on Folder tracks applies to track Track-based edits (such as changing the color of a material on all member tracks.
track), however, are not propagated to member When a Folder is open, Edit selections made on a tracks and only apply to the selected Folder track.
Folder track in Overview are implicitly extended Press Option+Control+Shift+P (Mac) or to all member tracks. Most edit commands can be Alt+Start+Shift+P (Windows) to extend the used without any restrictions (such as Clear, Cut, Edit selection up through member tracks.
Copy, and Paste) and apply to all member tracks.
Press Option+Control+Shift+; (Mac) or Alt+Start+Shift+; (Windows) to extend the Edit selection down through member tracks.
The following are disallowed for implicit selec- tions on member tracks whether the Folder track is open or closed:
• Commit (Track menu) • Toggling bypass of inserts and sends Edit Selection on Folder track in Overview The following are restricted on closed Folder tracks:
• Select/Split Notes (Event Operations) • Step Input (Event Operations) • Record Enable • Input Monitor • Special edit commands for plug-in automation • Real-Time Properties • Elastic Properties Clear applied to all member tracks When a Folder is closed, Edit selections made in Overview are implicitly extended to all member tracks. However, not all edit commands are avail- able for closed Folder tracks, and some restrictions apply. Commands that are unavailable for closed Folder tracks are grayed out in their respective menus.
Modifier Keys for Folder Track Selections The following key modifiers can be used to make track selections in the Edit and Mix windows, as well as the Tracks List:
• Option-Control-click (Mac) or Alt-Start- click (Windows) any track in the Edit or Mix windows, or in the Tracks List to select all tracks in the same layer (siblings).
• Command-Option-Control-click (Mac) or Control-Alt-Start-click (Windows) a track in the Tracks List to select (or deselect) siblings in different Folders.
• Option-Control-Shift-click (Mac) or Alt- Start-Shift-click (Windows) to extend the se- lection to sibling tracks.
Editing Routing Folder Track Automation Routing Folder tracks let you view and edit track automation, such as Volume, either in the corre- sponding track view or in Automation lanes. Rout- ing Folder track automation is independent of any automation on member tracks, and editing Routing Folder track automation does not affect the auto- mation on member tracks.

Track Data to Recall Pro Tools lets you specify what Track and Session data to recall with the Track Preset. These settings can be modified in a Workspace browser for any selected Track Preset (see “Track Presets in Workspace Browsers” on page 305).
Track Data to Recall dialog Press Command+R (Mac) or Control+R (Windows) to open the Track Data To Recall dialog from the Save Track Preset dialog.
When recalling a Track Preset by any means, press Control (Mac) or Start (Windows) to open and edit the Track Data to Import dialog. You can then specify what Track Data you want to recall.
Clips and media will only be stored with a Track Preset if the Clips and Media option is enabled.

300 Pro Tools Reference Guide Track Presets in the New Tracks Dialog To create a new track from a Track Preset:
1 Choose Tracks > New Track.
2 From the Track Type selector, choose Track Presets and select the Track Preset Category (such as “Avid”).
Selecting the Track Preset Category 3 Select the Track Preset.
Selecting a Track Preset Click Create.
The Track Preset is recalled into the session as a new track (or as new tracks) according to the current Track Data to Recall settings.
Track Presets in the New Tracks Dialog from Track Output Selector To create a new track from a Track Preset using the Track Output Selector:
1 Click the Output selector of a track and choose New Track.
Creating a New Track from a Track Output selector 2 From the Track Type selector, choose Track Presets and select the Track Preset Category (such as “Avid”).
Selecting the Track Preset Category Select the Track Preset.
Creating a new track from a Track Preset 4 Click Create.
The Track Preset is recalled into the session as a new track (or as new tracks) according to the current Track Data to Recall settings.
302 Pro Tools Reference Guide Track Presets in the Tracks Recalling Inserts from Track Right-Click Menu Presets To configure a track from a Track Preset using the Pro Tools lets you recall Inserts saved in a Track Track Right-click menu:
Preset. This lets you quickly configure any track 1 Right-click any Track Name in the Tracks List, with common effects chains saved in Track Pre- or in the Edit or Mix windows.
sets.
2 From the Right-click menu, choose Recall Plug-ins recalled from Track Presets adapt to Track Preset and select the Track Preset you the format of the current track when possible.
want to recall.
For example, if a Track Preset was saved as stereo, but you are recalling Inserts from it to a 5.1 track, any plug-ins stored with that Track Preset that support 5.1 are recalled as 5.1 in- serts. However, if a plug-in does not support the track format (for example, in this case, if there is no 5.1 version of a plug-in saved with the Track Preset), it is not recalled.
Selecting a Track Preset from the Track Right-click menu To recall inserts from a Track Preset, do one of the Recalling Track Presets to following:
Sends Drag and drop a Track Preset from a Workspace  browser to the Inserts view of an existing track.
Pro Tools lets you recall Sends configurations saved in a Track Preset. This lets you quickly con- Choose a Track Preset from the  Recall Inserts figure any track with common send configurations submenu in the Inserts selector of an existing saved in Track Presets.
track.
To recall sends from a Track Preset, do one of the following:
Drag and drop a Track Preset from a Workspace  browser to the Sends view of an existing track.
Choose a Track Preset from the Recall Sends  submenu in the Sends selector of an existing track.
Recalling inserts from a Track Preset Recalling sends from a Track Preset 304 Pro Tools Reference Guide Track Presets in Workspace Browsers Workspace browsers provide easy access to all of your Track Presets from the Track Presets alias in the Locations pane. Subdirectories of the Track Presets location let you organize Track Presets by Category— each subdirectory appears as a Track Preset Category in the New Tracks dialog. Search for Track Presets by name, category, or by matching metadata tags (just like with Soundbase) in any Workspace browser.
To open new Workspace browser focused on Track Presets:
Choose Window > New Workspace > Track Presets.
 To recall Track Presets from the Workspace into a session and create new tracks, do one of the following:
Drag and drop a Track Preset to the Tracks List or to blank space in the Mix or Edit windows.
 Double-click a Track Preset.
 Right-click a Track Preset and choose Recall Track Preset.
 Track Preset selected in a Workspace browser showing Track Data to Recall Tagging Track Presets Pro Tools lets you add metadata tags to Track Presets just like with Soundbase. For more information about working with tags, see the Pro Tools Reference Guide.
Searching Track Presets Pro Tools lets you search for track presets in a Workspace browser using basic or advanced search criteria, or by searching on metadata tags. For more information about searching in Workspace browsers, see “Searching Items” on page 357.
Searching Track Presets with Tags in a Workspace Filtering Track Data when Recalling Track Presets from Workspace When a Track Preset is selected in the search pane of a Workspace browser, the Track Data to Recall settings for the selected Track Preset appears in its own pane on the right side of the Workspace browser.
These settings are the same as those in the Track Data to Recall dialog. You can edit the Track Data to Recall settings for the selected Track Preset in the Workspace browser. The Track Preset is updated with any changes you make. These settings are applied when you recall the Track Preset from the Workspace browser or when you create a new track (Track > New) from the Track Preset.
For more information about the Track Data to Recall settings, see “Track Data to Recall” on page 298.

command. Similarly, you can import a selection of multiple Track Presets at once.

Import Track window to recall Sends configurations from the All selected Track Presets are imported to create Track Preset. See also “Recalling Track Presets to new tracks in your session or project.
Sends” on page 304.
Dragging to a Track Name Drag and drop a Track Preset from a Workspace browser to a Track Name in the Edit or Mix win- dow to configure that track based on the Track Pre- set. If the Track Preset contains more than one track, on the first track stored with the Track Preset is recalled.
Press Option+Shift (Mac) or Alt+Shift (Win- dows) when dragging and dropping to config- ure all tracks based on the Track Preset.
Chapter 16: Grouping Tracks Pro Tools provides a relative grouping function for Mix Groups, Edit Groups, and linking tracks and their controls. Mix/Edit Groups Mix Groups only affect mixing functions and Edit Groups only affect editing. Mix/Edit Groups link Grouping Tracks the grouping functions of the Mix Group and the Edit Group.
Groups are useful for editing several tracks in ex- actly the same way, or for mixing several tracks Edit Groups (such as a pair of stereo tracks or a submix) while keeping them at the same relative volume level.
Edit Groups affect the following items in the Edit and MIDI Editor windows:
Pro Tools provides the following grouping • Track View features:
• Up to 104 different groups are available, ar- • Track Height ranged in 4 banks of 26 Group IDs.
• Track Timebase • Groups can be nested (subgroups within • Audio and MIDI editing functions groups).
• Automation editing functions • Grouped faders or controllers preserve their lev- els relative to each other.
• Groups are assignable to an available VCA Mas- ter track.
Mix Groups Grouping Limitations Mix Groups can be set to affect the following Grouping does not affect these parameters:
items:
• Voice assignment • Main Mute • Output assignment • Solo • Inserting plug-ins • Send Level • Elastic Audio plug-ins • Send Mute • Main Volume Selectable Group Attributes • Main Pan You can select which parameters, or attributes, are • Main LFE Level (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio linked in groups by the following methods:
only) • By making the group an Edit Group, a Mix • Record Enable Group, or both (Mix/Edit Group).
• Input Monitoring • With Mix and Mix/Edit Groups, by selecting • Automation Mode from a list of attributes for the group.
• Object Controls (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio • With Mix Groups in Pro Tools Ultimate and Stu- with Dolby dio only, by choosing whether the selected attri- Atmos only) butes apply globally to all groups or to • Send Pan individual groups.
• Send LFE Level (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio only) • Plug-In Controls • Plug-In Bypass In Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio, when the Main Pan attribute is enabled for groups, grouped behavior applies to the Link, Front inverse, Rear inverse and Front/Rear inverse controls in stereo and multichannel panner windows.

314 Pro Tools Reference Guide Modifying Groups To modify a group:
1 Do one of the following:
• Choose from the Groups List Modify Groups menu.
• In the Mix window, click the Group ID indicator on a track and choose Modify from the pop-up menu.
• Right-click the Group name in the Groups List and choose Modify from the pop-up menu.
2 In the Groups dialog, choose the group you want to modify from the ID pop-up menu.
3 Change any of the following for the current group:
• Group name • Group type (Edit, Mix, or Mix/Edit) • VCA status • status Follows Global Modify ALL Group dialog • Track membership For Mix or Mix/Edit Groups, you can change • Attributes any of the following:
• items Linked Within Group • status Follows Globals Click OK.
4 • Mix Attributes 4 Click OK.
To modify the settings for the “All” group:
1 Right-click the “All” group name in the Groups Deleting Groups List and choose Modify from the pop-up menu.
One or all groups can be deleted at a time.
2 In the Group dialog, select Edit, Mix, or Mix/Edit to change the settings for the “All” group. If you Deleting a group cannot be undone.
select Edit only or Mix only, the “All” group will apply only to that Group type.
To delete a single group, do one of the following:
In the Mix window, click the Group ID indica-  tor on a track and choose Delete from the pop- up menu.
Right-click the Group name in the Groups List  and choose Delete from the pop-up menu.
To delete all currently active groups Setting Group Attributes Choose Delete Active Groups from the Groups  List menu. When creating a Mix Group or a Mix/Edit Group, you can select the Mix window parameters that The “All” group cannot be deleted.
will be linked for that group. These linked param- eters are the attributes of the group.
Duplicating Groups You can select attributes in the Globals page and You can duplicate a group and modify its settings then set individual groups to follow the Global set- in order to quickly set up a mix.
tings, or you can select attributes for groups indi- vidually.

Do one of the following:
Selecting Attributes in the • Click the Group ID indicator on a track and Globals Page choose from the pop-up menu.

• Right-click the Group name in the Groups List While creating or modifying a group, click Glo- and choose Duplicate from the pop-up menu.
bals in the Group dialog.

• Group name • Group type (Edit, Mix, or Mix/Edit) • VCA status (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio only) • Follows Global status (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio only) • Track membership • Attributes (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio only) • Linked Within Group items (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio only) Click OK.

your session.

1 While creating or modifying a Mix Group or a Mix/Edit Group, do one of the following:

• Enable to follow the base set of Follow Globals attributes. The Attributes page grays out to indi- cate that the group is following the selections in the Globals page.
For information on selecting attributes, see “Selecting Group Attributes” on page 317.

To enable the Edit Groups List Keyboard Focus, do one of the following:
To enable a group:
Click the Keyboard Focus button in upper right  In the Groups List, click the name of the group  of the Edit Groups List.
you want to enable. The name is highlighted to indicate that it is enabled. Press Command+Option+3 (Mac) or Con-  trol+Alt+3 (Windows).
To enable additional groups, click their names in the Groups List.
Moving a fader of a group member causes all other group members to move relative to it. If a fader be- longs to multiple groups, and the groups conflict when faders are moved, the fader will follow the top-most or “parent” group to which it belongs.
To disable a group:
Groups List Keyboard Focus enabled In the Groups List, click the name of the group  To enable and disable groups using the Edit and you want to disable. The name is unhighlighted Mix Groups List Keyboard Focus:
to indicate that it is not enabled.
With Groups List Keyboard Focus enabled,  type the Group ID letter (a–z) to automatically enable or disable the corresponding group.
Temporarily Isolating Control of Grouped Control Offsets an Item from Group Operation When the following controls are grouped with off- You can temporarily isolate control of a group sets and moved to their extremes, relative offsets item from group operation by Right-clicking on are preserved when the controls are moved back the item.
from their extremes:
You can also temporarily suspend group • Main Volume behavior for a track by Control-clicking • Main Pan (Mac) or Start-clicking (Windows) a group function. • Send Level • Send Pan The following items can be isolated from group operation:
For example, when a grouped Volume fader is • Pan slider moved to its maximum value, any other faders in that group that had higher values will remember • Channel Record Enable their relative offset whenever the first fader is • Channel TrackInput pulled down again.
• Send Pan slider In Automation views, this “overflow” is indicated • Channel Volume fader on the automation playlist by blue automation breakpoints at the extremes of the automation • Channel Pan slider playlist.
• Channel Mute button • Channel Solo button Setting Group Pan Controls to • Channel Record Enable button Ignore Offsets • Channel TrackInput button By default, offsets are preserved for grouped pan • Send fader controls. In some workflows, it is desirable to have grouped pan controls match absolute values rather • Send Pan slider than preserve offsets.
To set grouped pan controls to ignore offsets:
Choose Setup > Preferences and click Mixing.
Select the Use Absolute Pan Linking option.
When this option is enabled, grouped pan controls will snap to the absolute value of the pan control that is being adjusted.

Clips List Menu The Clips List menu provides tools to search, se- lect, sort, export, clear, and manage items in the Clips List.
To access the Clips List menu:
In the Edit window, click the Clips List menu.
 Click Show/Hide Clips List but- ton to hide Clips List Clips List All clips of all types that are recorded, imported, or created by editing appear in the Clips List. Items can be dragged from the list to tracks and arranged in any order. You can also preview audio and MIDI clips and clip groups in the Clips List.
Clips List menu Displaying Clips in the Clips List In the Clips List, whole-file audio clips are dis- played in bold, and stereo and multichannel audio The Clips List can show all clip types (audio, clips can be expanded to display individual chan- MIDI, video, Groups, Auto-Created), or only cer- nels.
tain clip types. This is useful for isolating the type of clips you want to work with while editing and If the Clips List Selection Follows Edit Selection arranging. Rules for Stereo and Multichannel Clips To audition through all channels of the main audition path:
For stereo and multichannel audio clips to be Shift-Option-click (Mac) or Shift-Alt-click  shown as collective clips, the component clips (Windows) on the signal in the Clips List.
must be the same length. If an existing stereo or multichannel clip has been dragged onto multiple mono tracks and edited such a way that one or Stereo and Multichannel more components are no longer the same length, Clips in the Clips List the stereo display is removed and the clips are dis- played as individual clips in the Clips List.
Stereo and multichannel clips, whether imported or recorded into Pro Tools, are displayed as single items in the Clips List. For example, two mono Naming and Displaying Clips source clips named “Main Piano.L” and “Main Pi- in the Clips List ano.R” are listed as “Main Piano (Stereo).” An ex- pand/collapse triangle indicates stereo and multi- A typical session can become cluttered with doz- channel clips.

Batch Clip Rename dialog.
Managing Clips in the Clips List In the course of editing a session, the Clips List can quickly fill up with many clips—with ones you have created intentionally and also with those that are automatically created by cutting, pasting, and separating other clips, or importing Clip Group files (or REX files as clip groups). Pro Tools lets you hide or remove clips in your session so you do not have to scroll through a long Clips List.
Hiding Auto-Created Clips You can hide clips that were automatically created during the course of editing.
To hide auto-created clips:
From the Clips List menu, deselect Show >  Auto-Created. With this option deselected, only user-defined clips appear in the Clips List.
User-defined clips include:
• Whole-file clips • Clips created during recording • Imported clips • Renamed clips • Clips created as a result of AudioSuite Batch Clip Rename dialog processing 4 Configure the Batch Clip Rename dialog as de- • New clips created with the Clip > sired. Capture command or the Edit > Separate Clip commands 5 Click OK.
• Clips created by trimming whole-file audio clips When auto-created clips are hidden, Pro Tools 4 Do one of the following:
warns you if the number of auto-created clips ex- • Click Remove to remove the unused clips from ceeds a certain threshold, and gives you the option the session.
of deleting them. If you choose to delete them, all • If clearing a whole-file audio clip and you want auto-created clips are deleted at the same time.
to permanently remove the audio file from your To ensure that you keep any specific auto-created hard drive, click Delete.
clips, rename them. When you name a clip, it is When deleting audio files for multiple clips, promoted from being an auto-created clip to a user- Pro Tools presents a warning dialog for each audio defined clip (see “Renaming Clips” on page 328).
file.
Clearing Unwanted Clips To bypass repeated warning dialogs:
In the Clips List, you can select unwanted clips and Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) the  then use the Clear command to remove them from Delete button in the Clear dialog. This perma- the session. Whole-file clips can also be perma- nently deletes each successive audio file from nently removed from the hard drive on which they your hard drive (for each of the unused clips) are stored. without any further warning.
The Clear command cannot be undone. Use this “power delete mode” with caution.
Deletion of these files cannot be undone.

Regular expressions are standardized text strings that describe search patterns. Use regular expres- The Librarian menu commands include:
sions when batch renaming tracks or clips to find Restores the default settings for and replace text based on more advanced criteria <factory default> Batch Naming. than simple find and replace text operations.
Save Settings Saves the current settings. This For more information about regular expres- command overwrites any previous version of the sions, visit https://www.regex101.com.
preset.
Press Command+Shift+S (Mac) or Control+ Shift+S (Windows) to save the current settings.
336 Pro Tools Reference Guide Some commonly used regular expressions for advanced find and replace text operations:
regular expressions function . Matches any single character.
\ - \ Lets the character following the second \ be considered differently. For example, since . (period) is used to match any character, type \. to match an actual . (period) in a search string.
\s Matches any instance of a single space (such as the space bar or tab).
\d Matches a single number.
\w Matches a single “word character” or letter.
+ Any of the above regular expressions or any literal character can be fol- lowed with + to match one or more of the preceding characters (for exam- ple, \w+ can be used to find any number of spaces created by the spacebar or tab).
[abc] Match a single instance of a, b, or c.
[^abc] Match any character except a, b, or c.
Performing Multiple Find and Replace Commands With enabled, any number of text patterns can be matched and replaced when batch Regular Expressions renaming tracks or clips. In the field, type the pattern you want to match inside parentheses and then Find separate patterns using (the pipe symbol). For example:
| find replace (Audio)|(VCA)|(Aux) (?1)(?2)(?3) The text following ?1 will replace the text Audio, the text following ?2 will replace VCA, and the text fol- lowing ?3 will replace Aux—(?1Aud)(?2Group Master)(?Bus) Removing Automatic Names Generated By Track Duplication Use the following regular expressions to remove all instances of .dup1, .dup2, or up to any number of du- plicates from track or clip names by replacing them with no character at all.
find replace \.dup\d+ \. searches for literal periods—as opposed to the special token . (period) since . itself is used to match any character—followed by dup, then \d (the regular expression for any number) followed by + (which matches any number of digits that follow .dup) and replaces all matches with nothing (effectively deleting that text).
Replacing Multiple Common AudioSuite Suffixes from Clip Names Use the following regular expressions to find text automatically added by common AudioSuite processes and remove them or replace them with something more appropriately descriptive.
find replace (-Norm)|(-EQ3-1B) (?1)(?2-HPF) This replaces -Norm with nothing (effectively deleting that text), and replaces -EQ3-1B with -HPF to indi- cate that the EQ3-1B AudioSuite processing on the selected clips was used to roll off low frequencies.

Pro Tools creates a centralized database automati- cally for all mounted volumes, including Transfer Pro Tools databases use two file types:
volumes, on your system drive. Pro Tools uses a Workspace.wksp Stores media and session file single database file that maintains an index of all metadata for a particular volume or catalog.
volumes (hard drives, networked drives, and re- movable media) and catalogs.
Wavecache.wfm Stores waveform overviews for all audio files referenced by each session.
On Mac, the database file is stored in /Users/Shared/Pro Tools.
There is also the MiscSettings file which stores the directory path for the Sound Libraries folder.
On Windows, the database file is stored in <Systemdrive>/Users/Public/Pro Tools.
Workspace Browser Layout and Tools Workspace browsers display all files in their database, including Pro Tools and non-Pro Tools files.
Unknown file types can be shown, as well as aliases, and desktop folders.

Location selected for search Browser Search field Simple Search enabled Search results Simple search results for the selected location Advanced Search Pro Tools provides advanced tools for searching files and volumes by specific categories. You can refine your search by providing multiple constraints on the search parameters. You can also easily switch from an advanced search to a simple search.
Volume selected for search Advanced Search settings Advanced Search enabled Search Checked Search results option (disabled) Advanced search results for the selected Volume To initiate an advanced search on a selected item in the Locations pane:

364 Pro Tools Reference Guide When you install Pro Tools, this folder is created in Adding and Removing Sound Library the following locations:
Locations You can add (or remove) different directories on macOS HD/Users/<username>/Documents/ Pro Tools/Sound Libraries/ your system whose contents are not actually copied or moved to the Sound Libraries folder. Added loca- Windows C:\Users\<username>\Documents\ tions are essentially aliases that point to the original Pro Tools\Sound Libraries location of the designated folder and no media is cop- ied or moved from the original location.
You can install audio content for Sound Libraries available from Avid as well as third-party vendors.

When searching on tags, all audio files in the selected Adding and Editing Tags location with matching tags appear in the search re- sults—the more tags you specify, the more selective Pro Tools lets you add and edit tags for audio files in your searches can be.
Soundbase. If you want to search your existing sam- ple libraries or custom audio files, you will need to add tags manually.
To add tags to an audio file:
1 Locate and select one or more audio files that you want to tag in any Workspace browser.
2 Right-click on a selected audio file in the Work- space browser and choose Tags.
Adding tags to selected audio files Choosing Tags in the Right-click menu for selected audio files You can also assign tags by clicking in the Tags column.
Creating a new tag for the selected audio file Select the tags you want to add or type in a new tag. You can add as many tags to any audio file (WAV or AIFF only) as you like. If more than one Choose from the Recently Used Tags options audio file is selected, you are prompted to confirm in the Right-click menu to quickly add any of that you want to add the tag to all selected files.
the last used tags to the selected audio files.
When done, press Enter.

Linking and Relinking Files A Pro Tools session is made up of references, or Choosing When to Relink links, to audio files and other session media. Audio Pro Tools lets you relink files while opening a ses- and video files must be stored on suitable Perfor- sion or after a session is already open, as follows:
mance volumes and be properly linked in order to be playable in the session.
Relinking when opening a session ensures that  the session opens with all media playable. Auto- The following types of files are not playable:
matic and manual relinking can be performed. See Transfer Files Files that are stored on volumes not “Opening a Session with Missing Files” on suitable for playback, such as network volumes or page 379.
USB thumb drives.
Relinking later (after a session is already open)  is the fastest way to open the session, but all miss- Missing Files Files that cannot be found where the ing items remain offline and unplayable. See session expects them, either because they have “Relink Window” on page 380.
been moved, or because they are stored on vol- umes that are not currently mounted (offline vol- Forcing a Relink umes).
If you cannot relink to the original file, you can force a relink to another file. See “Force Relinking Files” on page 383.

suitable for playback, such as USB thumb drives or network drives. These volumes appear as Transfer Copy and Relink provides a convenient way to volumes in the Workspace browser. copy files and relink the session or Catalog to the copies rather than to the originals.

378 Pro Tools Reference Guide Is the simplest Opening a Session with Missing Automatically Find and Relink method to relink sessions to required media, but it Files provides no way to compare files or verify links.
To open a session with missing files: This option cannot be undone. The Automatically option does the following:
When you open a session, Pro Tools opens the Find and Relink session with all available media, then shows • Searches all Performance volumes for all miss- you how many files are missing (if any) and ing items with matching Name, ID, Format, and asks how you want to proceed. (If told that files Length are unsuitable for playback, see “Transfer Files” • Links missing items to the first matches found on page 378.) • Commits links for all items possible Select one of the following:
Links, once committed, cannot be undone • Skip All—or press Command+S (Mac) or once the session is saved. The only way to re- Control+S (Windows).
vert to previous links is to close the session • Manually Find Relink—or press Command+M without saving changes.
(Mac) or Control+M (Windows).
If some files remain unlinked, the Task window • Relink—or press Automatically Find opens and a failed task appears in the Paused Tasks Command+A (Mac) or Control+A (Windows).
pane. Double-click the Task icon to open the Select 3 Regenerate Missing Rendered Files Relink window and manually find and relink files.

7 Commit Links To relink a Catalog:
1 Open the Catalog.
Committing Links 2 Select items to relink.
There is no Undo for relinking. Once a file has 3 Choose Relink Selected from the Browser menu been relinked and the session saved, the session al- in that Catalog window.
ways remembers the new link (the original link is forgotten). For this reason, no files are actually relinked until you click Commit Links.
Creating Catalogs of Folders Catalogs To create a Catalog of an entire folder and all Catalogs are similar to having a Favorites folder, its contents:
and serve as a way to collect and organize fre- Open the Workspace browser, if not already open.
stored. A Catalog is analogous to a folder full of aliases or shortcuts. Placing a file in a Catalog does Drag and drop a single folder onto the Catalog not create a copy of the file, and does not change icon in the Workspace browser.
where the file is stored. Rather, it stores a reference (or alias) to the file so that it can be found and ma- Pro Tools first indexes the folder, then a fully in- nipulated without having to search for its physical dexed Catalog is created, with the same name as location. the dropped folder.
Creating Catalogs Renaming a Catalog Catalogs can be added to the Workspace as new, Catalogs can be renamed at any time.
empty Catalogs, or you can select items first and then save a Catalog of all selected items. To rename a Catalog:
1 Click the Catalog item in the Workspace To create a new, empty Catalog:
browser until its name highlights.

Catalog.

The first step is to add files to a Catalog (files must be online to be added to a Catalog). Once added to a Catalog, if the items are taken offline they are listed in italicized text.
Offline files can be dropped from a Catalog to the Timeline just like online files. When you drop the files, a dialog appears asking if you want to relink the files or skip relinking. Click Skip All to load all the items as offline files (in the Timeline, their clips appear in light blue).
386 Pro Tools Reference Guide Task Manager Window For maximum performance and flexibility with file management tasks, Pro Tools provides the Task Man- ager window. File management with Pro Tools involves nearly constant creating, copying, converting, processing, searching, and indexing files. These tasks occur in the background, letting you continue record- ing, editing, and mixing without delay.
The Task window lets you monitor, pause and cancel ongoing tasks including file copying, fade regener- ation, and indexing. You can also use the Task Manager to monitor upload and download progress with projects.
View Toggle (Maximize/Minimize) Status Active Tasks Paused Tasks Task window, maximum view Opening the Task Window Task Manager Status The Task Manager Status indicator lights and ani- To open the Task Window, do one of the mates when the Task Manager is busy. When no following:
tasks are in progress, it is unlit. The indicator turns In Pro Tools, choose Window > Task Manager.
 red if the Task Manager requires your attention.
Press Option+’ (Mac) or Alt+’ (Windows) to  open the Workspace browser.
Click the Task Manager Status indicator in the  Edit Window toolbar.

ground to notify you. The incomplete task is shown in the Paused Tasks pane of the Task win- Progress Indicator Indicates processing is ongo- dow. ing.

does not interrupt current work. This allows you to continue recording and editing, and postpone file Active Tasks Pane management until a more convenient time.

About Canceling Tasks Task Prioritization Some tasks involve one or more sub-tasks. Delet- A task that is in progress may be interrupted by a ing a task cannot undo sub-tasks already com- task of higher priority. When this happens, the par- pleted. For example, if importing and converting tially completed task is halted and rescheduled multiple files appears as a single task, deleting that later in the queue, and shown with a progress bar in task before it is completed stops the import and the Active Tasks pane. When the higher priority conversion process, but files already converted and task is finished the first incomplete task in the imported remain on disk. If you want to cancel a queue resumes.
task involving multiple sub-tasks, you can instead let the process complete and then use to com- Undo pletely undo all sub-tasks, as available.
Pausing Tasks During Playback A global preference Pause During Playback is pro- vided in the Task window Browser menu. When enabled, Active Tasks are paused whenever Pro Tools is playing (or recording). This setting is especially useful when working on slower CPUs, or whenever you want to minimize system load for maximum playback and recording performance.
This preference maintains its setting until the next time it is changed.
Stored Tasks Any task that imports files into a session is stored with a session when it is closed, and resumes when the session is re-opened. For example, if you Im- with port Session Data Copy From Source Media selected, then and the session before Save Close the copy completes, the copy tasks resume when the session is reopened.
Grouped Tasks Some tasks consist of many smaller tasks. For in- stance, Copy and Relink may require hundreds of individual files to be copied. To help manage these tasks, they are grouped under the originating com- mand. So in the example of a Copy and Relink command, one copy task would appear with an Ex- pand/Collapse icon in the Task window. Clicking the icon shows each of the individual sub-tasks.
If there is more than one grouped task in the queue, all of the sub-tasks for the first group must be com- pleted before the sub-tasks of the second group can be processed.
If the session is saved and closed while several of these group tasks are in the queue, and if those tasks are the type that can be interrupted and saved when closing the session, all of the tasks reappear as part of the same group when the session is reopened.

Pro Tools Projects in the Artist Chat Window Use the Artist Chat window to invite people in your contacts to collaborate on individual projects. You can monitor collaboration activity on Pro Tools Projects, see who is collaborating on a project, and communi- cate with all project collaborators using group messaging in the Artist Chat window. The project owner (the person who created the project) can invite collaborators from their contacts.The project owner can also remove individual collaborators from the project.
To open (or close) the Artist Chat window:
Launch Pro Tools.
Log in to your Avid Master Account, either through the Dashboard window or by selecting 2 File > Sign In. You must be connected to the Internet and signed in to access the Artist Chat window.
Do one of the following:
• Choose Chat.
Window > Artist • Choose Projects.
Avid Link > Pro Tools • Press Command+Shift+= (equals) (Mac) or Control+Shift+= (equals) (Windows).
• Click the Show Artist Chat button in the Edit window toolbar or Transport window collaboration con- trols.

404 Pro Tools Reference Guide Chapter 20: Track Collaboration Sharing Projects and What is Shared?
Collaborating Most project data, media, track data, and conduc- Pro Tools lets you upload your projects to the tor rulers is shared when you choose to share a cloud and collaborate on the same project with project. Upload Track Changes When any participant makes a “share-able” change to a shared track, they take ownership of that track, even if only temporarily, and the Upload Track Changes control on that track turns green. When that person clicks the Upload Track Changes con- trol, it uploads the track changes to the cloud copy Shared track of the project. Other participants will notice that the Download Track Changes control for that track turns green in their local copy of the project and they can choose to download those changes or not.

418 Pro Tools Reference Guide Upload Conductor Ruler Changes When any participant makes a “share-able” change to a Conductor ruler, the Upload Ruler Changes control turns green. They can then click the control to upload their changes to the cloud copy of the project.
To upload Conductor ruler changes:
Make changes on a Conductor ruler (see “Track Tracks List Right-click menu for online, shared tracks Ownership” on page 414).
Do one of the following:
Click the Upload Ruler Changes control.
Conductor Ruler  Collaboration Controls Right-click the Upload Ruler Changes control  and choose Upload.

Chords, and Markers—and they are shown any time Track Collaboration is selected in the Edit Upload Ruler Changes Right-Click Menu Window View.

Unowned Conductor ruler You can take ownership of a Conductor ruler either by making “share-able” changes on that ruler (in Conductor Ruler Ownership Requested this case you automatically own the ruler for the time being) or you can click the Ruler Ownership You can click the Conductor Ruler Ownership control to relinquish ownership of that ruler and the other participant will take ownership of that ruler.
Conductor Ruler Owned by Another Participant When another participant owns a Conductor ruler, the Conductor Ruler Ownership control turns or- ange for everyone else.
Conductor ruler owned by another participant Ownership Pending If you want to take control of a Conductor ruler owned by another participant, you can make a “share-able” change on the Conductor ruler or click the Conductor Ruler Ownership control. The other participant is notified that the ownership of the Conductor ruler is being requested and you will see that the request is pending. If they relinquish ownership of that Conductor ruler, you will take ownership of it.

438 Pro Tools Reference Guide Pro Tools does not import proprietary sequence 4 In the Mix window, click the MIDI Output files. To use sequences from other MIDI applica- selector for each new track and assign a MIDI tions in a Pro Tools session, you will need to first instrument and channel.
save them as Standard MIDI Files. See the manu- facturer’s documentation for details on saving MIDI Import Options Dialog Standard MIDI Files.
The MIDI Import Options dialog lets you specify There are two types of Standard MIDI Files, both what Pro Tools does when importing MIDI files.
of which are supported by Pro Tools:
• Type 0 MIDI files store data for all MIDI chan- nels in a single track. When importing these files, Pro Tools separates the data by channel and places each track’s data in separate clips and tracks.
• Type 1 MIDI files, sometimes referred to as mul- titrack MIDI files, contain multiple tracks of MIDI data. When importing these files, each track’s data is placed on its own new MIDI track in the Pro Tools session.
Importing MIDI Files Using Pro Tools Menu Commands Pro Tools provides commands to import MIDI files.

Text Encoding Pro Tools allows entry and display of any lan- guage characters supported in Unicode anywhere in the Pro Tools application. This lets you use mul- tiple languages in session names, and in path names, file names and clip names.
However, Pro Tools 7.3.x and lower does not sup- port Unicode. When importing data from sessions or OMF sequences created or saved with older ver- sions of Pro Tools, you may need to identify the language encoding used in the imported elements.
To choose the language encoding when importing data from a legacy Pro Tools session:
Choose Data.
1 File > Import > Session Choose the language used in the legacy session from the pop-up menu.

in Minutes:Seconds, Bars|Beats, Samples, Time- code, or Feet/Frames.
Maintain Absolute Timecode Values This option places tracks at the locations where they were lo- cated in the source session. For example, if the cur- Sample Rate Conversion (SRC) rent session starts at 00:01:00:00, and the session Options from which you are importing starts at In the Import Session Data dialog, you can set op- 10:00:00:00, the earliest imported tracks can ap- tions that control how sample rate conversion is pear in your session is 9 hours and 59 minutes after applied to imported audio files. sion and the current session have the same sample rate, this portion of the dialog is unavailable.

the source session starts at 01:00:00:00 and con- tains a track that starts at 01:01:00:00, and the cur- Source Sample Rate For audio files created in any rent session start is 02:00:00:00, the track will be session, no matter what the session sample rate is, placed at 02:01:00:00 in the current session. you can have the sample rate conversion process treat the files in several ways to compensate for Map Start Timecode To This option places tracks pull up, pull down, and NTSC or PAL frame rates.

Conversion Quality This option lets you set the Adjust Session Start Time to Match Source Start Time quality of the sample-rate conversion process (see “Sample Rate Conversion Quality” on page 425).
When selected, the Adjust Session Start Time to Match Source Start Time option lets you automat- ically set the start time of the current Pro Tools ses- sion to match the start time of the session, or AAF or OMF sequence you are importing.

Translation Notes Format Translation Notes are presented in the following Audio File Format format:
Compatibility Issues Track Name <Timecode Start–Timecode End> AIFC Audio File Format “clip name (if available)”;message Avid Media Composer and other Avid editors sup- Translation Notes and Categories port audio files in the AIFC format, a version of the Audio Interchange File Format (AIFF) that sup- The Translation Notes File is separated into differ- ports compressed media. Pro Tools can import and ent categories. The following are sample messages use this format, but not compress audio in this for- in each category, with explanations of what has mat. happened and solutions if necessary.
Parsing Errors Sound Designer II Audio File Format Example A1<01:00:00:00–01:00:04:20>error reading Audio Files component “Assertion in OMF_Objects2x.cpp”, line 2871;substituting fill.
Pro Tools 11.x and later do not support Sound De- signer II (SD II) audio files. Open any AAF se- This error indicates a problem that may have orig- quence that references SD II files in Pro Tools 10.x inated in Pro Tools or the application that pro- and lower, or Pro Tools 2020.9 or later (macOS duced the AAF or OMF file. In the translated com- only). Any referenced Sound Designer II files are position, there will be silence or video black at the given interval.

Exporting Pro Tools Tracks as AAF or OMF Sequences Pro Tools lets you export individual tracks in AAF or OMF format. Use the Export Selected Tracks as AAF/OMF command.
AAF and OMF sequences exported from Pro Tools do not support video files. They also do not retain information about Elastic Audio, plug-in assignments, parameters, markers, routing, or grouping. It is recommended that you commit Elastic Audio tracks, and any plug-in or hardware insert processing prior to export.
Pro Tools can export AAF or OMF sequences linked to RF64/BW64 audio files. RF64/BW64 files are special WAV files that can be larger GB). If you are exporting a sequence from a session referencing RF64/BW64 WAV files, use AAF as the sequence container and MXF as the media file format.

To export clip-based gain data, the Enforce Avid Compatibility option must be enabled.
Importing MXF Files The clip gain of Pro Tools clips with static volume between -inf dB and +12 dB will translate directly MXF (Material Exchange Format) files are com- to clip-based gain upon export. Dynamic clip gain monly used with a NLE (Non-Linear Editor), such and values above 12 dB will be rendered for each as Avid Media Composer. There are several types clip in the resulting media, to optimize compatibil- of MXF files with varying file complexities. The ity with Avid video editing applications.
different types are referred to by their operational patterns. Pro Tools supports two different opera- Clip-based gain is not exported when tional patterns within MXF files: OP-Atom and exporting to OMF format.
OP1a.
Exporting Volume Automation to AAF or OMF OP-Atom MXF Files Pro Tools does export volume automation to AAF and OMF, but only if the Enforce Avid Compatibil- OP-Atom MXF files contain either one channel of ity and Quantize Edits to Frame Boundaries op- audio or one track of video per file. These files tions are disabled.
cannot contain both audio and video, nor more than one channel of either. OP-Atom files are opti- mized for post-production and editorial because they are easy to decode and play. However, since the audio and video channels are not self-con- tained, they are not a especially useful as a final de- livery format.

OMF/AAF Options When exporting tracks to AAF or OMF, Pro Tools provides the following OMF/AAF Options:
Enforce Media Composer Compatibility If the Enforce Media Composer Compatibility op- tion is enabled, all AAF and OMF files and se- quences will meet the specific requirements of Me- dia Composer. Sample rates are limited to 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz. Exported clips will be padded to the nearest frame boundaries (Quantize Edits to Pro Tools Bounce Mix dialog (MXF file type) Frame Boundaries). Exporting Session Exported Session Text Files Information as Text Session Information You can use the com- Export Session Info as Text The session text file starts with basic information mand to create a text file that contains extensive in- about the session or project. This information in- formation about your session or project.
cludes the session or project name, sample rate, bit This text file can contain a list of audio files, audio depth, timecode format, and number of audio clips, audio track EDL (Edit Decision List) infor- tracks, audio clips, and audio files, as shown in the mation, extended timestamp information, and in- following example.
formation about crossfades.
Session Information example Track EDLs are exported as tab-delimited text, SESSION NAME: Ripleys II-092700 with tabs between each column heading, and tabs SAMPLE RATE: 48000.000000 between each event parameter. You can use this data in a program for reading EDLs, or you can BIT DEPTH: 24-bit format the EDL data into tables using a word pro- TIMECODE FORMAT: 30 Frame cessor or spreadsheet application.
To export session information as text:
Choose Text.
1 File > Export > Session Info as Select whether to include the File List, Clips List, and track EDLs.
File List and Clips List Next, if you choose to include them, are the lists of audio files and clips.
478 Pro Tools Reference Guide Track EDLs Include Clips List The final item, if exported, is the list of track Select this option to export a list of the session or EDLs. A track EDL lists the track name, and all ed- project audio clips. The Clips List provides a list of its, including the event number, the clip name, clip all of the audio clips in the session or project, as start and end time, and clip duration. The clip time- well as the source audio file for each clip.
stamp is also exported, if you select this option.
Include Markers Subframes are shown in each time field if you se- lect this option.
Select this option to export a list of all markers in- cluded in the session or project.
Export Session Information Include Plug-In List as Text Options Select this option to export a list of the plug-ins used in the session or project.
Include Track EDLs (Playlists) You can export track EDLs (playlists). Track EDLs can be used to spot-check clip placement and edits, or in a conforming program for post ap- plications. In extreme circumstances the EDL can be used to recreate the entire session or project.
You can export session text for All Tracks or for Selected Tracks Only.
MIDI track EDLs are not exported.
When exporting track EDLs, the following options are available:
This option allows you to export Show Subframes subframe time information with track EDLs, if used in your session or project.
Include User Timestamps You can include user timestamps with track EDLs. User timestamps in- dicate a user-defined session or project location for the clip, or the original location of the clip when re- corded.
Export Session Text dialog Include File List Select this option to export a list of the session or project audio files. The File List provides a list of all the audio files in the session (and their hard drive locations) or project.

• References to all audio files within the clip For track EDLs, you can choose whether to show group crossfades, not to show them, or to combine cross- faded clips. track) Time Format • All MIDI data present in the clip group (such as notes, controllers, and Sysex) You can select the appropriate time format that ex- ported EDL information is based on. To import clip groups into the Clips List:

To import clip groups into an existing track:

2 Drag the files onto an existing track in the Edit Clip Group Import Options dialog window of the current session or project.

Exporting Clip Groups Exporting Clip Groups to Another Hard Drive Generally, if you are exporting clip groups to an- To export a clip group:
other hard drive, you should copy any referenced 1 Select one or more clip groups in the Clips List.

To export a clip group to a different hard drive and include its audio files:

2 Create a new session or project on the new drive.
3 Choose Setup > Preferences.

RF64 Sharing Sessions Created on Pro Tools supports the RF64 audio file format.
Different Computer Platforms RF64 is an extension to the WAV audio file format that addresses the 4 gigabyte size limitation of other WAV audio file formats. RF64 audio files Working with Mac-Based HFS+ are identified in Workspace browsers with the des- Drives on Windows Computers ignation “MBWF” in the Attributes field.

488 Pro Tools Reference Guide Sharing Sessions Created on Different Pro Tools Systems Pro Tools makes it easy to share sessions between different tiers of Pro Tools. There are some important differences between systems that can affect how session material is transferred.
For details on transferring sessions between Windows and Mac systems, see “Sharing Sessions Created on Different Computer Platforms” on page 487.

Scrolling Options You can change the Scrolling option while the Transport is running.
You can configure how contents of the Edit window scroll during playback and recording.
Page Scrolling During Playback To configure Scrolling options:
You can set Pro Tools to scroll the track display while playing, and also have the edit cursor appear Choose Options > Scrolling and select one of the  wherever playback stops.
following options:
No Scrolling The Edit window does not scroll To make the track display and the edit cursor during or after playback. The playback cursor follow playback:
moves across the Edit window, indicating the play- Enable the Timeline Insertion/Play Start Marker  back location.
Follows Playback button in the Edit window.
After Playback The playback cursor moves across the Edit window, indicating the playback location.
When playback has stopped, the Edit window scrolls to the final playback location.
Page The playback cursor moves across the Edit window, indicating the playback location. When the right edge of the Edit window is reached, its en- Timeline Insertion/Play Start Marker Follows Playback button enabled tire contents are scrolled, and the playback cursor continues moving from the left edge of the win- dow. For more information, see “Page Scrolling You can enable or disable the Timeline During Playback” on page 508. Insertion/Play Start Marker Follows Play- back option in the Operation Preferences.
Continuous See “Continuous Scrolling During Playback” on page 508.

the entire selection. This allows you to check, for When auditioning the end of a selection, playback instance, whether the beginning or end of a selec- begins before the end point by the pre-roll amount.

510 Pro Tools Reference Guide Transport Fade-in Be sure to disable the Fade-in Transport op- tion when recording. Audio that is recorded Pro Tools provides a Fade-in option for the Trans- with the Fade-in Transport option enabled port. Enable this option to fade in the levels for all will include a fade based on the set Fade-in source audio linearly, from silence to the current duration.
levels of the source audio when the transport starts.
The Fade-in time can be set between 0.000 and 4.000 seconds. This option is useful for avoiding Playing Timeline and Edit pops and clicks, or sudden boosts in volume when Selections with the Playhead relocating the playback location during playback.
(Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) When the Scrolling option is set to Center Play- head, selections in the Timeline do not determine when playback begins. The Playhead itself denotes where playback begins.
Timeline and Edit selections, however, can still be Fade-in time played when the Playhead is enabled.
Fade-in enable Transport window with Fade-in enabled (2 seconds) To play an Edit selection with the Playhead enabled:

When playing back a large number of tracks, Pro Tools may take a little longer to actually start playback. To avoid this delay, put Pro Tools in Playback Modes Prime for Playback mode before starting playback.
Pro Tools provides several Playback modes:
To enable Prime for Playback mode and start • Normal Playback mode playback:
• Half-Speed Playback mode 1 Do one of the following:
• Prime for Playback mode • Option-click (Mac) or Alt-click (Windows) Play in the Transport to put Pro Tools in Prime for • Loop Playback mode Playback mode.
• Dynamic Transport mode • Right-click the Play button and select Prime for Playback.
Normal Playback Mode The Stop button lights and the Play button flashes.
Normal Playback mode is simply when none of the other Playback modes are selected. In Normal 2 To begin playback instantaneously, click Play.
Playback mode, Pro Tools plays back at the ses- 3 Click Stop to stop playback.
sion sample rate.
When synchronizing to timecode, use Prime for Playback mode to play back large num- bers of tracks. This decreases the time it takes to lock to timecode.
512 Pro Tools Reference Guide Loop Playback Mode When enabled, a loop symbol appears in the Play button in the Transport window.
When Loop Playback mode is enabled, the se- lected track range repeats on playback. If there is no selection, playback occurs normally from the current Cursor location.
A selection must be at least 500 ms in length Loop Playback enabled for it to loop on playback.
4 Click Play in the Transport window.

Measuring the offset for MIDI Beat Clock in samples MIDI Beat Clock Sample Offset Since MIDI is not sample accurate, you may want to make several measurements at different grid locations and average them to come up with the best value for the MIDI Beat Clock Offset.
Once you have measured the latency for your MIDI Beat Clock-enabled devices, be sure to keep a record of these values in case you need to re-enter the MIDI Beat Clock Sample Offset in other sessions.
Ableton Link Pro Tools supports Ableton Link, a synchronization technology from Ableton that keeps Link-enabled ap- plications in time over a local network (LAN or WLAN). Link synchronizes musical beat, tempo, phase and start/stop commands across multiple applications running on the same or different devices. Applica- tions on devices connected to a local network discover each other automatically and form a musical session in which each participant can perform independently:
• Anyone can start or stop while still staying in time.
• Anyone can change the tempo and the others follow.
• Anyone can join or leave without disrupting the session.
520 Pro Tools Reference Guide Link-enabled applications on the same network are Showing and Hiding Ableton referred to as peers. Peers can be on the same com- Link Controls puter as well as on other computers on the network.
Controls for Ableton Link can be shown or hidden The tempo range supported by Ableton Link is in the Edit window toolbar and the Transport win- 20 bpm to 999 bpm. dow.

same local network.

522 Pro Tools Reference Guide The following bar and beat values are available:
• None (Launch Quantize is off and Pro Tools starts playback immediately regardless of beat/bar location, and tick-based tracks fol- low the network tempo) • 8 Bars • 4 Bars • 2 Bars • 1 Bar • 1/2 note • 1/4 note • 1/8 note • 1/16 note • 1/32 note • Triplet (select in addition to a beat value to make the selected beat value a triplet) The selected value is saved with the ses- Quantize sion.
Beat Cycle Position Indicator The Beat Cycle Position indicator shows progress through the bar/beat cycle.
Link Button Click the button to toggle Ableton Link on Link and off. The button lights blue when enabled.
When Ableton Link is enabled, your Pro Tools ses- sion is automatically recognized by all other Link-enabled applications and devices on the same local network.

Input Connections and Audio Levels Digital Clipping Avid HD audio interfaces operate as line-level de- Clipping occurs when you feed a signal to a re- vices and offer no pre-amplification. Low-level corder or mixer that is louder or “hotter” than the sources like microphones and electric guitars need device allows. On many analog tape decks, a little to be pre-amplified. You can do this with a quality clipping adds a perceived warmth to the sound due mixing board or dedicated preamp (such as PRE).
to tape compression. In digital recording, however, clipping causes digital distortion, which is undesir- PRE can be remote-controlled from within able and should always be avoided.
Pro Tools sessions.
