When a MIDI or Instrument track is set to Velocity view, or when the Velocity lane is revealed under a • Set the MIDI or Instrument track to Velocity track, each note’s attack velocity is represented view.
with a velocity stalk. The taller the velocity stalk, • Reveal the Controller lane for the MIDI or In- the higher the velocity value (0–127).
strument track and show Velocity.

1 Do one of the following: Click at the beginning of the note range, near the bottom of the velocity range, and drag to the • Set the MIDI or Instrument track to Velocity right and up until the line encompasses the view.
range of notes you want to include in the fade.

Changing velocities with the Line shape You can also use the Trim tool to scale the veloci- ties for all selected notes. This is useful if you like the velocity relationship between the notes, but Revealing the Velocity Lane want them all to be louder or softer.
2 Select any Grabber tool.
When in Velocity view, the Trim tool can be 3 Drag the top (diamond) of the velocity stalk up used to trim note durations in addition to or down. changing velocities.
To scale velocities with the Trim tool:
Set the MIDI or Instrument track to Velocity view.
Using the Selector tool or any Grabber tool, se- lect the range of notes to be edited.
With the Trim tool, click near the range of se- lected notes and drag up or down. Dragging up Dragging a velocity stalk increases the velocities for each note and drag- The velocities for a range of notes can be edited ging down decreases them.
with any Pencil tool.
For fine-resolution editing of velocity values, Command-click-drag (Mac) or Control-click- drag the top of the velocity stalk to the desired value.

794 Pro Tools Reference Guide To edit a continuous controller event with the Pen- cil tool, the controller first has to be added to the Automated MIDI Controllers list.
The controller is automatically added to the Automated MIDI Controllers list when you record controller information from an exter- nal device.
To manually enable a continuous controller for automation:
1 Do one of the following:
• Click the Track View selector for the MIDI or Instrument track you want to edit, and select Add/Remove Controller from the controllers Selecting Add/Remove Controllers from the Controller submenu.
Type selector on a Controller lane 2 In the Automated MIDI Controllers dialog, se- lect the MIDI Controllers range for the control- ler you want to automate.
Selecting Add/Remove Controllers from the Track Selecting the MIDI Controllers range View selector • If Controller lanes are revealed under the MIDI or Instrument track you want to edit, click the Controller Type selector for the Controller lane under the track and select Add/Remove Controller from the controllers submenu.
3 Select the controller that you want to automate MIDI continuous controllers are simply “unas- from the list on the left, and click Add. The se- signed” in the standard MIDI specification. This lected controller is added to the Automated can be confusing if you are editing data for several MIDI Controllers list on the right. “unassigned” or otherwise generic continuous con- trollers, so by having the controller number always shown next to the name in parenthesis, you can easily see which continuous controller you are ed- iting.
MIDI CC numbers shown in Track View and Controller Lane selectors (in addition to the Automated MIDI Controllers dialog standard name, if any) Repeat steps 2 and 3 as necessary until you have added all of the MIDI controllers you want for Clip Boundaries Shown in MIDI automation.

Aside from common con- troller number assignments like “Mod Wheel” (1), “Volume” (7), or “Sustain Pedal” (64), many 796 Pro Tools Reference Guide Keyboard Shortcuts for Navigating MIDI Using Tab to Transients with MIDI Clips and MIDI Notes Tab to Transients works with MIDI clips and MIDI notes and MIDI CC events on MIDI and In- strument tracks much like it does with audio clips and audio transients on audio tracks.
Tab to Transients Disabled When Tab to Transients is disabled, pressing Tab moves the insertion point to the next MIDI clip Insertion point tabbed to next MIDI event on a MIDI boundary regardless of the track view (just like track in notes view (Tab to Transients enabled) with audio clips).
Press Option+Tab (Mac) or Alt+Tab (Win- dows) to move the insertion point to the pre- vious clip boundary or MIDI event.
Hold the Shift key while pressing Tab—or Option+Tab (Mac) or Alt+Tab (Windows)— to move the insertion point and make or ex- tend the edit selection.
Insertion point tabbed to next clip boundary on a MIDI track in notes view (Tab to Transients disabled) Tab to Transients Enabled When Tab to Transients is enabled, pressing Tab moves the Edit insertion to the beginning of the next note on, note off, or clip boundary regardless of the track view (just like with audio transients), but does not make note selections.
Tabbing to Notes and MIDI CC Events in Tabbing to MIDI CC Events in MIDI CC Notes View with Controller Lanes Track Views Shown When a MIDI or Instrument track is set to any When a MIDI or Instrument track is set to Notes MIDI Controller track view and Tab to Transients view and Tab to Transients is enabled, press the is enabled, press the Tab key to move to the next Tab key to move to the next MIDI note or MIDI MIDI event shown in that track view. tion+Tab (Mac) or Alt+Tab (Windows) to move to the previous note or MIDI event.
In Pitch Bend view, moving from one pitch bend event to the next using the Tab to Transients In Notes view with MIDI controller lanes shown and Tab to Transients enabled, Tab to move to a MIDI CC event 798 Pro Tools Reference Guide Using the Arrow Keys in Notes Selecting Notes or Velocity View or in MIDI Hold the Shift key while using the Left and Right Editor Windows Arrow keys to extend the note selection.
In Notes or Velocity view on MIDI and Instrument tracks, or in MIDI Editor windows, you can use the Arrow Keys (with or without modifier keys) to navigate, select, transpose, or trim MIDI notes, or edit velocities.
Navigating Notes Use the Left and Right Arrow keys to navigate to and select the previous or the next note.
In Notes view, selecting notes using the Right Arrow key while holding the Shift key Navigating and Selecting Notes in Chords When navigating through chords with the Left and Right Arrow keys, each note in the chord is recog- nized as a single event. This lets you move through each note in the chord, selecting each individually.
In Notes view, moving from one note to the next using the Right Arrow key For MIDI and Instrument tracks in Notes view where any MIDI Controller lanes shown, the Left and Right Arrow keys only navigate through MID notes and skip all MIDI CC events shown in any Controller lane.
In Notes view, navigating the notes of a chord with the Right Arrow key Use the Shift key with the Left and Right Arrow To duplicate and transpose selected notes up or down by octave:
keys to select more than a single note in a chord.
Press Option+Shift+Up/Down Arrows (Mac) or  Alt+Shift+Up/Down Arrows (Windows).

The MIDI protocol lets you choose from a range of 128 programs (0–127). However, most MIDI in- Press Option+Control+Shift+Right Arrow  struments have several banks of 128 programs. To (Mac) or Alt+Start+Shift+Right Arrow (Win- specify a bank, a bank select message must be sent.

MIDI Patch Names Pro Tools supports XML (Extensible Markup Lan- guage) for storing and importing patch names for external MIDI devices (such as MIDI instru- ments). Pro Tools installs MIDI patch name files Patch Select buttons (.midnam) for the factory default patch names of Do one of the following:
many common MIDI devices.
• In the Patch Select dialog, select a program number. MIDI patch name files (.midnam) can be ed- ited in any text editor, or you can use third- party patch librarian and editor software to create your own custom patch names.
On Mac, these files reside in /Library/Audio/ MIDI Patch Names/Avid.
On Windows, these files reside in Program Files\ Common Files\Avid\Pro Tools\MIDI Patch Names\.

Default Controller Values and Chasing Though new MIDI tracks appear to have default values for continuous controller playlists, this is actually not the case. For example, when viewing a MIDI track’s Mod Wheel playlist, you’ll see that Note Chasing enabled in Playlist selector the breakpoint line is set to a default of zero. How- Make sure to disable Note Chasing when working ever, since the modulation wheel on your MIDI with samplers that are playing loops. If a MIDI controller may be purposely set to a different track, for example, is triggering a 4-bar drum loop value, the default value in the track is not transmit- and you begin playing at bar 3, the loop will begin ted when playing.
playing at the wrong time and be out of synchroni- For example, if you record Mod Wheel automation zation with the other tracks.
that changes from 0–90 towards the end of the track, and you then play from the beginning of the Controller and Program Chasing track, or from anywhere before the change, having Pro Tools always chases continuous controller value is reached is not likely to be what you want.

When Delay Compensation is active, MIDI and Instrument tracks are automatically de- lay-compensated to provide low latency mon- itoring. For more information, see “Delay Compensation for MIDI” on page 1123.

only and does not alter in any way how MIDI data is displayed in the Edit window. To configure the Global MIDI Playback Offset:
1 Choose Setup > Preferences and click the MIDI tab.
2 Enter the number of samples (–10,000 to 10,000) for the Global MIDI Playback Offset set- ting. A negative value causes the MIDI tracks to play back earlier than the audio tracks; a posi- tive value causes the MIDI tracks to play back later.
Approximate offset To allow for monitoring latency in Pro Tools host- in samples based systems, set the offset to a value that is Audio Transient recorded from MIDI device roughly equivalent to the H/W Buffer Size.
MIDI Note On 3 Click OK.

Random Enable Random and enter a percentage between 0 and 100%. Random is only available in Legato/Overlap Select to extend each note to the the expanded Real-Time Properties window. following note. The specified duration is then added to create a consistent amount of overlap.
812 Pro Tools Reference Guide Duration Amount If the Ticks/Notes pop-up menu is set to ticks, en- ter a duration as a number of quarter notes plus a number of ticks, such as 4|000. If the Ticks/Notes pop-up menu is set to a note size, enter a number to by which to multiply that note size, such as seven 16th notes.

Property to MIDI Input and Thru.
Velocity Velocity lets you change MIDI velocity by a per- centage or by constant value. Enter a percentage value in the Dynamics field to scale velocity around the median velocity of 64. You can also en- ter an offset by adding (+) or subtracting (–) values between 1 and 127. You can also enter Minimum and Maximum Velocity value limits. The Mini- Real-Time Properties Ticks/Notes pop-up menus mum (Min) and Maximum (Max) Velocity options Minimum and Maximum Durations are only available in the expanded Real-Time Properties window.
Enable either or both minimum and maximum du- rations and enter a value in quarter notes plus ticks Velocity can be applied as a Real-Time Prop- or as a multiple of a note size. menu. Enter the Delay amount in Tick or Millisec- ond values. Delay is limited to a maximum of value of 2000 ms or 999 ticks.

enabled MIDI and Instrument tracks. However, clips with clip-based Real-Time Proper- ties will take precedence over any track-based Real-Time Properties. 1 Choose Setup > Preferences, and click the MIDI tab.

Real-Time Properties Effects Displayed in the MIDI Event List The MIDI Event List takes into account the effect of Real-Time Properties whenever the Display option Events as Modified by Real-Time Properties is enabled. When editing or inserting an event in a track or clip with active Real-Time Properties, Pro Tools takes the properties into account and ed- its or inserts the event in a different place if neces- sary to match the effect of the Real-Time Proper- ties.
Real-Time Properties Effects Displayed in the Edit Window MIDI events appear where they sound in the Edit window, MIDI Editor windows, and the Score Ed- itor window, with the Display Events as Modified option enabled in the by Real-Time Properties MIDI preferences. However, MIDI events are not actually written to their modified locations until you write the Real-Time Properties to the track or clip.
When moving MIDI events, the actual locations (pitch or time) move by the amount the events are dragged, but the effect of Real-Time Properties is displayed.
When inserting an event in a track or clip with ac- tive properties, Pro Tools takes the properties into account and edits or inserts the event in a different place if necessary to match the effect of the Real- Time Properties.

820 Pro Tools Reference Guide Mirrored MIDI Editing MIDI Input Display Mirrored MIDI Editing lets you edit MIDI clips The MIDI Input display shows note and chord and have your edits apply to every copy of the names as they are received by tracks shown in the same MIDI clip. This can be particularly useful Edit and MIDI Editor windows. For example, when editing looped MIDI clips. when an Instrument or MIDI track is record-en- abled and a MIDI controller is assigned to its Input, If Mirrored MIDI Editing mode is disabled, note names and recognized chords are displayed as and you edit notes of looped clips in Notes you play your controller.
view, the edit flattens the loop. Similarly, ed- iting clip groups separates the clip groups.

Superimposed Notes View The Notes pane in MIDI Editor windows lets you view and edit MIDI notes superimposed from different MIDI and Instrument tracks such that you can easily edit multitrack arrangements in a single constellation of color-coded notes. The Notes pane lets you edit MIDI notes much the same way as in Notes view on MIDI and Instrument tracks in the Edit window, except you can edit MIDI notes on multiple MIDI and In- strument tracks at the same time.
You can also view and edit MIDI notes in Notation view in the Notes pane (see “Notation View” on page 832).
MIDI Editor window, Notes pane with five superimposed Instrument tracks Color Coding MIDI Notes You can color code MIDI notes by Track or by Velocity.
Color Coding by Track When Color Coding by Track is enabled, tracks in the MIDI Editor are temporarily assigned one of sixteen fixed colors, in the order they appear in the Tracks List.
828 Pro Tools Reference Guide To Color Code MIDI notes by Track:
Enable the Color Code MIDI Notes By Track button  Color Code MIDI Note By Track Enabled in MIDI Editor window Color Coding by Velocity When Color Coding by Velocity is enabled, MIDI notes are all the same red color (hue) on all tracks dis- played, and MIDI notes vary in color saturation (from a light red to a dark red) based on the Note On ve- locity. Notes with low velocities are lighter in color and those with high velocities are darker.
To Color Code MIDI notes by Velocity:
Enable the Color Code MIDI Notes By Velocity button.
 Color Code MIDI Notes By Velocity enabled in MIDI Editor window No Color Coding When neither Color Coding by Track nor Color Coding by Velocity are enabled, notes are drawn in the MIDI Editor in as the same color they are in the Edit window—using the color coding options as set in the Track Color Coding settings in the Display Preferences.
No Color Code enabled in MIDI Editor window Editing MIDI Notes You can edit any note on any track shown in the Notes pane. You edit MIDI notes in the Notes pane the same way as in Notes view for MIDI and In- strument tracks in the Edit window, except that notes from different tracks are superimposed.
For more information on editing MIDI notes, see Chapter 35, “MIDI Editing.” “Blad Pad Midi” track pencil-enabled Manually Inserting MIDI Notes With the Pencil tool, click at a time and pitch lo- Using the Pencil tool, you can manually insert cation to insert a MIDI note on the pencil-en- MIDI notes to any pencil-enabled track shown in abled track with the default Note On velocity the Notes pane. If only one track is viewed in the and the selected Default Note Duration.
MIDI Editor window, that track will automatically be pencil-enabled. If multiple superimposed tracks are shown in the Notes pane, MIDI notes will be inserted on the pencil enabled tracks only.
For more information on manually inserting MIDI notes, see “Inserting MIDI Notes” on page 780.

Note Selector Use the Note Selector tool to select notes on one or more staves in the Score Editor window. Selected notes highlight blue. Selected notes can be deleted, moved, transposed, and processed (using Event Operations).
Press the F7 key to select the Note Selector tool.
The Note Selector tool makes a range selection that only includes MIDI notes and Marquee zooming with the Zoomer tool velocities. It does not include any other MIDI The zoomed area fills the entire Edit window.
data, such as program changes or continuous controller data.

Editor window If you want triplet note values, also select the Trip- let option. Mirrored MIDI Editing Mirrored MIDI Editing lets you edit MIDI clips and have your edits apply to every copy of the same MIDI clip. This can be particularly useful when editing looped MIDI clips.
Default Note Duration selector In Mirrored MIDI Editing mode, the Mirrored MIDI Editing button flashes Red once as an edit is Default Note On Velocity made to alert you that your edit is being applied to more than one clip.

the Edit window, MIDI Editor window, or Score Editor window.

Enable the Keep Window On Top option in the Score Editor window menu to ensure that the win- dow remains in the foreground when working with other windows. When this option is disabled, clicking on another window (such as the Edit or Mix windows) brings that window to the front and the Score Editor window (whichever one you are working with) may be hidden behind it.

how individual MIDI and Instrument tracks appear in the Score Editor. You can set the Clef, Display Quantization, and whether or not to display the track at “concert pitch” or as a transposing instru- ment (such as a B-flat tenor sax).

• Grand Staff Follow Globals • Treble Clef When the Follow Globals option is selected, the track attributes use the global attributes settings • Bass Clef (see “Globals” on page 847). This option is en- • Alto Clef abled for all tracks by default.
• Tenor Clef Display Quantization Display Transposition The Display Quantization setting lets you select the From the Key pop-up menu, select the key of quantization grid for the display of MIDI notes in Transposition for the track. For instruments that music notation. MIDI notes in Pro Tools still transpose from another octave, you can also adjust maintain their original start time and duration, but the Octave slider. For example, for a Bass Clarinet this setting affects what note values are displayed.
track, select the Key of B-flat and set the Octave The notation display is quantized to the nearest se- slider to +1. This way, when you print the Bass lect note value.
Clarinet part, the player will be able to read the part Straighten Swing and concert pitch will sound correctly a major ninth below where the part is written.
When enabled, the Straighten Swing option causes “swung” notes to be displayed as straight notation.
Attributes For example, a run of swung eighth notes will be displayed as straight eighth notes.
The Attributes tab lets you specify the notation dis- play quantization settings for the selected track. If Allow Note Overlap the Clef is set to Grand Staff, you can also set the Split Point to divide notes between the treble and Pro Tools only displays a single rhythmic line on a bass clefs.
single staff. When two notes that start at different times overlap, the first note will be truncated when the second note begins. If the Allow Note Overlap option is enabled, Pro Tools displays the full length of overlapping notes using tied notes.
846 Pro Tools Reference Guide Split Point Score Setup Window Options When the Clef for the selected track is set to Grand Staff, the selected Split Point setting determines at which pitch the notes are placed in either the upper or lower staff of the Grand Staff.
Automatic When selected, Pro Tools splits notes between the upper and lower staves of the Grand Staff based on logical note groupings.
Fixed When selected, you can specify a particular pitch at which to split notes between the upper and lower staves of the Grand Staff.
Globals The Globals tab provides the same settings as the Attributes tab (see “Attributes” on page 846). You can select which tracks follow the settings in the Globals tab in the Attributes tab for each track.
Score Setup The Score Setup window lets you set up the page layout and staff spacing. You can enter the Title and Composer for the score. You can also select what elements of the score to display. Changes to Score Setup window the Score Setup update instantly in the Score Edi- Information tor.

The Score Editor provides simple score editing tools for entering, moving, and transposing MIDI notes as music notation:
• The Trim tool lets you lengthen or shorten the duration of notes.
• The Note Selector tool lets you make object se- Selecting notes with the Grabber tool lections of notes in the score.
• The Grabber tool lets you select and move notes Transposing Notes to another time or pitch location on the same You can use the Grabber tool or the Pencil tool to track.
manually transpose a note up or down. You can • The Pencil tool lets you insert notes and move also use the Transpose Event Operation (Event > them to another time or pitch location on the Event Operations > Transpose) to transpose se- same track.
lected notes.
Selecting Notes To transpose a single note:
1 Click the Grabber tool.
You can use either the Note Selector tool or the Grabber tool to select notes on one or more staves.
2 Drag the note you want to transpose up or down.
To select notes on one or more staves with the Note Selector tool:
Click the Note Selector tool.
Drag in the score to highlight the notes you want to select.
Transposing a note with the Grabber tool To transpose all selected notes:
Click the Grabber tool.
Drag around the notes you want to Transpose to Selecting notes with the Note Selector tool select them.
3 Drag any of the selected notes up or down to Inserting Notes transpose them.
Use the Pencil tool to manually insert notes in the Score Editor. When you insert a note, the Score Editor automatically places it on the Grid and cre- ates rests where appropriate.
You can also enter notes by recording MIDI, im- porting MIDI from a file, or using Step Input Transposing selected notes with the Grabber tool (Event > Event Operations > Step Input).

The Insert submenu lets you insert key changes, meter changes, and chord symbols into the score at the location of the Edit cursor.
Key Inserts a key change.
Inserts a meter change.
Meter Chord Inserts a chord symbol.
Entering multiple note of the same pitch with the Line Pencil tool Cut Notation Display Track Settings The Cut command cuts the current Edit selection. Choosing the Notation Display Track Settings op- tion opens the Notation Display Track Settings di- Copy alog (see “Notation Display Track Settings” on page 845).
The command copies the current Edit selec- Copy tion.
Score Setup Paste Choosing the option opens the Score Score Setup Setup dialog (see “Score Setup” on page 847).
The Paste command pastes the clipboard to the current Edit selection or at the current Edit cursor Send to Sibelius location. Any MIDI notes already present at the same location are overwritten. Choosing the Send to Sibelius option command sends the score exactly as it appears in the Score Merge Editor window to Sibelius notation software (see “Exporting Scores” on page 855).
The Merge command pastes the clipboard to the current Edit selection or at the current Edit cursor Print Score location and merges the pasted data with any MIDI notes already present at the same Choosing the Print Score command prints the location. score (see “Printing Scores” on page 856).
Clear Key Changes The command clears (deletes) the current Clear Edit selection.
You can add, edit, and delete Key Signatures in your score. Key Signatures affect the note spelling Event Operations of MIDI notes in the score. For example, MIDI The Event Operations submenu lets you select any note number 58 (“B-flat 3”) is B-flat in F major, of the Event Operations windows (such as Quan- but it is A-sharp in B major.
tize or Change Velocity).
You can also add and delete Key Signatures in the Key Signatures ruler in the Edit Open in MIDI Editor window or in any MIDI Editor window.
Choosing the Open in MIDI Editor option opens the tracks shown in the Score Editor in a MIDI Editor To add a Key Signature:
window.
1 With the Note Selector, Grabber, or Pencil tool, Right-click at the location in the score where Open in MIDI Event List you want to change key and select Insert > Key The option opens the se- Change.
Open In MIDI Event List lected track in the MIDI Event List.
2 In the Key Change dialog, select the mode (ma- jor or minor), the key, its location and range, and how you want it to affect pitched tracks.
852 Pro Tools Reference Guide 3 Click OK.
The selected Key Signature is displayed in the score and the note spellings for notes after the key change are updated accordingly.
To edit a Key Signature:
1 With the Grabber tool, double-click the Key Signature you want to edit.
2 Enter values in the Key Change dialog and click OK.
Meter Change dialog 3 If you want the inserted meter event to fall To delete a Key Signature:
cleanly on the first beat of the nearest measure, With the Grabber tool, Option-click (Mac) or  select the Snap To Bar option.
Alt-click (Windows) the Key Signature you want to delete. 4 Select a note value for the number of clicks to sound in each measure. For a dotted-note click value, select the dot (.) option in addition to the note value.
Meter Changes You can add, edit, and delete Meters in your score. For some meters, it may be desirable to use a Meter changes affect the number of bars in the dotted value for the click. For instance, if us- score (and in your session). When changing meter, ing a meter of 6/8, a dotted quarter note click notes are not moved, but the barlines will be re- (yielding two clicks per measure) is generally drawn. more suitable than a straight eighth note click (six clicks per measure).
You can also add and delete Meters in the Meter ruler in the Edit window and in MIDI 5 Click OK to insert the new meter.
Editor windows.
To edit a Meter change:
To add a Meter change:
With the Grabber tool, double-click the Meter Do one of the following: event you want to edit.
• Choose Event > Time Operations > Change Enter values in the Meter Change dialog and Meter.
click OK.
• With the Note Selector, Grabber, or Pencil tool, Right-click at the location in the score To delete a Meter change:
where you want to change meter and select With the Grabber tool, Option-click (Mac) or  Insert > Meter Change.
Alt-click (Windows) the Meter event you want to delete.
In the Meter Change dialog, enter the Location and Meter for the meter change.
Chord Symbols and Diagrams Chord Quality selector Chord selector Bass selector You can add, edit, and delete chord symbols and chord diagrams (guitar tablature) in your score.
Chord symbols have no effect on MIDI data. Also, chord symbols are not included when exporting MIDI data from Pro Tools to a MIDI file, but they are included when exporting to a Sibelius file (.sib) or when using the Send to Sibelius command.
You can also add and delete chord symbols in the Chord Symbol ruler in the Edit window and in MIDI Editor windows. However, Chord Diagrams (guitar tab) are only displayed in the Score Editor window.
To insert a chord symbol:
1 With the Note Selector, Grabber, or Pencil tool, Right-click at the location in the score where you want to add a chord symbol and select In- sert > Chord.
Chord Diagrams 2 In the Chord Change dialog, select the name for Chord Change dialog the root of the Chord from the Chord selector 3 Select the quality from the Chord Quality (such as D).
selector (such as major or minor).
4 Select the bass note of the chord from the Bass selector (such as B-flat for an inverted G minor chord).
5 Select a Chord Diagram (guitar tablature) from the available options.
6 Click OK.
The selected chord symbol is placed above the top staff in the system.
854 Pro Tools Reference Guide Exporting Scores Pro Tools lets you export the score from your ses- sion as a Sibelius (.sib) file. You can then open your score in Sibelius as transcribed by Pro Tools, edit the notation in Sibelius, and print the score and parts from Sibelius.
Sibelius 5.x or higher is required to open .sib files exported from Pro Tools.
Chord symbol and diagram in the score To export a score from Pro Tools:
1 Configure the score for your session in Score To edit a chord symbol:
Editor window.
1 With the Grabber tool, double-click the chord symbol you want to edit. 2 Choose File > Export > To Sibelius.
2 Make changes in the Chord Change dialog, and 3 Select a destination and click Save.
click OK.
To send the score from your Pro Tools session to Sibelius, do one of the following:
To move a chord symbol:
1 Configure the score for your session in the With the Grabber tool, drag the chord symbol to  Score Editor window.
a new location.
2 Do one of the following:
To delete a chord symbol:
• Choose File > Send to Sibelius.
With the Grabber tool, Option-click (Mac) or  • Right-click in the Score Editor and choose Alt-click (Windows) the chord symbol you Send to Sibelius.
want to delete.
Pro Tools exports all Instrument and MIDI tracks To display or hide chord symbols in the score: shown in the Score Editor to Sibelius as a .sib file and launches Sibelius if it is installed on your com- In the Score Settings dialog (File > Score  puter. You can then further edit the notation in Si- Setup), enable or disable the Chord Symbols belius and print the score or parts from Sibelius.
option.
To display or hide chord diagrams (guitar tab) in the score:
In the Score Settings dialog (File > Score  Setup), enable or disable the Chord Diagrams option.
Printing Scores Pro Tools lets you print the score from your Pro Tools session. The score will be printed ex- actly as it appears in the Score Editor window.
Configure the Score Setup and show or hide any MIDI and Instrument tracks. Only those tracks that are shown in the score will be printed.
To print the score from your Pro Tools session:
Open the Score Editor window and configure your score.
Choose Score.
2 File > Print Configure the Print dialog as necessary and click (Mac) or (Windows).
Print OK Press Command+P (Mac) or Control+P (Windows) to print the score.

Number of Events The number of MIDI events on the selected track are displayed at the top of the MIDI Event List.

864 Pro Tools Reference Guide Part VII: Arranging Chapter 39: Time, Tempo, Meter, Key, and Chords In Pro Tools, editing and arranging is typically Timebase Rulers and done on tracks against a fixed Timeline.
Conductor Rulers Clips and events can be anchored to points in abso- lute time, or relative time, as measured on the ses- sion’s Timeline. The Timeline includes two differ- ent types of rulers for measuring time: Timebase rulers and Conductor rulers.
Pro Tools also provides a Markers ruler for Memory Location (see Chapter 40, “Memory Locations”).
The Timebase rulers can measure time in two dif- ferent ways: sample-based time (absolute time) All rulers displayed and tick-based time (relative time). For example, the Minutes:Seconds ruler measures absolute time Timebase Rulers and the Bars|Beats ruler measures relative time.
Any or all of the following Timebase rulers can be displayed at the top of the Edit window and in The relative time of the Bars|Beats ruler is deter- MIDI Editor windows:
mined in part by tempo and meter. Tempo, Meter, and Key Signature rulers reside in Conductor Bars|Beats Displays the Time Scale in bars and tracks. Tempo and meter events affect the timing beats. Use this Time Scale if you are working with of tick-based tracks, and also provide the tempo musical material that must align with bars and and meter map for the Bar:Beat grid and Click.
beats. The Bars|Beats Time Scale is relative and is You can edit tempo events in the Tempo ruler or affected by the session’s tempo and meter map.
Tempo Editor.
Key changes can affect MIDI notes when applying transposition. You can edit key and key changes in the Key Signature ruler.
• 119.88 drop To ensure your tracks align with the bars and • 120 fps beats in your session, record with a click (see • 120 fps drop “Recording with a Click” on page 528). not be set as the Main Time Scale. The secondary Displays the Time Scale in min- timecode ruler can be set to show any Pro Tools Minutes:Seconds utes and seconds. As you zoom in farther with the supported frame rate.

rates as the Timecode ruler.
The Minutes:Seconds Time Scale is absolute.
Feet+Frames Displays the Time Scale in feet and Timecode Displays the Time Scale in frames. The frames for referencing audio-for-film projects. Displays the Time Scale in samples. This Samples • 23.976 fps format is very useful for high-precision sample ed- iting. The Samples Time Scale is absolute, based • 24 fps on the sample rate.

For more information on Memory Location markers, see Chapter 40, “Memory Locations.” Displaying Rulers To display all rulers:

Select View > Rulers > None (“Main Time  Ruler View selector in the Edit Window.
Scale” on page 870.) To add a specific ruler to the display, such as the Markers ruler, for instance:

 Main Time Scale While all Timebase rulers can be displayed simul- taneously in the Edit and MIDI Editor windows, there is only one that represents the Main Time Scale. This ruler is also called the Main Timebase ruler.
The Main Time Scale determines the time format Main Counter selector (in the Edit window) used for:
If a Timebase ruler is displayed, click its name  • The Main Counter in the Transport so it becomes highlighted.
• The Main Counter at the top of the Edit window Setting the Main Time Scale to the timebase or MIDI Editor window currently displayed in the Sub Counter • Start, End, and Length values switches the two Time Scales, setting the Sub Time Scale to the previous timebase of the • Pre- and post-roll amounts Main Time Scale.
• Grid and Nudge values The Main Time Scale can be set to the following formats:
• Bars|Beats • Minutes:Seconds • Timecode Switching the Main Time Scale from Bars|Beats to • Feet+Frames Min:Sec • Samples Navigating with the Main Counters Setting the Main Time Scale The Main Counters (at the top of the Edit window To set the Main Time Scale, do one of the and in the Transport window) provide a convenient following:
way to navigate to a specific time location.

870 Pro Tools Reference Guide When working in Bars|Beats, you will often want The Sub Counter to specify tick values for a number of operations, including:
Below each Main Counter is a Sub Counter that can be shown or hidden. The Sub Counter provides • Placing and spotting clips an additional timing reference.

Duration pages of the Event Operations window • Setting the Grid and Nudge values To show the Sub Counter in the Transport window:

rulers such as Minutes:Seconds will scale to fit any tempo changes while bar lengths remain constant.
The following table lists the number of ticks for each of the main note sizes:
Sub Counter selector (in the Edit window) Note Value Normal Dotted Triplet Tick-Based Timing 1/2 note 1920 2880 1280 nal MIDI resolution of 960,000 pulses per quarter note (ppq). However, when the Time Scale is set to 960 ppq and the Timebase is tick-based.
In the Bars|Beats Time Scale, Pro Tools is tick-based (960 ticks to a quarter note). Some amount of sample-rounding may occur when placing events at certain locations (see “Sample Rounding and Edit Operations” on page 874).
Tick-Based Audio and MIDI Tick- and Sample-Based Timebases Tick-based audio is fixed to the relative time of the Bars|Beats Timebase ruler, and moves relative to Pro Tools lets you set any track timebase to either the sample Timeline when tempo and meter sample-based or tick-based.
changes occur. However, MIDI and Elastic Audio events, and tick-based audio clips (non–Elastic Audio tracks in Pro Tools are sample-based by de- Audio) respond differently to tempo changes in re- fault. This means that audio clips and events are lo- spect to duration. MIDI note events and Elastic cated at specific sample locations, and they do not Audio clips change their duration when tempo is move from that location if the tempo changes in adjusted, while non–Elastic Audio audio clips do the session—even though their bar and beat loca- not. Tempo changes affect only the location of the tions do change.
beginning (or sync point) of each audio clip in a MIDI and Instrument tracks in Pro Tools are tick- tick-based (non–Elastic Audio) audio track.
based by default. This means that MIDI clips and You can select whether a track is sample-based or events are located at a specific bar and beat loca- tick-based when it is created, or change timebases tions, and they do not move from that location if later.
the tempo changes in the session—even though its sample location does change.
To have all new tracks default to tick-based, enable the New Tracks Default to Tick Time- Sample-Based Audio and MIDI base option in the Editing Preferences.
With a sample-based audio track, all audio clips in Tick-Based Elastic Audio the track have an absolute location on the absolute Timeline (for example, on the Samples or Min- Tick-based Elastic Audio tracks automatically ap- utes:Seconds Timebase ruler). Audio stays fixed to ply Real-Time (or Rendered) Elastic Audio pro- the sample time, regardless of any tempo or meter cessing (TCE) when changing tempo. For more in- changes occur in a session.
formation, see “Tempo Changes and Tick-Based Elastic Audio Tracks” on page 879.
On sample-based MIDI and Instrument tracks, all MIDI events in the track have an absolute location Switching Timebases on the absolute Timeline (for example, on the Sample or Minutes:Seconds Timebase ruler).
All Pro Tools tracks can be switched between be- MIDI events stay fixed to the sample time, regard- ing sample-based or tick-based.

If the clip contains a sync point, the sync point de- termines where the audio clip is fixed to the grid.

Generally, when applying tempo changes with Minimizing Automation Drift tick-based MIDI and Elastic Audio, this is desir- In general, automation drift is more pronounced able. In fact, you will probably want to set all of the with larger sample-based audio clips. By creating tracks in your session that contain automation to many small clips, the effects of automation drift tick-based. This way any tempo changes affect all can be minimized, because the start points of each tick-based track automation and track material subsequent clip changes as the tempo changes.
equally. However, when working with sample- When tempo is decreased, automation expands.
based (non-Elastic) audio on tick-based tracks, When tempo is increased, automation contracts.
tempo changes affect the start location of audio clips, but not their duration.
Since the length of each sample-based audio clip in a track is unaffected by changes in tempo, existing automation data is not aligned with the audio after a tempo change is made to a tick-based audio track.

Changing Clip Overlap/Underlap After tempo changes with tick-based audio, and other edits, clips may overlap in undesired ways.
To correct this, a clip can be brought to the front, or sent behind neighboring clips as needed.
To change the clip overlap or underlap:
1 In the Edit window, choose the clip or clips you want to re-order.
Tempo conformed audio, tempo curve 2 Do one of the following:
Conform to Tempo can only be applied to • Choose Clip > Bring to Front to make the clip clips and cannot be applied to clip groups. To overlap the neighboring clips.
conform clip groups to the tempo, ungroup • Choose Clip > Send to Back to make the clip the clip group, then apply Conform to Tempo underlap the neighboring clips.
to the underlying clips, and then regroup those clips.
If multiple overlapping clips are selected, Pro Tools will apply the command to each clip as Alternate Playlists and Tempo that clip relates to the neighboring clip on the right.
Alternate playlists on Elastic Audio-enabled, tick- based tracks are only affected by tempo changes.
Conform to Tempo Otherwise, alternate playlists are not affected by Pro Tools lets you conform clips on Elastic Audio- editing Warp or Event markers in the main playlist.
enabled tracks (both sample- and tick-based) to the session tempo. If necessary, the Conform to Tempo command analyzes the selected clips to determine Graphic Tempo Editor its tempo and duration (in bars and beats), and then applies Elastic Audio processing to conform the The Tempo Editor, a resizeable window beneath clip to match the session tempo. the Tempo ruler in the Edit Window, lets you view and edit tempo information graphically.
To conform a clip to the session tempo:
Select the clip you want to conform to the ses- 1 Displaying the Tempo Editor sion tempo.
The Tempo Editor is an expansion of the Tempo Do one of the following:
2 ruler, opening in the rulers section of the Edit Win- • Choose Tempo. dow.
Clip > Conform to • Right-click the clip you wan to conform to Tempo Editor expand/collapse triangle tempo and select Tempo.
Conform to Tempo Resolution Tempo Density Pro Tools analyzes the clip’s entire file for Elastic Add Tempo Change Audio events and tempo. If a tempo is detected, Tempo events and a duration in bars and beats is determined, the clip conforms automatically to the session tempo.
If a tempo and duration is not detected (such as with a clip that is a single drum hit or with a long clip with no clear tempo), the clip is not conformed to the session tempo.
You can also use telescoping warp in Warp view to manually conform a long clip to the session tempo. See “Telescoping Warp” on Tempo Editor page 1013.

Tempo Editor Select Tempo, then select  View > Rulers > Editor.

Individual tempo events can be dragged with  any of the Grabber tools to adjust their location or value.
Zooming and Scrolling in the Tempo Editor A group of selected tempos can be scaled up or  down with the Trim tool.
To zoom in vertically in the Tempo Editor:
New tempo events can be drawn in with the Click the Tempo Editor Zoom In button (“+”).   Pencil tool to replace existing ones.
To zoom out vertically in the Tempo Editor:
Tempo events can be copied and pasted,  Click the Tempo Editor Zoom Out button (“–”). nudged, and shifted.
 Drawing Tempo Events Tempo events can be drawn in the Tempo Editor using the follow Pencil tool shapes.
The Free Hand shape draws freely ac- Free Hand cording to the movement of the mouse. The shape is reproduced as a series of steps according to the Zoom In and Out buttons in the Tempo Editor Tempo Edit Density setting. (For more informa- To scroll vertically in the Tempo Editor, do any of tion, see “Selecting Tempo Edit Density” on the following:
page 882.) Click the Scroll Up Arrow to scroll up.
 Line The Line shape draws in a straight line from Click the Scroll Down Arrow to scroll down.
 click to release. Tempo values change in steps ac- cording to the Tempo Edit Density and Resolution.
Drag the Scroll bar up or down.
 The Parabolic shape draws the best pos- Parabolic sible curve to fit your freehand drawing. The shape is reproduced as a series of steps according to the Tempo Edit Density setting.
S-Curve The S-Curve shape draws a best possible fit of an S-Curve to your freehand drawing. The shape is reproduced as a series of steps according to the Tempo Edit Density setting.
The Triangle, Square, and Random Pencil tool shapes do not apply to tempo events.
Selecting Tempo Resolution (BPM Rate) Quarter-note click Dotted quarter-note click The Tempo Editor lets you specify the beat note value for tempo events by changing the Tempo Resolution. The beat note value selected for the Tempo Resolution is usually based on the meter.
For example, in 4/4 the beat is a quarter note and in 6/8 the beat is (usually) a dotted-quarter note.
Tempo curve with different click values Selecting Tempo Edit Density The Tempo Editor lets you specify the density of tempo events created in the Tempo ruler when you draw a tempo curve with the Pencil tool.
Tempo Resolution selector and pop-up menu To select the Tempo Resolution (BPM rate) for Pencil tool edits, do one of the following:
Click the Tempo Resolution selector and select  the appropriate note value from the pop-up menu. Linear Sample Display The display of the Time- line is sample-based and the location of tick-based events (like Bars and Beats) shift after a tempo curve is drawn. That is, as you change tempo, the location of bars and beats move against the fixed sample-based Timeline (such as Minutes:Sec- onds). When working with sample-based material, such as when you are tracking or mixing, make sure that Linear Sample Display is selected. This is especially true if you intend to align the tempo and meter map to sample-based events.

Tempo Start and End Displays the tempo, in beats per minute (BPM), for the start and end points of the selected range. Changing the tempo causes the end time value to change.
Specifies and displays a numerical and Curvature graphic representation of the tempo curve. Nega- tive numbers indicate a more rapid tempo change at the beginning of the time range, and positive numbers indicate a more rapid tempo change at the end of the time range. This value can be set with the Curvature slider.

Specifies and displays a numerical and Curvature graphic representation of the tempo curve. Identify Beat Command The Identify Beat command lets you establish a tempo/meter map for audio that was recorded without a click, or for imported audio with un- known tempos.
The Identify Beat command analyzes a selection range (usually with a distinct number of beats or Stretch page (Advanced Option) measures) and calculates its tempo based on the Advanced When this option is selected, the specified meter. In doing this, Bar|Beat Markers selection range changes to the Main Time Scale for the calculated tempo are inserted and appear in format, and additional and modified options be- the Tempo ruler at the beginning and end of the se- come available.
lection; in addition, meter events are inserted into the Meter ruler.
Selection Start and End Specifies the range of tempo events that you want to modify. When an Use Beat Detective to generate Bar|Beat Edit selection is made, the Start and End fields will Markers within a selection that includes display the selection boundaries.
rhythmic changes on beats or sub-beats (see Chapter 34, “Beat Detective”).
Stretch To End Specifies a new end point for the clip to which you want to apply the selected tempo Bar|Beat Markers events. Changing the end point causes the Stretch percentage to change.
Bar|Beat Markers look similar to tempo events, but instead have small blue triangles to indicate their Stretch To Start (Advanced Option) Specifies a location.
new start point for the clip to which you want to apply the selected tempo events. Changing the end point causes the Stretch percentage to change.
Stretch Specifies the percentage of time for the se- lected tempo events to cover.
Bar|Beat Marker Choosing Bar|Beat Markers or 4 In the Bar|Beat Markers dialog, specify the start Tempo Events and end locations for the inserted Bar|Beat Markers. Since this example deals with a one- Because tempo events are tick-based, and Bar|Beat bar loop, enter 1|1|000 and 2|1|000.
Markers are sample-based, they cannot be mixed.
If a session contains tempo events and you attempt to insert Bar|Beat Markers, existing tempo events are converted to Bar|Beat Markers (and vice versa).
Tempo events can also be manually converted to Bar|Beat Markers and vice versa. When converting between Tempo events and Bar|Beat Markers, some amount of sample rounding may occur.
To use either tempo events or Bar|Beat Markers:
1 Press Command (Mac) or Control (Windows) and click the Add Tempo Change button. Identify Beat dialog 2 Select either Tempo Events or Bar|Beat 5 If necessary, specify a time signature for the Markers from the pop-up menu. start and end range.
6 Click OK to automatically calculate the new tempo and insert the necessary Bar|Beat Mark- ers and meter events. Any existing tempo and meter events residing within the selection are deleted.
Tempo ruler pop-up menu Identifying Beats Identifying the beat of a one-bar drum loop and creating Bar|Beat Markers for it:
1 Place a one-bar drum loop at the beginning of an audio track.
2 Select View > Rulers > Samples. This ensures that the selected audio material will be sample- Bar|Beat Markers inserted accurate.
When working with a selection, the Identify Beat 3 Select the audio clip with the Time Grabber tool command only calculates a single tempo for the se- and choose Event > Identify Beat.
lected range. If the tempo varies from measure to measure, or beat to beat, you’ll need to use the Identify Beat command for each tempo variance (making sure to accurately define a precise selec- tion range or beat location for the tempo change).
894 Pro Tools Reference Guide To accurately define tempos for a range of audio • Neighboring MIDI events, along with the with the Identify Beat command, make certain that Bars|Beats ruler, shrink or expand as necessary the initial selection represents an accurate length of to adjust for the new tempo.
bars and beats. You may want to first loop the se- lection on playback to see if it plays accurately (see “Loop Playback Mode” on page 513). To avoid drift, and remain sample-accurate, select the audio material with the Time Scale set to Samples rather than Bars|Beats.
When identifying beats, select as large an area as possible. For example, if you have a four-bar-long audio file you want to Dragging a Bar|Beat Marker identify, select the entire four bars instead of just one bar, in order to minimize rounding When dragging a tempo event:
errors (see “Sample Rounding and Edit Op- • The tempo event is placed at a new bar and beat erations” on page 874).
location. The sample and Timecode locations for the event are updated as well.
Dragging Bar|Beat Markers • The BPM value for the dragged tempo event re- Bar|Beat Markers can be dragged to new locations mains constant, as do any other tempo events in so they can align with audio clips that have been the session.
moved, or so that they can align to a slightly differ- • Neighboring MIDI events and audio clips on ent point within an audio clip. This results in tick-based tracks, along with the ruler, shrink or neighboring MIDI data being adjusted to align expand as necessary to adjust for the new tempo with the new tempo map.
location.
Because Bar|Beat Markers are sample-based and Editing Bar|Beat Markers tempo events are tick-based, they behave differ- ently when you drag them in the Tempo ruler.
A Bar|Beat Marker can be edited to redefine its bar and beat location, which also redefines the start or When dragging a Bar|Beat Marker:
end point of the range being analyzed for tempo.
• Its BPM value is recalculated along with the This is different from dragging a Bar|Beat Marker.
Bar|Beat Marker to its immediate left. Bar|Beat Markers to the right of the dragged marker re- main unchanged.
• Its bar and beat location is dragged with the Bar|Beat Marker. If the Bar|Beat Marker was originally placed at 3|1|000, it remains there (un- less it is edited).
• Its sample and Timecode locations change, as calculated for by the new tempo for the Bar|Beat Marker. Edit Bar|Beat dialog To edit a Bar|Beat Marker:

tion for the Bar|Beat Marker.

session to replace the default meter (of 4/4), and 4 Click OK. they can be inserted anywhere within the session for additional meter changes.

To display the Meter ruler:
With the Grabber tool, Option-click (Mac) or  Alt-click (Windows) the Bar|Beat Marker. Select Meter.
 View > Rulers > Inserting Bar|Beat Markers One Current Meter at a Time As meter events are encountered during playback, Sample-Based Material with Varying Tempos the session’s current meter is displayed in the You can insert Bar|Beat Markers one at a time by Transport window.
setting an Edit insertion point (instead of making a selection) before using the Identify Beat com- mand. The ability to identify each beat, one at a time, is especially useful when working with sam- ple-based material with varying tempos.
For instance, if you have a measure that accelerates slightly, you can insert a Bar|Beat Marker on each Current meter displayed in Transport window beat so the tempo is accurately reflected.
Inserting Meter Events To insert a meter event:
Do one of the following:
• Choose Event > Time Operations > Change Meter.
• Click the Add Meter Change button at the right of the Meter ruler.
Bar|Beat Markers on each beat After the Bar|Beat Markers are inserted, further ad- justments in the tempo map are possible by drag- ging each of the Markers, as necessary, to align with the associated beat within the audio.
Add Meter Change button 896 Pro Tools Reference Guide • With the Grabber, Control-click (Mac) or 4 Select a note value for the number of clicks to Start-click (Windows) at the location where sound in each measure. For a dotted-note click you want to insert the event. value, select the dot (.) option.
For some meters, it may be desirable to use a dotted value for the click. For instance, if us- ing a meter of 6/8, a dotted quarter note click (yielding two clicks per measure) is generally Manually inserting a meter event more suitable than a straight eighth note click (six clicks per measure).
• Double click the meter display in the Trans- port window.
5 Click OK to insert the new meter event. The new meter event is inserted and appears in the Meter ruler.
Current meter displayed in Transport window Inserted meter event 2 In the Meter Change dialog, enter the Location Each meter event has a small yellow triangle next and Meter for the meter change.
to it that indicates its location. These triangles can 3 If you want the inserted meter event to fall be selected for copying and pasting, and they can cleanly on the first beat of the nearest measure, be double-clicked to edit the meter event.
select the Snap To Bar option.
Editing Meter Events Existing meter events can be edited, deleted, cop- ied, and pasted.
To edit a meter event:
1 In the Meter ruler, double-click the meter event.
2 In the Meter Change dialog, enter a new Loca- tion or Meter for the event.
3 Click OK.

If the Timeline selection includes any audio clips on tick-based tracks, the audio clips are separated at the Start point, and the new clip containing the previous selection is shifted to the end point.
Using the pop-up menu, you can also choose to in- sert time into All or No sample-based markers and tracks. If All is selected and the selection includes any audio clips on sample-based tracks, the se- lected portions of the audio clips are separated at the Start point, and the new clip containing the pre- vious selection is shifted to the end point.

Realign The Realign controls let you choose which items shift when time is cut, as follows:
• If your Main Time Scale is set to Bars|Beats, you can choose to realign meter events only, or choose to realign a combination of Meter and Tempo rulers together, all tick-based markers and tracks, and your choice of sample-based tracks.
• If your Main Time Scale is set to an absolute timebase (such as Min:Secs), you can choose to independently realign conductor events, tick- Cut Time window based markers and tracks, and your choice of sample-based tracks.

Renumbering Bars You can use the Renumber Bars command to re- number all bars in the session, effectively chang- ing the bar locations for all clips, meter and tempo events while leaving their position intact. In doing Key Signature ruler so, however, the Timecode and sample locations of the session data are not changed. To view the Key Signature ruler, do one of the following:
To renumber bars:
Select Signature.
View > Rulers > Key  Choose Bars.
1 Event > Renumber From the Ruler View selector in the Edit win-  Specify the bar you want to renumber, along dow, select Key.
with the new bar number, then click Renumber.

Show the Key Signature ruler.

• Select View > Rulers > Key Signature Staff.
Renumber Bars dialog • Click the Show/Hide triangle on the Key Sig- nature ruler.
Key Signatures Changing Key Signatures The Key Signature ruler lets you add key signa- tures to Pro Tools sessions. Key signatures can be You can add, edit, or delete key signatures.
imported and exported with MIDI data. This is es- pecially useful when exporting MIDI sequences To add a key signature:
for use in notation programs like Sibelius (see “Ex- Place the cursor in the Timeline where you want porting Sibelius Files” on page 443). The default to add a key signature.
key signature is C major.
Do one of the following:
Key Signature Ruler • Choose Event > Add Key Change.
• Click the Plus (+) button in the Key Signature The Key Signature ruler lets you add, edit, and de- ruler.
lete key signatures. You can use key signatures to indicate key and key changes in your Pro Tools In the Key Change dialog, select the mode (ma- session. Key signatures can also be used for certain jor or minor), the key, its location and range, diatonic functions, such as transposing in key or and how you want it to affect pitched tracks.
constraining pitches to the specified key.
Click OK.

includes the key signature you want to delete and choose Edit > Clear or press Delete. To Lets you specify to where you want the key change to take effect: to the next Key Signature marker, to a selection, or to the end of the Key Change Dialog session.
The Key Change dialog lets you specify the mode Snap to Bar Snaps the Key Signature marker to (major or minor), the key (from seven flats to the barline.
seven sharps), the range affected (for example, from Bar 1 to the next key change), and whether or Edit Pitched Tracks Enabling the Edit Pitched not MIDI notes on pitched tracks are transposed or Tracks option lets you transpose existing MIDI constrained to key (see “Pitched Tracks” on notes on Pitched tracks up or down, either diatoni- page 906). The Key Change dialog opens when- cally or chromatically based on the key change, or ever you add or edit a Key Signature marker.
constrain pitches to the new key. For more infor- mation on Pitched tracks, see “Pitched Tracks” on page 906.
Transpose When the Edit Pitched Tracks option is enabled, lets you transpose existing MIDI notes on Pitched tracks up or down, diatonically or chro- matically. For example, if the session is in C major and you are adding a key change to D minor, select Edit Pitched Tracks, Transpose, Up, and Diatoni- cally to transpose notes on Pitched tracks up a whole step and then lower the third, sixth, and sev- enth scale degrees by a half step. The sequence C, D, E is transposed to D, E, F (natural, rather than Key Change dialog sharp as happens when transposing chromatically).
When transposing diatonically, chromatic notes are maintained when changing key. For example, when changing key from C major to E major, the note B-flat is transposed to D-natural. This lets you maintain “blue-notes” when changing keys and ap- plying diatonic transposition.
Constrain Pitches To Key When the Edit Pitched Tracks option is enabled, lets you constrain pitches to the notes of the new key. This means that any Pitched enabled in Playlist selector diatonic pitches of the old key on Pitched tracks that are not in the new key are individually trans- Importing Key Signatures posed to the nearest diatonic pitch of the new key.
For example, when changing from C major to D When importing MIDI (or session data with major, the sequence C, D, E, F, G changes to C- MIDI), you can select whether or not to import any sharp, D, E, F-sharp, G. key signatures stored in MIDI files.
Constraining pitches to key also constrains any Import MIDI chromatic pitches to the new diatonic scale. For The Import MIDI dialog includes the Import Key example, when changing from C major to D major, Signature from MIDI File option. When selected, the sequence C, D, D-sharp, E changes to C-sharp, key signatures (if present) are imported into D, D, E.
Pro Tools with any other MIDI data.
Pitched Tracks Pitched tracks are MIDI or Instrument tracks that can be affected by transpositions due to key changes (including constrain to key). MIDI and In- strument tracks are “pitched” by default. However, if you have MIDI or Instrument tracks assigned to drum machines or samplers, you do not want those tracks to be affected by key changes because of key mappings. You can deselect the Pitched option for specific MIDI and Instrument tracks so that they will not be affected by key changes.
Import MIDI dialog To select (or deselect) Pitched tracks:
Click the track’s Playlist selector and select (or Import Session Data  deselect) the Pitched option.
The Import Session Data dialog includes the Im- port Key Signature/Chord Map option. When se- lected, key signatures (if present) and chord mark- ers (if present) are imported into the current session with the imported session data.

Chord Quality Bass Note Extract Chords From Chord selector selector selector Selection Pro Tools lets you analyze selected MIDI clips to identify chords, and then add them to the Chord Symbols ruler in the Edit window as well as in MIDI Editor windows. Depending on the Score Setup Display settings (File > Score Setup), ana- lyzed chords will also be shown as Chord Symbols and Chord Diagrams in the Score Editor window.
When analyzing MIDI clips, Pro Tools can only recognize chords that are available in the Chord Change dialog. Chord Symbol markers are added to the Chord Symbol ruler quantized to quarter- note intervals on the Timeline. Chord extraction is useful for scoring in addition to providing chord symbols for charts and recording overdubbed per- formances.
Chord Diagrams Chord Change dialog 908 Pro Tools Reference Guide To analyze MIDI clips for chord content and add Chord Symbol markers to the Chord Symbols ruler:
1 Select one or more whole MIDI clips.
2 Choose Event > Extract Chords from Selection.
Pro Tools adds Chord Symbol markers to the Chord Symbols ruler for all chords detected in the selected MIDI clips.

View > Clip >  Separation Grabber When the Layered Editing option is enabled, any partially overlapped clips are revealed when cut- The Separation Grabber replaces the entire se- ting, clearing (deleting), or moving the overlap- lected range on the destination track Timeline.
ping clip. Note that if any clip is fully overlapped by another, larger clip, it is removed from the time- line. In Figure 1, the clip “Audio 1_01” (1) is moved (2) to fully cover one clip and partially cover another clip (3). When that clip (“Audio 1_02-01”) is cleared, the fully covered clip is gone, but the partially covered clip is revealed (4).
Moving a selection with the Separation Grabber Layered Editing Pro Tools provides the option to enable layered edit- ing so that you don’t lose partially overlapped clips while editing. This is true for both audio and MIDI.
To enable (or disable) layered editing, do one of the following:

Arranging Clips in Shuffle • Choose Edit > Snap To > Next.
Mode • Right-click the clip or Edit selection and In Shuffle mode, you can move clips freely within choose Snap to Next.
a track or onto another track, but their movement is • Press Option+Control+. (Mac) or Alt+Start+.
constrained by other clips. That is, if you place (Windows).
several clips in a track, their start and end points The selected clip snaps to the following clip on the automatically snap to each other. You can then track so that the two clips are “butt-spliced.” “shuffle” their order, but you cannot separate them from each other and you cannot make them overlap as in Slip mode. In Shuffle mode, adding another clip to the beginning of a track moves all subse- quent clips to the right by the length of the clip added.
To shuffle clips:
Enable Shuffle mode (see “Edit Modes” on page 629).
Drag a mono clip from the Clips List to an empty track. The clip snaps to the beginning of the track.
Drag a second clip from the Clips List to the same track, somewhere in the middle. The start point for the second clip snaps to the end of the first clip.
With the Time Grabber tool, drag the second clip to the beginning of the track.
Pro Tools “shuffles” the position of the two clips.
The second clip now occurs first, yet the two still cling together.
Experiment more with Shuffle mode by drag- ging additional clips to the track and rearrang- ing them.
You can also use the Snap To commands with an Edit selection that includes multiple clips Locked clips (see “Locking Clips” on page 938), on one or more tracks.
and all clips occurring after the locked clip, are not displaced when other neighboring clips are moved in Shuffle mode. If there is not enough room to place or duplicate a clip in front of a locked clip, the insertion area is disabled.

Grabber tool and placed freely within a track, or onto other tracks. Shuffling Multiple Tracks and Multichannel Clips Drag a clip from the Clips List to an empty track.
Selections across multiple tracks or on multichan- nel tracks can be shuffled. Unlike shuffling clips Drag a second clip from the Clips List to the on a single track, any partially selected clips are cut same track, somewhere in the middle. Drag the clips to different locations within the track to get a feel for moving them in Slip mode.
Try placing the second clip so that it slightly overlaps the first clip. Play back the results.
Spotting Clips Spot mode is useful for sessions in which you want to spot clips to precise locations based on any of the Time Scales. This is useful when performing post production tasks. In Spot mode you can spot a clip by specifying a Timecode frame or bar and beat location, by capturing an incoming timecode address, or by using the clip’s time stamps.
For even quicker spotting, if you are using Shuffling a clip results in cutting the channel VITC, use the Auto-Spot Clips command to spot a clip to the current Timecode frame location with the Time Grabber tool. For more informa- tion, see “Auto-Spotting Clips” on page 1346.

Right-Click Commands for Spotting Clips You can use Right-click commands with a key combination to spot clips in a track.
In earlier versions of Pro Tools, Right-click- ing in a clip with the Grabber tool would spot the clip to a selection.
To spot a clip to a selection:
Click or drag with the Selector tool to locate the cursor or make a selection in the track where you want to spot the clip.

These modes control how When a clip is created, it is time stamped relative the Grid is applied (see “Absolute and Relative to the Timecode start time specified for the ses- Grid Mode” on page 932).
sion. This Original Time Stamp is permanently stored with the clip and cannot be changed. If a clip To temporarily suspend Grid mode and is ever moved, it can easily be placed at its original switch to Slip mode while dragging a position from the Spot dialog.

Defining the Grid Value In addition to affecting the placement of clips, the Time Stamps and Matches Grid value also constrains Timeline and Edit selec- Clips with identical User Time Stamps appear to- tions, and determines how the Clip > Quantize to gether in the Matches submenu in the Right-click Grid command works.
menu when auditioning takes (see “Selecting Al- ternate Takes on a Track” on page 562). To set the Grid value:
1 Do one of the following:
• From the View > Main Counter menu, select Arranging Clips in Grid Mode the Time Scale for the Grid value.
Grid mode provides several useful capabilities for • To keep the Main Time Scale and use a differ- moving and placing clips in track playlists. This ent time format for the Grid, deselect Follow mode is especially useful for lining up clips at pre- Main Timebase in the Grid value pop-up cise intervals, as when working with a session that menu.
is bar- and beat-based. Grid boundaries, depending on the Main Time Scale, can be based on frames, bar and beat values, minutes or seconds, or a num- ber of samples.
2 Do one of the following: Absolute and Relative Grid Mode • From the Grid value pop-up menu, select the Grid mode can be applied in Absolute or Relative time value for the Grid boundaries.
mode.
In Absolute Grid mode, moving any clip snaps  the clip start to Grid boundaries. If a clip’s start point falls between beats, and the Grid is set to 1/4 notes, dragging the clip will snap its start time to the nearest 1/4 note (the current absolute Grid value). Similarly, when moving MIDI notes (and other MIDI data), the start of the first MIDI event in the selection snaps to the nearest Grid location.
Likewise, when cutting or copying and pasting clips or MIDI data while in Grid mode, the start of the clip or the start of the first MIDI event snaps to the nearest Grid location.
In Relative Grid mode, clips can be moved by  Grid (or Nudge) units. If the start point of a clip is offset from the Grid, the clip maintains its relative Grid value pop-up menu showing Bars|Beats grid offset at the new location. Similarly, when • To define a Grid based on the session’s Mark- moving MIDI notes (and other MIDI data), the ers, selections, and clip boundaries, select start of the first MIDI event in the selection main- Clips/Markers from the Grid value pop-up tains its relative offset from the nearest Grid loca- menu. tion. Likewise, when cutting or copying and pasting clips or MIDI data while in Relative Grid To increase the grid value incrementally: mode, any offset from the Grid is preserved. Also when pasting and merging MIDI data (Edit > Spe- Press Shift+Equal (=).
 cial Paste > Merge MIDI) while in Relative Grid mode, the pasted and merged MIDI data maintains To decrease the grid value incrementally:
its relative grid offset.
Press Shift+Minus (–).

Replacing Clips from the Clips Replace Clip Dialog Options List The Replace Clips command is also available in the Clips List menu. To use this command, make sure there is a clip selected in a track and a differ- ent (replacement) clip selected in the Clips List.
To replace clips using the Clips List menu:
Select a clip in a track to be replaced.
Do one of the following:
• Right-click the replacement clip in the Clips List and choose from the pop- Replace Clips up menu.
• Select the replacement clip in the Clips List and choose from the Clips List Replace Clips menu. (If the Clips List Selection Follows Edit preference is enabled, Command- Selection click (Mac) or Control-click (Windows) the selected clip to deselect it, then Command- click (Mac) or Control-click (Windows) the replacement clip to select it, and then choose from the Clips List menu.) Replace Clips Configure the Replace Clip dialog (see “Re- Replace Clip dialog place Clip Dialog Options” on page 934).
The following options are available in the Replace Click OK.
Clip dialog:
Replace: Original Clip Only Replaces only the se- Replace Clip and Multichannel Tracks lected clip with the replacement clip dragged from the Clips List.
The Replace Clip command supports dragging multichannel clips from the Clips List to multi- channel tracks, provided they are the same channel format. For example, you can replace a selected stereo clip in a stereo audio track with another ste- reo clip from the Clips List. However, you cannot replace it with two mono audio clips. Additionally, you cannot replace clips in multiple mono tracks with multichannel clips.
934 Pro Tools Reference Guide Re- Replace: All Instances of the Original Clip Clip matching uses all specified Match criteria.

as your selection with the same original start and end times will be replaced.

For example, suppose you had an audio clip for a door slam that included the creak of the door clos- ing, the actual slam, and the reverb of the slam. Us- ing a sync point for the slam sound in the clip lets you spot it to a specific time in the session.

The Shift command works the same regardless of the Edit mode. For example, adjacent clips are overlapped in Shuffle mode, the Spot dialog does Shift Command not appear when in Spot mode, and shifted mate- rial does not snap to the Grid when in Grid mode.
Use the Shift command to move track material for- ward or back in time by a specified amount. The Shift command can operate on selections, clips, MIDI notes, MIDI controller data, and Space Clips automation breakpoints.
The Space Clips command sets the space between selected clips on the timeline by a specified To shift a selection or clip:
amount in the selected timebase. This can be useful With the Selector or Time Grabber tool, select for arranging selected clips, adding space between the track material you want to shift. The se- songs when mastering, or even creating beat pat- lected material can reside on multiple tracks.
terns among other possibilities.

shifted. Entering a value in one Timebase field automatically updates the others.

In Shuffle mode, Edit-locked clips, and all clips occurring after the locked clip, are not displaced when other neighboring clips are moved.

Allow the edit. If you allow an edit that keeps any part of the clip intact, the clip remains locked.

To duplicate and extend the Edit selection:
Make an Edit selection that include audio or MIDI on a track.
Do one of the following:
• Choose Edit > Selection > Duplicate and Ex- tend.
• Press Command+Option+Control+Shift+D (Mac) or Control+Alt+Start+Shift+D (Windows).
944 Pro Tools Reference Guide The material is placed immediately after the selec- Repeating Clips tion’s end point, and duplicated the specified num- ber of times.
The Repeat command is similar to Duplicate, but lets you specify the number of times the selected In Shuffle mode, the repeated data is placed di- material is duplicated.
rectly after the end of the selection. Clips occurring after it slide to accommodate the repeated material.

tracks (see “Editing Across Multiple Tracks” on page 628).
You can repeatedly paste copied data until it com- pletely fills a selection (see “Special Cut, Copy, Paste, and Clear Commands” on page 627).
You can also loop clips (see “Clip Looping” on page 947).
To repeat a selection or clip:
If working with material that is bar- and beat- based, such as loops, set the Main Time Scale to Bars|Beats.
If you want to constrain the selection to the cur- rent Grid value, set the Edit mode to Grid.
Make an Edit selection.
Choose Repeat. In the Repeat dialog, en- 4 Edit > ter the number of times you want the material to repeat, then click OK.
Repeat dialog Press Option+R (Mac) or Alt+R (Windows) to open the Repeat dialog for any selected clips.
Chapter 42: Clip Loops and Groups Pro Tools lets you loop clips and group clips Looped clips (all iterations) display a Loop icon in (called a clip group). the lower, right corner or each loop iteration.
Looped clip Clip Looping Source clip Loop iterations Pro Tools lets you loop audio clips, MIDI clips, and clip groups. Looping clips is an easy and pow- erful way to repeat a single clip on a track or clips across tracks for composing and arranging. Loop- ing clips provides more flexibility than the Repeat and Duplicate commands.
Loop icons Looped clips repeat the source clip as many times as specified in the Clip Looping dialog, or enough Looped clip to fill the specified Loop Length (such as 30 sec- onds or until the next clip on the track). The source Looping a clip does not loop any automation clip is the original clip selected for looping. Loop associated with the source clip. Use the Copy iterations are all looped clips following the source Special and Paste Special Repeat To Fill Se- clip. In cases where a specific number of repeti- lection commands to copy automation for the tions has not been indicated, the last loop iteration source loop to all loop iterations (see “Auto- is truncated to fill to the end of the selection or mation and Looped Clips” on page 950).
specified Loop Length.
Once looped, the looped clip can be edited much like a clip group. For example, selecting and mov- ing a looped clip selects and moves the source clip and all its loop iterations together.
• Select the Loop Until End of Session or Next Creating Looped Clips Clip option. The looped clip will be repeated until the end of the session, or until the next You can create looped clips using the Loop com- clip on the track. If necessary, the last loop it- mand or using the Loop Trim tool.
eration will be truncated to fit.
