950 Pro Tools Reference Guide To copy and paste automation from the source clip Clip groups can be placed on tracks alongside stan- to loop iterations:
dard clips, and edited using many of the same ed- 1 Select the source clip. iting techniques as regular clips. Certain edits to a clip group will apply to all clips contained by the 2 Choose Edit > Copy Special and one of the Copy clip group, such as Cut or Delete. Other edits only Special commands (All Automation, Pan Auto- apply to the boundaries of the clip group and do not mation, or Plug-In Automation), depending on affect the underlining clips, such as Trim.
what automation you want to copy.
Each clip group is saved with the session as a Clip Group file (.rgrp). Clip group files can be exported from and imported into different Pro Tools ses- sions.
Selected Pan automation for Special Copy Clip groups are especially useful for:
Select the entire looped clip. • Grouping tick-based audio clips that have been separated into many small clips, such as with in- Choose 4 Edit > Paste Special > Repeat to Fill dividual hits of a drum pattern. Many such small Selection.
clips can easily be created with Beat Detective or the command, or Separate Clip At Transients imported as REX or ACID files.
• Grouping parts and sections of your session to facilitate composition and arranging. For exam- Pan automation Special Paste to Fill Selection ple, grouping the clips of a brass section during the chorus to copy it to the next chorus.
Clip Groups Clip groups are completely independent of and unique from Mix and Edit Groups.
A is a collection of any combination of clip group audio and MIDI clips that looks and acts like a sin- gle clip. Clip groups are essentially containers Editing Elastic Properties is disallowed for holding one or more clips. Clip groups can be cre- clip groups.
ated on single or multiple adjacent audio, MIDI, and Instrument tracks. Clip groups let you “nest” Clip groups are not supported on audio multiple clips into “macro” clips for groove and tracks set to Playlists view. Changing an tempo manipulation, editing, and arranging.
audio track to Playlist View automatically ungroups any clip groups on the track.
The clip group appears as one clip with the clip Creating Clip Groups groups icon in the lower left corner. Clip groups also appear in the Clips List.
To create a clip group:
1 Select one or more clips on one or more tracks.
(For more information on multitrack clip groups, see “Multitrack Clip Groups” on page 953).
Clip Group icons on an audio track and in the Clips List Clip Group Timebase Format Selecting clips to be grouped on a single audio track Clip groups are created in the same timebase for- The size of the selection determines the size of the mat (samples or ticks) as the tracks on which they clip group. The selection can start and end on any are created. Multitrack clip groups can include clip boundary, empty space, or even in the middle both sample-based and tick-based tracks.
of a clip. Selections starting or ending within a clip will separate the clip at the selection boundary when you create a clip group. Clip groups created from object-based selections include all selected and unselected clips between the first and last se- lected clip on the track.
Do one of the following:
• Choose Clip > Group.
Audio Clip Group icon on an audio track • Right-click the Edit selection and choose Group.
Press Command+Option+G (Mac) or Con- trol+Alt+G (Windows) to group selected clips.
The Group and Ungroup Clip menu MIDI Clip Group icon on a MIDI track commands apply to any Edit selection regardless of the current Track View.
Clip groups can be created from empty selections. This can be useful when working in Shuffle mode to preserve the gaps between clips.
952 Pro Tools Reference Guide If you used the Ungroup All command, the Regroup Ungrouping Clips command recreates all previous nested clip groups.

Multitrack Clip Groups The clip group disappears, revealing all underlying Multitrack clip groups (clip groups created across clips and any nested clip groups. When there are multiple tracks) are useful for grouping parts (such multiple nested clip groups, the Ungroup com- as multi-miked drum tracks), and for composing mand will ungroup the front-most top-layer clip and arranging. ated across any combination of audio, MIDI, and Instrument tracks, and can include either or both To ungroup a clip group (and include all of its tick-based or sample-based tracks.

1 Select a clip group. Multitrack clip groups work much like single-track clip groups. Multitrack clip groups appear as a sin- 2 Choose Clip > Ungroup All.
gle object across adjacent tracks.
To create a multitrack clip group:
Regrouping Clips Select clips across multiple adjacent tracks.
The Regroup menu command undoes the last Un- group command and regroups all the individual clips as a clip group. This lets you ungroup a grouped clip, edit its underlying clips, and regroup it to continue working on higher-level composition and arranging.
To regroup a clip group:
Select any clip from the ungrouped clip group.
Choose Regroup. Selecting clips to be grouped across multiple tracks 2 Clip > Press Command+Option+R (Mac) or Con- trol+Alt+R (Windows) to regroup selected clips that were formally part of a clip group.
2 Choose Clip > Group. Separated Multitrack Clip Groups You can insert, move, hide, or delete tracks in mul- titrack clip groups, but it may break the clip group.
A separated clip group displays a break in the Clip Group icon. Separated clip groups will continue to function as a single clip group, but the separated icon indicates that the clip group displayed is somehow incomplete or separated across nonadja- cent tracks.
Clip Group icon Separated Clip Group icon A clip group is separated when you do any of the following:
• Insert a track within a multitrack clip group.
• Move a track that is part of a multitrack clip group so that it is no longer adjacent with the Mixed Clip Group icon (for a multitrack clip group of sample- and tick-based audio, and tick-based MIDI) other tracks of the clip group.
• Hide a track that is part of a multitrack clip Multitrack clip groups create nested clip group.
groups of multiple clips by track before • Delete a track that is part of a multitrack clip grouping them across tracks.
group.
• Change the tempo of a mixed sample-based and The Regroup command supports multitrack tick-based clip group.
clip groups.
• Record into a clip group.
• Change playlists on a track that is part of a mul- titrack clip group.
954 Pro Tools Reference Guide • Change to Playlists view. Changing Clip Group Timebase • Apply manual warping in Warp view.
The timebase format of a clip group can be changed by:
There may be situations where you want separated • Changing the track’s timebase.
clip groups. For example, if you use the same ac- companiment on verses one and two of a song, you • Dragging the clip group to a track with a differ- can group the parts of verse one and copy them as ent timebase.
a clip group for verse two, but still have a continu- Changing the timebase creates a copy of the origi- ous vocal track in the middle of those clip groups.
nal clip group. Both clip groups (the original and If you want to delete a track and keep the the copy) appear in the Clips List, but they have clip group intact, first ungroup the clip different timebases.
group, then delete track, and then Regroup the clip group. The clip group will be recre- Converting Samples to Ticks ated intact, but without the deleted track.
When dragging a clip group from a sample-based track to a tick-based track, the length of the clip group does not change. This is because clip groups Clip Groups on Tick-Based are converted from samples to ticks after they are Tracks moved into tick-based tracks. The length of the When changing tempos, clip groups on tick-based clip group only changes with subsequent tempo tracks adjust their length by adjusting the position changes. If appropriate, change the local tempo on of all enclosed clips accordingly. This can be use- the tick-based track to match the tempo of the sam- ful for arranging rhythmic material and for playing ple-based clip group before moving a sample- back clip groups imported from REX and ACID based clip group to a tick-based track.
files.
Converting Ticks to Samples When dragging a clip group from a tick-based track to a sample-based track, the length of the clip group does not change unless it is moved to an- other time location with a different tempo. This is because clip groups are converted from ticks to samples after they are moved into the sample- based tracks.
A clip group on a tick-based audio track at 120 BPM and at 160 BPM Multitrack Clip Groups with Sample- and Tick- Editing Audio Clip Groups Based Tracks Certain audio editing commands create new clips Multitrack clip groups can include both sample- over clip groups. To use these commands and based and tick-based tracks. However, changing maintain the clip group, ungroup the clip group, the tempo separates the clip group between sam- perform the edit, and then regroup the clip group.
ple-based tracks and tick-based tracks.
The following edit commands create new clips over clip groups:
• AudioSuite processing of a grouped clip results in a new clip over the clip group.
• Consolidating a selection of a grouped clip cre- ates a new audio file and clip over the clip group.
• Recording into a clip group creates a new audio file and clip over the clip group.
• Pencil tool waveform redraw results in a new A multitrack clip group separated across sample- based and tick-based tracks after changing tempo clip over the clip group.
Tabbing to Transients and Clip Editing Clip Groups Boundaries Clip groups are edited in much the same way as With Tab to Transients enabled, the Tab key regular clips: They can be named, moved, cut, cop- moves the Location Cursor to transients and clip ied, pasted, trimmed, muted, locked, and so on.
boundaries within a clip group.
However, there are a few significant differences between editing regular clips and clip groups.
With Tab to Transients disabled, the Tab key moves the Location Cursor to clip group boundar- Editing MIDI Clip Groups ies only (and the sync point, if present).
If a MIDI clip within a clip group is modified in For more information, see “Tabbing to Tran- any way, a new clip copy is created and placed sients” on page 671.
over the clip group. For example, if you record, draw in a new note, edit MIDI controller data, or quantize a Timeline selection, a new clip is created over the clip group.
956 Pro Tools Reference Guide Trimming Clip Groups Fades and Crossfades on Clip Trimming clip groups with the Standard Trim tool Groups works the same way as trimming regular clips, re- Clip groups can have fades and crossfades just like gardless of whether you are trimming a single- regular clips. Fades only apply to audio clips. In track clip group or a multitrack clip group.
addition to crossfading between clip groups, you Trimming a clip group does not trim the underly- can also crossfade between clip groups and regular ing clips, but rather trims the clip group boundar- audio clips.
ies. All underlying clips retain their original length and location. This is true for all underlying audio and MIDI clips, and nested clip groups. If you trim the clip group shorter, underlying clips may not be heard on playback because they are outside the clip boundaries of the trimmed clip group.
When trimming clip groups with the TC/E Trim tool, it applies only to audio or MIDI clips, de- pending on which clip type in the group you trim, Two adjacent multitrack clip groups with crossfades on and creates a new clip over the clip group.
the audio tracks, but not on the MIDI track If you ungroup clips after trimming a clip group in Ungrouping a clip group removes any clip (shorter), audio clips falling outside of the bounds group level fades or crossfades. Fades and of the current clip group are trimmed to fit the clip crossfades are restored by the Regroup group boundary or removed.
command.
Recording Clip groups that contain clips with fades are When recording audio or MIDI, new clips are cre- not allowed on Elastic Audio-enabled tracks.
ated over (in front) of clip groups instead of being included in the clip group. To record into a clip group, first ungroup the clip group, then record, When changing tempo with clip groups on and then regroup the clip group. The clip group tick-based tracks, you may need to Ungroup, will be recreated intact with the newly recorded redo or create new fades, and then Regroup material. in order to maintain the fades.
For information on working with fades and crossfades in Pro Tools, see Chapter 32, “Fades and Crossfades.” Clip group files store the following metadata:
Clip Groups and Clip Gain • References to all audio files within the clip Clip gain settings for individual clips are preserved group in clip groups. Additionally, you can adjust the clip • Clip names and relative location in tracks gain settings for clip groups. Clip gain editing on • Embedded fades and crossfades clip groups behaves just like individual clip gain editing (see “Clip Gain” on page 973). • Clip gain settings for the clip groups as well as for individual clips within the group • Clip group names and format (single or multi- track) • All MIDI data present in the clip group (such as notes, controllers, Sysex, and so on) • Track names Clip group with clip gain settings shown • Elastic Audio Warp markers and TCE factor To adjust the clip gain settings for individual clips • Clip timebase (samples or ticks) within a clip group, the clips must first be un- • Sync points grouped, edited, and then regrouped.
• Loops Clip gain settings are preserved when clip groups • Tempo map are exported. Clip groups can then be imported into other sessions with the same clip gain settings. Clip group files do not store the following:
• Automation • Plug-ins Importing and Exporting Clip • Track routing Group Files • Meter map Pro Tools can export and import clip groups as clip group files (.cgrp) to do any of the following: • Clips List information • Separate clip group metadata from audio files to For more information, see “Importing and avoid unnecessary file copy operations when ex- Exporting Clip Group Files” on page 480.
porting audio clip groups composed from multi- ple source files • Export MIDI data as part of a clip group • Create multitrack loops Clip groups created in Pro Tools 10 and later (.cgrp) cannot be imported into earlier ver- sions of Pro Tools, but region groups (.rgp) created in earlier versions of Pro Tools can be imported into Pro Tools 10 and later.
958 Pro Tools Reference Guide Part VIII: Processing Chapter 43: AudioSuite Processing AudioSuite plug-ins are used to render processing AudioSuite Window and modify audio files on disk. AudioSuite pro- cessing applies non-real-time, or rendered, audio When you select a plug-in from the AudioSuite processing to selected audio clips in your menu, the AudioSuite window appears. From this Pro Tools session. Depending on how you config- floating window you can access and edit the con- ure an AudioSuite plug-in, it will either alter the trols for the selected AudioSuite plug-in.
original source audio file or create an entirely new audio source file.
For information on individual AudioSuite plug-ins included with Pro Tools, see the Audio Plug-Ins Guide.
AudioSuite Menu AudioSuite plug-ins are accessed through the Au- dioSuite menu. Plug-ins can be organized in the AudioSuite menu in Pro Tools by Flat List (with plug-ins in alphabetical order), Category, Manu- AudioSuite window, EQ III 1-Band shown facturer, or Category and Manufacturer.
For more information, see “Plug-In Menu Organization” on page 1135.
To open the AudioSuite Plug-In window:
Make an Edit selection that includes the audio clip, or clips, you want to process.
From the AudioSuite menu, select the plug-in you want.

Mono Mode Processes each contiguous channel within a selected clip as a separate mono track. For Target Button example, a clip comprised of six mono tracks would be processed as six mono tracks.
Pro Tools lets you view multiple AudioSuite plug-in windows at the same time. The AudioSuite Stereo Mode Processes each contiguous pair of plug-in window provides a Target button. Untar- channels within a selected clip as a stereo track.

Preview Button To adjust the volume for previewing AudioSuite processing:
The Preview button in the AudioSuite window lets you audition the effect of a plug-in before you pro- 1 Click the Preview Volume control.
cess the audio. By adjusting the plug-in controls 2 In the resulting pop-up fader, drag up to in- while you listen to this audio preview, you can crease the preview volume or drag down to de- fine-tune the effect. Not all AudioSuite plug-ins crease the preview volume.
support previewing.
The Preview function routes audio to the specific outputs selected for the Audition Path setting in the Outputs page of the I/O Setup dialog. Make sure you have configured this option correctly for your system or you may not be able to hear previewed audio.

Processing To render a clip with AudioSuite handles:
Because AudioSuite processes are performed on the specific clips that you select, it is important that 1 Make the audio selection that you want to ren- you select only those clips, or parts of clips, you der.
actually want to process.

settings.

Selecting Tracks for Delay or Reverb Processing • In the Handle Length field, enter a value for the Handle Length (0.00 to 60.00 seconds).
Because some AudioSuite effects such as delay The default Handle Length can be set in the and reverb add additional material to the end of the Preferences dialog.
selected audio, it is important that you make a se- lection that is longer than the original source mate- rial so that the plug-in can write it to the audio file.
If you select only the original material, without leaving additional space at the end, any reverb de- cay or delay that occurs after the end of the clip will be cut off.
To compensate for this, place the clip in a track and Handle Length field in the AudioSuite plug-in window select the audio plus an amount of blank space at • Select the Whole File option to render the the end of the clip equal to the amount of delay or whole file. lection will appear in the Timeline.

970 Pro Tools Reference Guide Conditions for AudioSuite Rendering with Handles, Fades, Clip Gain, and Metadata Most AudioSuite plug-ins provide several options for rendering clips, but only certain settings let you ap- ply AudioSuite handles. For example, AudioSuite Handles are not available with Entire Selection mode (since the point of this option is to render the whole selection only). Fades and crossfades, and clip gain set- tings are preserved or rendered depending on the File mode and AudioSuite Rendering mode settings. Ad- ditionally, clip metadata can be preserved for rendered clips under certain conditions. The following con- ditions apply.
Handle creation, and Fade, Clip Gain, and Metadata preservation criteria for AudioSuite rendering Rendering Handle File Mode Whole File Fades Clip Gain Metadata Mode Behavior Overwrite Clip By Clip No Handles Unavailable Preserved Preserved Preserved File Overwrite Entire No Handles Unavailable Rendered Rendered Preserved File Selection Individual Clip By Clip Handles Available Preserved Preserved Preserved Files created Individual Entire No Handles Unavailable Rendered Rendered Preserved Files Selection Continuous Clip By Clip Handles Unavailable Rendered Rendered Not File created at the preserved beginning and end of the new continuous file Continuous Entire Handles Unavailable Rendered Rendered Not File Selection created at the preserved beginning and end of the new continuous file Some plug-ins that rely on the Edit selection for processing (including plug-ins that require an anal- ysis pass) may not work as expected when using handles. In this case, it is recommended that the han- dle length be set to zero.
Destructive recording and Destructive Punch recording are not supported with AudioSuite rendered clips with handles.
Chapter 44: Clip Gain and Clip Effects Static and Dynamic Clip Gain Clip Gain You can have either static or dynamic clip gain set- Pro Tools provides clip-based gain for quick and tings for a single clip. Static clip gain simply easy gain matching of clips (formerly called re- means that there is a single gain setting for the en- gions in Pro Tools) from different sources in a tire clip (see “Simple Clip Gain Adjustment” on Pro Tools session. Clip-based gain is applied pre- page 975). Dynamic clip gain means that you can mixer (pre-fader and before any plug-in process- have gain settings for a clip that vary over time (see ing). This is especially useful when working with “Graphically Editing Clip Gain” on page 976).
field recordings and sample libraries in post-pro- duction sessions.
Clip Gain Info View By adjusting the clip gain for individual clips on a When Clip Gain Info view is enabled (View > single track, you can match their relative gain lev- Clip > Clip Gain Info), the Clip Gain Fader icon is els so that you do not have to execute complex shown at the beginning of the clip, in the lower left track volume automation to compensate.
corner. If the clip uses static clip gain, the static Clip Gain value (–144 dB to +36.0 dB) for the clip is displayed to the right of the Clip Gain Fader icon. For a clip that has dynamic clip gain (using breakpoint gain settings), the Clip Gain value is not shown.
Multiple clips with different (static) Clip Gain settings Clip Gain commands (including the Clip Gain The clip gain settings stay with the clip, which Fader and its Right-click commands) only apply to means you can move, and copy and paste clips the single, whole clip with which it is associated.
with their corresponding clip gain settings. Clip gain settings can be imported from AAF and OMF sequences exported from Media Composer. Like- wise, clip gain settings can be exported from Pro Tools with AAF sequences (but not OMF) to be imported into Media Composer.
All clips have a clip gain of 0 dB by default. Clip gain can be adjusted from –144 dB to +36.0 dB. Clip Gain icon To show or hide Clip Gain icon, do one of the Render Clip Gain following:
Choose Render Clip Gain in the Right-click menu Select or deselect Info.
 View > Clip > Clip Gain to render the current clip gain settings for the clip.
Press Control+Shift+“=” (Equal) (Mac) or After the clip is rendered with its new gain, the clip  Start+Shift+“=” (Equal) (Windows). gain settings are reset to 0 dB.
Show/Hide Clip Gain Line Clip Gain Fader Icon Right-Click Menu Select in the Right-click You can Right-click the Clip Gain Fader icon for Show/Hide Clip Gain Line menu to show or hide the Clip Gain Line for all any individual clip to access relevant clip gain clips.
commands for that clip.
Clip Gain Line The Clip Gain Line lets you edit the clip gain set- tings for any given clip using breakpoint editing, much like with track-based volume automation.
However, unlike track-based volume automation, the clip gain settings are always associated with Clip Gain Icon Right-click menu the clip rather than with the track. The Clip Gain Line can be shown or hidden for all clips in the The Clip Gain Fader icon Right-click menu oper- Edit window.
ates independently of the Clip Gain sub-menu that is accessible when Right-clicking on an Edit selec- tion. When rendering clip gain settings, the result- ing clip provides handles for trimming out the clip based on the Default Handle Length settings in the Processing Preferences.

Ultimate and Studio software. Clip effects use the host processor (Native processing) on all sys- tems.

986 Pro Tools Reference Guide EQ and Filters Clip Effects Settings Clip effects provides a high-quality 4-band para- (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) metric equalizer and two separate filters for shap- ing the frequency spectrum of your audio.
Input Trim and Polarity Invert EQ controls Frequency Graph The EQ section of the Clip Effects display (see Figure 2 on page 988) provides an interactive Fre- quency Graph display that shows the response Input Trim and Polarity Invert controls curve for the current EQ settings on a two-dimen- Input Trim sional graph of frequency and gain.The Frequency Graph display also lets you modify frequency, The Input Trim control sets the input gain for clip gain, and Q settings for individual EQ bands by effects before EQ processing. This lets you make dragging their corresponding points in the graph.
up gain or prevent clipping at the input stage.
The Frequency Graph display also plots the fre- quency, Q, and filter shape of the two filters (when To Trim the input signal, do one of the following:
either or both are enabled).
Click in the Input Trim field and type a Trim  value (–36.0 dB to +36.0 dB).
Adjusting EQ and Filter Controls on the Frequency Graph Click and drag up or down to adjust the  Trim Input Trim setting. You can adjust the following EQ and Filter con- trols by dragging control points directly in the Fre- Polarity Invert quency Graph display:
The Polarity Invert button at the top of the Input Control-Shift-click (Mac) or Start-Shift-click section inverts the phase (polarity) of the input sig- (Windows) the control point for any frequency nal, to help compensate for phase anomalies that band to solo that frequency band. ments or because of mis-wired balanced connec- tions.

992 Pro Tools Reference Guide Sets the amount of gain reduction that is ap- Depth Side Chain Processing plied regardless of the input signal. For example, if the Limiter is set at a Threshold of –20 dB and Depth is set at 0 dB, up to 20 dB of gain reduction is applied to the incoming signal (at 0 dB). If you set Depth to –10 dB, no more than 10 dB of gain reduction is applied to the incoming signal.

Elastic Audio is useful in several common work- Sound Design and Special Effects flows: working with loops, correcting perfor- mances, remixing, sound design and special ef- Use Elastic Audio to achieve special effects with fects, and film scoring. extreme or widely variable TCE or pitch manipu- lation. Use the Varispeed algorithm to achieve Working with Loops tape-like effects for speed and pitch change.
If you work with loops, Elastic Audio lets you pre- view your loops in Workspace browsers at their native tempo (BPM) or at the session tempo (BPM). You can even preview the loop while the session is playing back. When you find the loop Film Scoring Create and configure a new session or project:

Elastic Audio Tracks Any audio track in your Pro Tools session can be Elastic Audio enabled. Elastic Audio tracks can be either sample- or tick-based. Sample-based Elastic Audio-enabled tracks let you apply real-time or rendered Elastic Audio processing by editing in Warp view, applying Quantize, and using the TCE Trim tool. However, only tick-based Elastic Audio tracks also automatically apply Elastic Audio pro- cessing based on tempo changes in the session.
To have all new tracks be tick-based, enable Elastic Audio Plug-In pop-up menu the New Tracks Default To Tick Timebase op- tion in the Editing preferences. Any audio clips on the track temporarily go offline while they are analyzed and come back online when the analysis is finished. The waveform for When creating new tracks by dragging and audio clips that are offline for Elastic Audio anal- dropping audio from a Workspace browser, if ysis appears grayed out.
the Audio Files Conform to Session Tempo option is enabled, Pro Tools creates tick- When recording to a real-time Elastic Audio-en- based Elastic Audio-enabled tracks regard- abled track, once recording is stopped, the newly less of whether the Elastic Audio analyzed file recorded audio temporarily goes offline while it is is tick-based or sample-based. If the Audio analyzed and comes back online as soon as the Files Conform to Session Tempo option is not analysis is finished.
enabled, Pro Tools creates regular sample- based tracks.
With Pro Tools Ultimate with HDX, Elastic Audio is disallowed on voiced tracks. Use dy- namic voice allocation for tracks on which you want to use Elastic Audio.

Real-Time and Rendered Elastic Audio Processing Real-Time Elastic Audio processing means that any changes to Elastic Audio processing—whether it be a tempo change or a manual TCE warp, or Elastic Audio Plug-In selector and pop-up menu pitch shifting—take effect immediately. However, Real-Time Elastic Audio processing can be very demanding on your system’s resources. Conse- quently, you may want to use Rendered Elastic Audio processing in some cases. With Rendered 1006 Pro Tools Reference Guide Elastic Audio Plug-Ins To toggle between Real-Time and Rendered Elastic Audio processing, Command-Control- Pro Tools provides several algorithms for real- click (Mac) or Control-Start-click (Windows) time and rendered track-based Elastic Audio pro- the Elastic Audio Plug-In button.
cessing. The selected Elastic Audio plug-in deter- mines how the audio is processed. Next Setting button Previous Setting button Elastic Audio plug-in controls Polyphonic Plug-In Track Selector Lets you access any other audio The Polyphonic plug-in is a general, all-purpose track in the session. algorithm that is effective with a wide range of ma- terial. phonic plug-in supports clip-based pitch shifting.

You will lose the current plug-in settings if they are not saved before you use the Next Elastic Audio plug-in: Polyphonic and Previous Setting buttons. Save your Follow plug-in settings as presets using the Plug-In Settings menu. Selecting the Follow option enables an envelope follower that simulates the original acoustics of the audio being stretched. Click the Follow button to enable or disable envelope following.

Monophonic Plug-In The Monophonic plug-in is best suited to mono- Recommended Type of Material phonic material where you want to keep the for- Window Size mant relationships intact, such as with vocals. The General purpose 30–40 ms Monophonic plug-in is also well suited to mono- phonic instrumental lines, such as a bass track. The Percussive 20 ms or lower Monophonic plug-in provides no plug-in specific Pads and other legato 60 ms or higher parameters.
material Rhythmic Plug-In The Rhythmic plug-in is best suited to material with clear attack transients, such as drums. The Rhythmic plug-in supports clip-based pitch shift- ing.
Elastic Audio plug-in: Monophonic Elastic Audio pitch shifting is not supported with the Monophonic algorithm.
Elastic Audio plug-in: Rhythmic Chapter 45: Elastic Audio 1009 design, and audio loop applications. Use it to ma- The Monophonic plug-in analyzes pitch as nipulate audio loops for tempo matching or to well as peak transients in order to provide change vocal tracks for formant correct pitch shift- higher quality TCE to pitched material. Con- ing.
sequently, analysis for the Monophonic algo- rithm takes longer than for the Polyphonic, Rhythmic, and Varispeed algorithms. Also, when switching from another Elastic Audio plug-in to Monophonic, Pro Tools reana- lyzes any audio on the track to collect the ex- tra analysis data.
Varispeed Plug-In Elastic Audio plug-in: X-Form Use the Varispeed plug-in to link time and pitch Quality changes for tape-like speed change effects, and post production workflows. The Varispeed plug-in Select either Maximum or Low (Faster) from the provides no plug-in specific controls.
Quality pop-up menu. Maximum is the slowest pro- cessing algorithm, but provides the highest quality results. Low (Faster) produces relatively good re- sults and is much faster than the Maximum setting.
Formant For audio material with clear formants, enable For- mant to preserve the formant shape of audio when applying TCE processing.

formant shape, which is apparent if viewed using a spectrum analyzer. Warp Markers Elastic Audio Markers in Warp Warp markers anchor the audio to a specific point View in the Timeline. Warp markers are only displayed in Warp view. In Warp view, a Warp marker ap- Three types of markers are visible in Warp view:
pears as a thick black vertical line with a triangle at Event markers, Warp markers, and Tempo Event– its base.
generated Warp markers. Use Event markers and Warp markers as control points for applying Elas- To better understand how Warp markers work, tic Audio processing.
think of the audio clip as a rubber band, the Time- line as a ruler, and Warp markers as pins. Warp markers can be used to pin a specific point of the rubber band (the audio) to a specific point on the ruler (the Timeline). If you pin down one point of the rubber band to one point on the ruler, use a sec- ond pin to pin down another point of the rubber band to another point on the ruler, and then place a third pin equally between them on the rubber band, but fix it to a point on the ruler closer to the second pin, the rubber band will be stretched (or ex- Event marker panded) between the first and middle pins, and re- Warp marker Tempo Event laxed (or compressed) between the middle pin and Warp markers the last pin.

Automation does not follow manual warping of audio clips, including TCE trimming and quantization of Elastic Audio. However, au- tomation does follow tempo changes on tick- based tracks.
Telescoping Warp Telescoping Warp can be applied to any audio clip before or after a Warp marker as long as no other Warp marker precedes or follows it accordingly.
The audio can be on a sample- or tick-based track.
Telescoping Warp is especially useful for adjust- Before and after warping, applying Telescoping Warp ing audio files to match the session tempo map and with the clip start fixed Bar|Beat grid.

change the properties for selected clips. Event Sensitivity Property When you change the Event Sensitivity prop- The Event Sensitivity property in the Elastic Prop- erty, Pro Tools writes temporary filtered erties window lets you filter Event markers based Elastic Audio analysis files (.aan) to the ses- on the analysis confidence level. The confidence sion’s Rendered Files folder. Any unused level for any detected transient event is based, in temporary filtered analysis files are purged part, on the clarity of the transient. For example, if when you close the session.
the file is a drum loop, loud accented hits will be analyzed with a higher degree of confidence than a Reset Button soft, unaccented hit.

1022 Pro Tools Reference Guide Pitch Shift Property 4 Adjust the Pitch Shift settings up (“+”) or down (Polyphonic, Rhythmic, and X-Form Only) (“–”) by an amount in semitones and cents.
The Pitch Shift property in the Elastic Properties window lets you pitch shift whole clips on Elastic Audio-enabled tracks using the Polyphonic, Rhythmic, and X-Form plug-ins. You apply real- time or rendered pitch shifting in semitones and cents by +/– 2 octaves.
Elastic Audio Clip-Based Pitch Shifting Elastic Properties window (Polyphonic, Rhythmic, and X-Form Only) To transpose the pitch of an audio clip in the Transpose window:

rithm).

after pitch shifting with another plug-in, the Choose Event > Event Operations > Transpose.

Removing Clip Pitch Shifting If you have applied any pitch shifting to a clip, you can remove pitch shifting and revert the clip to its original pitch. This can be useful if you are not sat- isfied with the results and want to revert to the pre– pitch shifted clip.
1024 Pro Tools Reference Guide Moving Elastic Audio Between Tracks Pro Tools analyzes, conforms, and commits audio in different ways when moving audio clips between tracks with Elastic Audio enabled and tracks without Elastic Audio enabled. The following table shows the different results for moving clips between real-time Elastic Audio-enabled tracks, Rendered Elastic Audio- enabled tracks, and audio tracks without Elastic Audio.
Moving Elastic Audio between tracks From To Result Real-time Elastic Rendered Elastic Render clip with destination track’s Elastic Audio plug-in Audio–enabled track Audio–enabled track Real-time Elastic Audio track Render clip with source track’s Elastic Audio plug-in and Audio–enabled track (no Elastic Audio) commit the clip to the destination track Rendered Elastic Real-time Elastic Apply real-time Elastic Audio processing using the Audio–enabled track Audio–enabled track destination track’s Elastic Audio plug-in Rendered Elastic Audio track Commit to track Audio–enabled track (no Elastic Audio) Audio track Real-time Elastic Calculate Elastic Audio analysis and apply real-time (no Elastic Audio) Audio–enabled track Elastic Audio processing using the destination track’s Elastic Audio plug-in Audio track Rendered Elastic Calculate Elastic Audio analysis and render the clip (no Elastic Audio) Audio–enabled track using the destination track’s Elastic Audio plug-in Pitched Elastic Audio– Audio track Render clip pitch shift with source track’s Elastic Audio enabled track (no Elastic Audio) plug-in and commit the clip to the destination track Clips that are committed, either by disabling Elastic Audio on a track or by moving a clip to a track with- out Elastic Audio enabled, are written to disk as new audio files (see “Committed Clips” on page 1003.) Commit Up To This Insert Pro Tools lets you commit a track or Edit selection up to an individual plug-in insert on a track. For example, you might want to commit a virtual in- strument plug-in, but continue to adjust processing plug-ins, like EQ or dynamics.
To commit a track up to a single insert:
1 Ensure that Inserts view is shown in either the Edit or Mix window.
2 Right-click on the insert up to which you want to commit the track and choose Commit Up To Source MIDI Track (middle) assigned to a Virtual This Insert.
Instrument plug-in on the Instrument track (top) and the Committed track (bottom) When more than one MIDI track are routed to the same Instrument plug-in, you can commit one, some, or all of the MIDI tracks. Only selected MIDI tracks are committed. For example, if you have four MIDI tracks routed to a single Instru- ment plug-in on another track (audio, Auxiliary In- put, or Instrument), and you select only MIDI tracks 1–3, the committed track will include only Commit Up To This Insert the notes from those tracks while the notes from MIDI track 4 are excluded. Similarly, if you have a Press and hold the Option key (Mac) or the complex MIDI arrangement and you only want to Alt key (Windows) to Commit all tracks up to commit one part of it, select the MIDI track you the selected insert.

Frozen Auxiliary Input track with source audio track shown—Freeze icon highlighted 1036 Pro Tools Reference Guide On audio tracks, any plug-ins on the track are made Freezing Audio and Instrument Tracks inactive and the waveform updates to show the When freezing audio and Instrument tracks, the processed, or “frozen” audio.
rendered (“frozen”) audio starts at the beginning of each clip on the track and ends either at each clip end point or after the processed audio of the clip (such as with time-based plug-in processing like reverb or delay) falls below –144 dBFS for at least 60 seconds. Muted clips are not rendered.
Freezing Auxiliary Input Tracks When freezing Auxiliary Input tracks, audio is ren- dered (“frozen”) starting when audio is detected at the track input. Audio stops rendering when the au- Frozen audio track, before and after—Freeze icon dio signal drops below –144 dBFS for 60 seconds.

When freezing a track that has a plug-in that is assigned to Auxiliary • Clip gain Output Stems, you are prompted to also freeze any • Clip effects tracks that are assigned to the plug-in Auxiliary • HEAT (if set to pre-insert) Outputs. • Elastic audio The following track controls are maintained and are not rendered with track freeze:
• Volume • Volume Trim • Mute • Pan • Send Level • Send Level trim • Send Mute • Plug-in automation for plug-ins that are not fro- zen (when using Insert) Freeze to Chapter 47: Committing, Freezing, and Bouncing Tracks 1037 Freeze Up To This Insert Editing Restrictions on Frozen Tracks Pro Tools lets you freeze a track up to an individ- ual plug-in insert on the track. For example, you Frozen tracks disallow most editing functions. To might want to freeze a virtual instrument plug-in, edit a frozen track, unfreeze it, make your edits, but continue to adjust processing plug-ins, like EQ and then re-freeze the track.
or dynamics.
The following editing actions are not allowed on frozen tracks:
To freeze a track up to a single insert:
• Editing clips or clip contents is disallowed.
1 Ensure that Inserts view is shown in either the Edit or Mix window. • Elastic Audio is disallowed.
• Clip gain and clip effects are disallowed on fro- 2 Right-click on the insert up to which you want zen tracks.
to freeze the track and choose Freeze Up To This Insert.
• AudioSuite rendering is disallowed on frozen tracks.
• Tempo changes, Song Start changes, and en- abling or disabling the Conductor Track are not allowed when there are frozen tracks in the ses- sion.
• Insert and Cut Time operations are disallowed in a session if there are frozen tracks.
• HEAT is unavailable for frozen Auxiliary Input Freeze Up To This Insert (Freezing up to insert B) and Instrument tracks.
When any plug-in processing on a track is frozen, • Instrument Tracks are removed from the MIDI frozen inserts are italicized and grayed out. The Editor window while frozen and MIDI parame- also display the Track Freeze icon.
ters including Real Time Quantize settings can- not be adjusted until the track is unfrozen.
• Clips used on a source playlist for a frozen track cannot be removed from the session until the Track Freeze icon track is unfrozen.
The following editing actions are allowed on fro- zen tracks:
• Editing and writing of all basic track automation parameters including sends.
Track frozen through inserts A and B • Editing and writing of automation on unfrozen plug-ins as well as addition and removal of plug-ins on unfrozen inserts (when using the Freeze Up to This Insert functionality) • Changing the track output path.

Bounce Track Pro Tools lets you bounce individual tracks from your session or project. Track Bounce is useful for delivering audio files from tracks in the session without having to re-route sub-master stems or in- dividual tracks for bus recording. You can just se- lect the desired source tracks and Bounce them to disk.
Chapter 47: Committing, Freezing, and Bouncing Tracks 1039 You can Bounce the following track types: audio, Track Bounce Dialog Auxiliary Input, Master Fader, and Instrument The Track Bounce dialog provides the same op- tracks.
tions as the Bounce Mix dialog (see “Bounce Op- tions” on page 1233), but with the additional op- To bounce a track from your session or project:
tion to render (or not render) track volume and pan Do one of the following:
automation.
• Select the track you want to bounce and then choose Bounce.
Track > • Right-click the track you want to bounce and select Bounce.
• Press Command+Option+Shift+B (Mac) or Control+Alt+Shift+B (Windows).
In the Track Bounce dialog, configure the op- tions as desired.
Click Bounce.
If there is a timeline selection, the bounce includes only the selection. Otherwise, the bounce starts at the beginning of the session or project and ends with the last sample of audio on the track.
If the source track is assigned to multiple output paths of different channel widths, and Pan automa- tion is rendered, a new file is created for each chan- nel width. For example, if the output of the source track is assigned to a mono output path, a stereo output path, and a 5.1 output path, three separate files are created, each with the corresponding channel width. If the track output is assigned to multiple output paths of the same channel width, Track Bounce dialog only one file is created. If Pan automation is not Render Automation rendered, only one new file is created and it will have the channel width of the Format selected in The Render Automation options determine the Track Bounce dialog.
whether or not Volume or Pan automation is ren- dered for bounced tracks.
Select to render Volume automation from Volume bounced tracks.
Pan Select to render Pan automation from bounced tracks.

Operations.
You can also apply quantize, duration, delay, and transpose to MIDI nondestructively us- ing MIDI Real-Time Properties. See “MIDI Event Operations Window Real-Time Properties” on page 810.
The Event Operations window provides com- To open a specific Event Operations window page:
mands to transform groups of MIDI notes to affect pitch, timing, and phrasing. The Quantize and Re- Choose Operations, followed by Event > Event  store Performance Event Operations can also be one of the Event Operations (such as Quantize).
applied to audio clip start times (or sync points) and Elastic Audio events. The Transpose Event Operation can also be applied to whole clips on Elastic Audio-enabled tracks.
The Event Operations window has nine pages, one for each Event Operations command.

window can be applied to MIDI notes, audio clips, To undo a command in the Event Operations and Elastic Audio events. For MIDI, quantization window:
adjusts MIDI note locations and durations so that timing is improved, or so that a particular rhythmic Choose Edit > Undo.
 “feel” is achieved. For audio clips, quantization adjusts the start times (or sync points) of audio clips. For Elastic Audio, quantization adjusts to the location of detected transient events and applies Elastic Audio processing accordingly.
1042 Pro Tools Reference Guide The Quantize Event Operations window lets you To open the Quantize window:
apply Grid Quantize based on rhythmic values Choose Event > Event Operations > Quantize.
 (such as a 16th note) or using Groove Quantize based on Groove Templates. Slightly different op- Press Option+0 (Mac) or Alt+0 (Windows) tions are provided for Grid Quantize and Groove to open the Quantize window.
Quantize.
The and Quantize Event Operation Quantize to commands can both be used to quantize clips.
Grid However, each command also has a specific addi- tional use.
Use the Quantize to Grid command (Clip > Quan- tize to Grid) to adjust the start points (or sync points) for selected clips. The Quantize to Grid command can be applied to both MIDI and audio clips (see “Quantizing Clips to Grid” on page 693).
The Quantize Event Operation, on the other hand, can be applied to MIDI notes and Elastic Audio events individually in addition to audio clip start times (or sync points). Some notes or events may be moved back in time, others forward. For MIDI notes only, some may be shortened, some length- ened, and some more drastically affected than oth- ers.
Event Operations window, Quantize You can also quantize MIDI input, see “Input To select Grid Quantize:

tings for use in future sessions, save them as From the Quantize Grid pop-up menu, select a  part of a session template (see “Session Tem- groove template.
plates” on page 188).
Chapter 48: Event Operations 1043 Grid Quantize The Quantize window can apply Grid Quantize when a specific rhythmic value is selected in the Quantize Grid pop-up menu. When a groove tem- plate is selected in the Quantize pop-up menu, Groove Quantize can be applied. For information on Groove Quantize, see “Groove Quantize” on page 1048.
What to Quantize The What to Quantize settings determine what as- pects of any selected MIDI notes are quantized:
Note Ons (attacks), Note Offs (releases), or both.
You can also select whether to quantize audio clips or Elastic Audio events. Depending on what type of track material is selected, not all options will be available. If only MIDI is selected, the Audio Clips and Elastic Audio Events options are unavailable.

Chapter 48: Event Operations 1047 these controls determine which notes are affected Groove Templates and how drastically they are changed. Addition- Groove templates (such as DigiGroove templates) ally, use the Randomize option, which adds a per- are “quantization maps” derived from real musical centage of randomness to the quantization, to performances. The rhythmic character of each per- make tracks sound less “mechanical.” formance is analyzed and stored as a groove tem- plate. Groove templates can be used to transfer the feel of a particular performance to MIDI notes Groove Quantize (Timing, Duration, and Velocity), Elastic Audio events, and audio clips.
In the Quantize window, you can apply Groove Quantize when a groove template is selected in the DigiGroove templates can be created using Beat Quantize Grid pop-up menu. Groove quantize ad- Detective. Beat Detective analyzes an audio selec- justs MIDI note locations and durations, Elastic tion for transient peaks according to a defined Audio events, and audio clips according to a threshold and maps the rhythmic relationships to a groove template rather than a strict quantization 960 parts per quarter note (ppq) template. Beat De- grid. Groove templates extract the particular rhyth- tective can also analyze a MIDI selection for Note mic feel of a recorded audio or MIDI performance Ons and velocities to generate groove templates.
so that is can be applied to another MIDI sequence These template can then be used with Groove or audio selection. DigiGroove templates can be Quantize, as well as with Beat Detective.
generated using Beat Detective.
For information on Beat Detective and creating groove templates, see Chapter 34, “Beat Detective.” DigiGroove templates are also available from Avid and third-party manufacturers.
Numerical Sound’s Feel Injector Templates (960 ppq) are supported in Pro Tools, but DNA Groove Templates (192 ppq) are not.
Groove Quantize Settings Quantize Grid Pop-Up Menu Determines which groove template to use for quantization. All groove template files in the Grooves folder are available in the Quantize Grid pop-up menu.
Grooves files are stored in the Grooves folder in the Root Settings Folder. You can change the loca- tion of the Root Settings Folder in the Operation Quantize options with a groove template selected Preferences (see “User Media and Settings Loca- tion” on page 116).

For example, a two-bar groove template lections are based on different meters, the template repeats starting at every odd-numbered bar in the will be repeated or truncated to match the number session (bar 1, 3, 5, 7…). of beats in the selection.
For example, if the Groove Template contains fewer beats than the selection, such as a one-bar template in 6/8 being applied to a one-bar selection 2-bar 2-bar 2-bar 2-bar groove groove groove groove in 4/4, the template repeats to make up the differ- template template template template ence.
2-bar groove template, repeating template grid Template Mapping for Equal Meters And Odd Numbered Bars 4/4 1-bar selection If a two-bar groove template is applied to a selec- tion of one bar of the same meter, only the first bar of the template is used. If the selection does not en- compass bar boundaries, for example 1|2|000 to 6/8 bar 1 repeated 2|1|000, the groove template only modifies the se- (beats 3–6 not used) lected notes.
4/4 1-bar selection Applying a 1-bar groove template in 6/8 to a 1-bar selection in 4/4 4/4 bar 1 4/4 bar 2 (not used) If the Groove Template contains more beats than the selection on a track, such as a one-bar template Applying 2-bar groove template to a 1-bar selection, in 6/4 being applied to a two-bar selection in 4/4, odd numbered bar only uses the appropriate number of beats.
Template Mapping for Equal Meters And Even Bars 4/4 1-bar selection If a selection starts on an even bar, only the appro- priate portion of the groove template will be used.
For example, if the selection is two bars long and starts on an even bar, the template applies bar 2 be- fore bar 1.

Quantization can also be applied to clip start times 3-bar selection of changing meters (or sync points) on all audio tracks, whether they are Elastic Audio-enabled, or sample- or tick- based. This lets you quickly quantize audio clips without applying TCE, and is especially useful (not used) when working with “one-shot” clips (single-event clips) such as sliced clips or clip groups.
Use Restore Performance to undo quanti- zation of audio and MIDI. See “Restore Performance Command” on page 1053.

Chapter 48: Event Operations 1053 To restore the original time, duration, velocity, or pitch for selected MIDI notes, audio clips, or Elastic Audio Events:
Select the MIDI notes, audio clips, or Elastic Audio Events you want to restore.
Choose 2 Event > Event Operations > Restore Performance.
Select which performance attributes to restore.
Click Apply.
Any changes made through the Quantize, Change The Restore Performance command can be Duration (MIDI only), Change Velocity (MIDI undone.
only), or Transpose commands are undone and the original “performance” is restored. Also, any The following MIDI note, Elastic Audio Event, MIDI notes that were recorded with Input Quan- and audio clip attributes can be restored:

1054 Pro Tools Reference Guide Change Velocity Flatten Performance The Change Velocity command in the Event Oper- The Flatten Performance command in the Event ations window automatically adjusts Note On (at- Operations window “locks in” the current perfor- tack) and Note Off (release) velocities for selected mance for selected notes, creating a new “restore MIDI notes. Use it to make notes louder or softer, to” state for the specified note attributes when us- or to create crescendos or decrescendos.
ing Restore Performance.
Velocities can also be edited manually with the Pencil and Grabber tools (see “Manually Editing MIDI Notes” on page 782).
You can also change velocity nondestructively in real-time using MIDI Real-Time Properties.

Chapter 48: Event Operations 1059 To transpose MIDI notes:
1 If you will be transposing a group of tracks, en- able the Edit Group.
2 Select the MIDI notes you want to transpose.
3 Choose Event > Event Operations > Transpose.
Press Option+T (Mac) or Alt+T (Windows) to open the Transpose window.
4 Select the Transpose option (By, From and To, All Notes To, or In Key).
5 Set the corresponding pitch fields.

ber entry box to enter the pitch to which to trans- 3 Select Transpose in Key.

1060 Pro Tools Reference Guide To transpose an Elastic Audio clip:
1 Make sure the clip, or clips, you want to Trans- pose are on Elastic Audio-enabled tracks.
Elastic Audio pitch transposition is not sup- ported with the Monophonic algorithm.
2 With the Grabber or Selector tool, select the au- dio clip you want to transpose. Only clips that are completely selected will be affected.
3 Choose Event > Event Operations > Transpose.
4 Do one of the following:
• Adjust the Transpose By settings by an amount in semitones and cents.
• Adjust the Transpose From and To settings by an amount in Semitones and Cents.

Chapter 48: Event Operations 1063 To enter MIDI notes with Step Input (using an Step Input Command external MIDI device):
1 Make certain your external MIDI device is Step Input in the Event Operations window lets properly connected and working with you use a MIDI controller to enter notes individu- Pro Tools.
ally, one step at a time. This gives you precise con- trol over note placement, duration, and velocity.
2 Choose Event > Event Operations > Step Input.
With MIDI step input you can also create musical 3 Select the Enable option.
passages that might be difficult to play accurately, or at a fast tempo.
When Step Input is enabled, each previously record-enabled MIDI or Instrument track is taken out of record enable, and if the Default Thru Instrument preference is defined, it is disabled.
4 Choose the MIDI or Instrument track for step entry from the Destination track pop-up menu.
5 Choose the note value you want to enter from the Step Increment section.
6 Play a note or chord on your MIDI instrument.
This enters the note and moves to the next step of the insertion point.
If you do not have a MIDI keyboard or other external MIDI device, you can use the MIDI Keyboard window to enter MIDI notes using Step Input. See “MIDI Keyboard Window” on page 584, To increase a note’s length while using Step Input:
Step Input window 1 Play and hold a note on your MIDI instrument.
Use Step Input to enter MIDI notes in the When a note is being held on the MIDI Score Editor or in MIDI Editor windows.
keyboard, Next Step changes to read “Increment.” 2 Choose the note value that you would like to add and press the Increment button.
Release the note on your MIDI keyboard to move to the next step insertion point.

1066 Pro Tools Reference Guide Chapter 49: HEAT HEAT (Harmonically Enhanced Algorithm To enable (or disable) HEAT in a session, do one of the following:
Technology) is a software option that adds “analog color” to Pro Tools Ultimate or Studio software.
Select (or deselect) Options > Activate HEAT.
 HEAT is available in both DSP and Native for- Select (or deselect) the HEAT On/Off button in mats. To use HEAT, iLok authorizations are re-  the Mix window in HEAT view.
quired for both HEAT and Pro Tools Ultimate or Studio.
You can also enable the Enable HEAT in New Ses- option in the Pro Tools Preferences to have sions HEAT is applied to all active audio tracks in your HEAT automatically enabled for all new sessions.
session, emulating magnetic recording tape com- bined with harmonic coloration commonly experi- To enable (or disable) HEAT for new sessions:
enced with analog mixing consoles. Use HEAT to Choose Setup > Preferences.
soft-saturated distortion.
Click the Processing tab.
In the DSP Management section, select (or de- select) the Enable HEAT in New Sessions op- Enabling HEAT tion.
When enabled, HEAT is applied to all active audio Click OK.
tracks in your session, emulating the effect of an analog mixing console. HEAT is supported with all mono, stereo, and surround formats.
About HEAT Processing With HDX systems, HEAT uses DSP resources on HEAT provides global controls for Drive and all active audio tracks. This is important to know Tone. These affect all audio tracks where HEAT is because as you increase (or decrease) the number not bypassed.
of audio tracks in the session, you also increase (or decrease) the amount of DSP resources required to keep HEAT hot. Note that HEAT does not use Drive DSP resources on inactive audio tracks.
The control introduces non-linear processing Drive that emulates magnetic recording tape combined With Pro Tools Ultimate Software on Native sys- with harmonic information commonly found in an- tems or Pro Tools Studio software, HEAT uses the alog gear.
host-processor.

Think of it as a tube or tape doing soft clip- with the 3rd and 5th harmonics). HEAT also emu- ping, “squashing” the peaks and causing analog lates the recording and reproduction equalizers of compression over a wide dynamic range.
tape decks that further modifies the harmonic con- tent. As a result, when turning the Drive control How Much Processing Should I Use?
counter-clockwise, HEAT fattens up the bottom and mid-range of the processed audio and smooths HEAT processing is level and program dependent.
high-frequency transients as you increase the Although a general setting usually works for many processing. sources, some settings that work best for one source may not have the desired effect for another Turning the control clockwise has a more ag- Drive source. If you need a general purpose setting try gressive effect, adding an even harmonic series adding two steps of the Drive control (in either di- commonly found in triode tube circuits to the odd rection).

to discover what sounds best to you.

ing on the Drive control setting. The amount and character of the harmonic content is changed by this control, in a non-linear fashion, unlike what a tone control in an equalizer would do.

Master Controls To access HEAT Master Controls on C|24:

Mixing involves making decisions about elements such as volume levels, panning, and effects in your Calibrating Audio Interfaces studio. Inserts, post-fader Master Faders and Paths (plug-ins or hardware) Master Faders can be assigned to main or sub- paths as defined in the I/O Setup. Multiple Master Faders cannot be assigned to the same path.

tem’s audio processing power. Master Faders pro- vide up to five post-fader inserts and no sends.

• Control submaster (bussed tracks) levels Set the output of the Master Fader to the main • Apply dither or other inserts to an entire mix output path.

1 Create an Auxiliary Input track.

port 3–8 channel multi-mono and multichannel • Plug-Ins and hardware inserts route the signal plug-ins.

Instrument tracks include two sets of inserts • Some Instrument plug-ins accept audio from the Views: Inserts A–E (inserts 1–5) and Inserts F–J track input (letting you use them as processing (inserts 6–10). The View menu provides separate plug-ins).

 • Inserts can be bypassed or made inactive.

View > Mix Window > Inserts Track Output Format and To display inserts in the Edit window:
Plug-Ins Select any of the following:
 Pro Tools supports mono, multi-mono, stereo, and • View > Edit Window > Inserts A–E.
multichannel plug-ins (Pro Tools Ultimate and • View > Edit Window > Inserts F–J.
Studio only).
Because inserts process in series, changing the plug-in format can alter the channel format. For example, inserting a mono-to-stereo plug-in on a mono Auxiliary Input changes the signal path from that plug-in through the rest of the track. This re- stricts all hardware inserts or plug-ins after the ste- reo plug-in to be stereo-in/stereo-out plug-ins (or the supported multichannel format).
1086 Pro Tools Reference Guide To set the stereo pan depth:
HEAT 1 Choose Setup > Session to open the Session HEAT (Harmonically Enhanced Algorithm Tech- Setup window.
nology) is a software option that adds “analog 2 Select an option from the Pan Depth selector.
color” to Pro Tools mixes. HEAT is applied to all active audio tracks in your session, emulating the effect of an analog mixing console. Use HEAT to open up your mixes with warm, analog-modeled soft-saturated distortion. Using HEAT in conjunc- tion with the Stereo Pan Depth option (in the Ses- sion Setup window) lets you emulate the effect of classic analog consoles right in Pro Tools.
Session Setup window, selecting Pan Depth For more information, see Chapter 49, “HEAT.” Stereo Pan Depth Options The following options are available:
Pan Depth Stereo Pan Depth –2.5 dB The standard stereo pan depth setting in Pro Tools lets you select the pan depth for stereo Pro Tools 8.0.x and lower. When opening output paths. You can set the Pan Depth in the Ses- Pro Tools 8.0.x or earlier session in Pro Tools 8.1 sion Setup window. The Pan Depth setting lets you and later, this is the default option.
set the amount of signal attenuation when a track is The industry standard pan depth for sur- –3.0 dB routed to a stereo output and panned to center.
round mixers and many consoles. This is the de- fault setting for new Pro Tools 8.1 and later ses- The Surround Mixer requires slightly more sions.
DSP resources than the Stereo Mixer. Conse- quently, if you plan to regularly mix in stereo –4.5 dB The standard pan depth for many and use a Pan Depth of –2.5, you may want to British analog consoles.
use the Stereo Mixer plug-in.
The standard pan depth for full mono –6.0 dB compatibility.
For greater than stereo formats (such as 5.1), all surround output panning is fixed at – 3.0 dB. The variable pan depth options are only available for stereo outputs.
Chapter 50: Basic Mixing 1087 Views in the Mix and Edit Windows The Mix and Edit windows can be configured to show or hide various mixing controls, using menu commands or view selectors.
Most of these views are available in both windows, unless noted otherwise.
Window View Selectors Both the Edit window and Mix window provide Window View selectors to configure their views.
Mix Window View selector Displaying Views To display all views in the Mix or Edit window, do one of the following:
Select (or Window) View > Mix Window Edit >  All.
Click the Mix Window View selector (or Edit  Window View selector), and select All.
To display none of the views in the Mix or Edit window, do one of the following:
Edit Window View selector Select View > Mix Window (or Edit Window) >  None.
Click the Mix Window View selector (or Edit  Window View selector), and select None.

(such as Comments).

page 1100.

Inserts (A–E) View Inserts (F–J) View Sends (A–E) View EQ Insert indicator EQ curve Sends (F–J) View I/O View EQ Curve view in the Mix window For supported unlinked multi-mono EQ plug-ins (such as EQ III), the EQ Curve display shows a Views in the Mix window color-coded EQ curve for each channel.
EQ Curve View (Mix Window Only) The EQ Curve view in the Mix window indicates whether or not an EQ plug-in is inserted on the track, and if so, displays the curve of that EQ. The EQ Insert indi- cator is lit when there is an EQ plug-in inserted on the track and it is grayed out if there is no EQ plug- in inserted on the track. The EQ Curve updates to show any changes to the EQ inserted on the track.
If there is more than one EQ inserted on a track, the EQ Insert indicator EQ Curve view displays the sum of the EQs.
Not all EQ plug-ins support EQ Curve view.

Sends Pro Tools lets you insert up to ten sends (A–E and F–J) on each audio track, Auxiliary Input, Routing Folder, or Instrument track.
Sends provide the following features:
• Sends can be set as pre- or post-fader.
• Sends can show level meters.
• Send level, pan, mute, and LFE controls can be configured to follow Groups.
Assigning a send to a stereo bus path • Send level, send mute, and send pan (for stereo The send can be a mono or stereo (or any of the and multichannel sends) are fully automatable. supported multichannel formats for surround mix- See “Automating Sends” on page 1186. ing) output or bus path. Paths that are already as- signed are highlighted.
• Send controls can be displayed and edited from the Mix or Edit windows, or in their own output Multichannel sends (Pro Tools Ultimate and Stu- windows.
dio only) can be assigned to any path of any chan- • Sends can be assigned to available output and nel width. The sends can then be moni- tored and processed through an Auxiliary Input (or Instrument track), recorded to audio tracks, and bounced to disk. (Bouncing and mixdown are ex- plained in Chapter 54, “Mixdown.”) Chapter 50: Basic Mixing 1095 To change the default setting for sends:
1 Choose Setup > Preferences and click Mixing.
2 Do one of the following:
• Select Sends Default to “–INF.” • Deselect Sends Default to “–INF” and new sends will default to unity gain (0 dB).
3 Do one of the following:
• Select Send Pans Default to Follow Main Pan.
• Deselect Send Pans Default to Follow Main Pan to have panning control over the sends independent of the panning on the track’s main output path.
To remove a send from a track:
Click the Sends button on the track and choose  No Send from the pop-up menu.

dows) the Send Level fader.

Chapter 50: Basic Mixing 1105 To open a Send window:
Output Windows for Tracks Click the Send Assignment button in the Mix or and Sends  Edit window.
Track Output and Send windows provide standard Pro Tools track, send, Target, and other controls.
Track outputs and sends can be opened for display and editing in dedicated windows.
• Track Output windows provide track fader, pan, automation, solo, and mute controls.
• Send windows provide send level, pan, automation, and mute controls.
• Standard Output and Send window controls pro- vide additional routing, assignment, and display settings.
To open a track Output window:
Click the Output Window button in the channel  strip.
Opening a Send window To view a different track Output or Send window:
Click an Output Window button, or Send  Assignment button, in the Mix or Edit window.
If a similar Output window was already open with a highlighted Target icon (red, lit), the newly se- lected send will open in its place.
Opening a track Output window 1106 Pro Tools Reference Guide Opening Multiple Output Track or Send Fader, Pan Windows Controls, and Solo, Mute, and Automation Controls Multiple Track Output and Send windows can be Output windows provide the associated track’s displayed simultaneously using either of the fol- Volume fader, Pan controls, Solo and Mute lowing methods.
switches, and Automation Mode selector. Use To open additional Track Output or Send windows: these to adjust or automate the controls of the Out- put window.
Shift-click any Track Output Window button or  Send Assignment button.
Panner Linking To set a window to remain open when opening Stereo outputs provide two panning controls, one additional Track Output or Send windows:
for each side of the left/right pair. Multichannel Make sure the Target is disabled.
 outputs provide a multichannel panner.
Target enabled Target disabled The Output window provides controls to link chan- nels for precise panning of stereo and multichannel tracks.
In the linked state, all sides match changes to any other side’s Pan control. This is Absolute Link mode. To mirror panning changes, see “Inverse Linked Panning” on page 1108.
When unlinked, Pan controls are completely inde- pendent of each other.

Chapter 50: Basic Mixing 1109 The channel order is determined by the configura- Signal Routing for Monitoring tion of the output paths in the I/O setup.
and Submixing The bussing and mixing features in Pro Tools sup- port many possibilities for submixing and monitor- ing, including the ability to:
• Monitor and mix playback of audio tracks with audio inputs or with MIDI instruments • Bus submixes for effects processing or monitor- ing (including headphone or “cue” mixes) • Mix submixes with Master Faders Monitoring and Mixing Audio with Auxiliary Input Tracks Output Meters channel order determined by the Use Auxiliary Input tracks to monitor and mix au- Output configuration in the I/O Setup dio from external sources, such as MIDI instru- For all Pro Tools systems using stereo output ments, tape, or microphone inputs, in a Pro Tools paths, stereo meters are shown (L/R).
mix.

Instrument track configured to control and monitor an external MIDI instrument (K2600 shown) 1112 Pro Tools Reference Guide Audio is routed to a submix by assigning any com- bination of sends and track output to available bus or output paths.
When routing mono signals with internal mix busses, be sure to use mono busses rather Instrument plug-in than splitting stereo busses. Since Pro Tools keeps stereo busses phase aligned, splitting stereo busses can result in inaccurate delay Inputs from plug-in compensation.
outputs Submixing with Track Outputs or Sends You can use both track outputs and sends to route audio for submixing, depending on whether you want a discrete or send and return submix.
Discrete Submix Output Pro Tools lets you discretely route source audio through a submix. This is preferred for dither or other mastering processing, where you do not want unprocessed audio heard in addition to the pro- cessed signals. Use track outputs (not sends) to as- sign tracks to a bus path for discrete submixing. In this arrangement, the balance of processed and un- processed signal is controlled by plug-in wet/dry settings.

cessors.

1 Assign each track’s main output to your main Send to effect bus mix outputs.

To create a monitor submix:
1 Assign each track’s main output to your main mix outputs.
2 Assign a send (mono or stereo) and set the send Controls level of effect return destinations on the source tracks to the output Controls level of dry signal path connected to the external device. Config- ure the sends for pre- or post-fader, as needed.
3 Choose Track > New.
4 Specify the track type (Aux Input) and format (stereo or mono), then click Create.
5 From the Auxiliary Input track’s Output Path Send/return setup for an external device selector, select the output path (not the main outputs) for monitoring the mix.
1116 Pro Tools Reference Guide Adjust the Auxiliary Input track fader to control Creating a Master Send Level the volume for the monitor mix. Control A Master Fader can control the overall level of bus and output paths.
To create a Master send level control:
Choose New.
1 Track > Specify the track type (Master Fader), and mono, stereo, or any of the supported multi- channel formats for surround mixing.
Click Create.
Do one of the following:
• Set the output of the Master Fader to the same path that you are using to send to an Auxiliary Input track.
• Set the output of the Master Fader to match the path that you have chosen for your effects send.
You can then adjust send levels to balance the source tracks, and use the Master Fader as a master level control for the entire submix.
Soloing Tracks in a Submix Soloing any tracks implicitly mutes all other tracks, including Auxiliary Inputs. Solo-safing an Auxiliary Input prevents this implicit muting and Drum submix for monitoring lets the Auxiliary Input continue to pass audio when its source tracks are soloed.
To solo safe an Auxiliary Input:
Command-click (Mac) or Control-click (Win-  dows) the Solo button on the Auxiliary Input track.
To solo an individual track that is part of a group:
Control-click (Mac) or Start-click (Windows)  the Solo button.

This field lets you adjust track delays manually while Delay Compensation is enabled. The User Offset is added to or subtracted from the amount of delay applied by Track Compensation.

Dly indicator right-click menu • For manually time-aligning a track if a plug-in is When enabled, the timing of the meter is delayed incorrectly reporting its delay.

Dither minimizes quantization artifacts by intro- Ensure that is 1 Automatic Delay Compensation ducing very low-level random noise to a signal, enabled (Options Compensation).

To enable a MIDI control surface in Pro Tools: If you want to prevent control surface MIDI data from reaching Pro Tools, you can deselect its 1 Choose Setup > Peripherals and click the MIDI source port in the MIDI Input Enable window.
Controllers tab.
2 Choose your MIDI control surface device name from the Type pop-up menu.
3 Choose the MIDI control surface’s source port from the Receive From pop-up menu.
4 Choose a destination port from the Send To pop-up menu.
5 If your MIDI control surface has more than eight channel strips, or if you are using multiple devices of the same type in series, choose the appropriate number of channel strips from the pop-up menu under #Ch’s.
6 Click OK.
You can configure additional MIDI control sur- faces (up to a total of four) by repeating the above steps. Make sure that none of the control surfaces are assigned to the same MIDI source and destina- tion ports or channels in the Pro Tools Peripherals dialog.

Drag the insert to a new insert location. Plug-In Windows  A Plug-In window appears whenever you click a plug-in’s Insert button on a track. This floating window lets you adjust the controls of any real- time plug-in insert in use on a track.

Insert button and selector on a track Option-drag (Mac) or Alt-drag (Windows) the  insert to a new insert location. Duplicated plug- Track Preset Plug-In Mapping in inserts retain their original settings and auto- controls controls controls mation.

 Side-Chain Filters Some plug-ins feature key high-pass and low-pass Plug-In Automation and Safe filters. These controls let you define a specific fre- Modes quency range in the side-chain signal with which to trigger the plug-in effect. A common production All real-time plug-ins can be fully automated, and technique is to use these controls to filter a drum support all Pro Tools Automation modes (Write, track so that only specific high frequency sounds Touch, and Latch, plus Trim).
(such as a hi-hat) or low frequency ones (such a The Auto button opens the Plug-In Automation di- tom or a kick) trigger the effect.
alog, where you can enable individual plug-in con- trols for automation recording (see “Automating Using a Key Input for External Plug-Ins” on page 1187).
Side-Chain Processing The Safe button engages Automation Safe mode.
To use a key input for external side-chain When enabled, existing plug-in automation is pro- processing:
tected from being overwritten (see “Record Safing Click the plug-in’s Key Input selector and select Plug-In Automation” on page 1189).
the input or bus carrying the audio you want to use to trigger the plug-in; or select Search to find the desired key input by typing the path Side-Chain Input name.
The side-chain input is the split-off signal used by a plug-in's detector to trigger dynamics processing, and is generally drawn internally from the input signal. However, some plug-ins let you switch be- tween internal and external side-chain processing.
With external side-chain processing, a plug-in's detector is triggered by an external signal (such as a separate reference track or audio source) known as the key input.
Searching and selecting a Key Input Chapter 51: Plug-In and Hardware Inserts 1143 2 Click the External Key to activate external side- chain processing.
3 Press the Spacebar to begin playback. The plug- in uses the input or bus that you chose as a key input to trigger its effect.
4 During playback, do any of the following to fine-tune key input triggering:
• To hear the audio source you have selected to control the key input, enable Side-Chain Lis- ten or Key Listen (depending on the plug-in).
When finished, disable Side-Chain Lis- ten/Key Listen to resume normal plug-in monitoring.
• To filter the key input so that only specific frequencies trigger the plug-in, use the filter- ing controls (if available) to select the fre- quency range.
Assigning a plug-in bus output to the input of an 5 Adjust the plug-in’s Threshold control (if avail- Auxiliary Input track able) and other controls as needed.
A plug-in with an output bus assigned to the Delay Compensation for Side input of another track cannot be moved to Chains that track.
Pro Tools lets you apply automatic Delay Com- pensation to side-chains. A plug-in with any assigned output busses cannot be removed or replaced until all plug- To apply Delay Compensation to side-chains:
in output busses are unassigned.
Choose Setup > Preferences.
Click the Mixing tab.
2 Plug-In Presets Enable the Compensate Side Chains option.
Pro Tools makes it easy to create your own library Click OK. of plug-in settings, or presets. Using the Plug-In Settings menu, you can copy, paste, save, and im- port these settings from plug-in to plug-in, or from session to session.
Plug-in Bus Outputs Some plug-ins (such as some virtual instrument or multi-band processing plug-ins) provide separate bus outputs you can use to route audio from the plug-in to the input of another track for additional processing, monitoring, or recording.
