Dragging and dropping a plug-in settings file to an Insert Insert Right-Click menu in the Mix window Chapter 51: Plug-In and Hardware Inserts 1149 Plug-In Map Files Plug-In Mapping Plug-In maps can be exported (as .pim files) for use (Control Surfaces Only) in other sessions and on other systems with a sim- Pro Tools lets you customize the arrangement of ilar type of control surface (8-fader surface, plug-in parameter controls on supported control 24-fader surface, or ICON surface).
surfaces. You can save these customized plug-in Control Surface Mapping maps as presets and export them for use across dif- ferent sessions and systems. Plug-In Maps are When you create a custom plug-in map, its param- stored in the Plug-In Maps folder in the Pro Tools eters are arranged in pages that correspond to the Root Settings folder. You can change the location controls on the control surface. The size of a page of the Root Settings Folder in the Operation Pref- depends on the type of control surface. Each cus- erences (see “User Media and Settings Location” tom plug-in map allows up to 200 pages of mapped on page 116).
parameters.
Plug-In Learn Mode Plug-In mapping is stored according to the follow- ing types of control surfaces:
Plug-Ins can be put into Learn mode, from which any automatable plug-in parameter can be mapped to a channel strip encoder, switch, or (in Flip • C|24 mode) fader on the control surface.
• Command|8 Plug-In Map Presets • D-Control A map for a plug-in can be saved and recalled for • D-Command any plug-in of the same type.
• EUCON-compatible controllers • M-Audio controllers You can save multiple map presets for a single plug-in, and switch between maps at any time If you import a plug-in map that was created during a session.
on a different type of control surface, it will appear as an inactive choice in the Map Pre- You can also use multiple map presets on different sets menu.
instances of a plug-in at the same time. This is use- ful for virtual instrument plug-ins with multiple configurations, or for multi-user workflows on large systems.
You can set a custom plug-in map to be the default map for a plug-in, so that the same map is used for all default instances of that plug-in.

1156 Pro Tools Reference Guide Assigning Hardware Inserts Using Hardware Inserts To assign an external hardware insert to a track:
You can connect external devices, such as reverb Select an insert from the track Insert selector.
or effects processors, to your Pro Tools system and  use them as hardware inserts on individual tracks or on submixes.
Pro Tools inserts utilize corresponding hardware input and output channels to send and return the audio. Inserts can be defined, and their channels mapped to audio interface channels on the Insert page in the I/O Setup dialog.
If you plan to use a device as an insert, be sure to connect the device to matching inputs and outputs on your system. For example, a hardware insert be- ing supplied by outputs 7–8 needs to be returned to hardware inputs 7–8.
Delay Compensation can be applied to Hard- ware Inserts. See “H/W Insert Delay (Com- pensation) Page” on page 59.
To define a hardware insert: Selecting a hardware I/O insert Choose Setup > I/O, then click the Insert tab.
Select an insert path, or click New Path to create a new Insert path.
Hardware Insert window Double-click the Path Name to enter a custom path name for the insert.
Bypassing Hardware Inserts Make sure the insert path is set to the correct Hardware inserts do not provide a bypass control.
format (mono, stereo, or other).
To monitor playback without a hardware insert, ei- Map inserts in the Channel Grid as needed. In- ther set it to Inactive, remove it by reassigning it to sert and Output paths have special rules regard- No Insert, or use a bypass switch on the hardware ing channel mapping (see “Valid Paths and device itself. (See “Making Inserts Inactive” on Requirements” on page 79). page 1132 for more information.) Chapter 51: Plug-In and Hardware Inserts 1159 Chapter 52: DSP Mode DSP Mode for Ultra-Low DSP Mode States Latency Monitoring The following track-based DSP Mode states are (HDX Hybrid Engine or Pro Tools | Carbon Only) possible, as indicated by the DSP Mode icon:
HDX systems using the HDX Hybrid Engine and DSP Mode Enabled When DSP Mode is enabled Pro Tools | Carbon systems let you use DSP Mode on a track, the DSP Mode button turns bright to ensure ultra-low latency input and record moni- green. All Native plug-ins with DSP versions toring with dedicated HDX DSP.
switch to DSP while Native only plug-ins are by- passed. The entire signal path for the track runs on DSP Mode can be can enabled or disabled on a the DSP mixer in Pro Tools | Carbon or the DSP on track-by-track basis for Audio, Auxiliary Input, In- HDX cards.
strument, Routing Folder, and Master Fader tracks.
Native Mode (DSP Mode Disabled) When DSP To ensure the lowest possible latency for input and Mode is disabled on a track it is in Native Mode record monitoring, DSP and Native plug-ins can- and the DSP Mode button is gray. All plug-ins on not be active on the same track while DSP Mode is the track are Native only.
enabled. For tracks with DSP Mode enabled, all When DSP Mode Safe is enabled Native plug-ins on the track (if any) switch to DSP DSP Mode Safe on a track, DSP Mode will not be automatically en- (if a DSP version is available). Any Native-only abled on that track.
plug-ins on the track are automatically bypassed while in DSP Mode.
DSP Mode Auto Enabled If any track is part of the signal chain of another track that is set to DSP If you want to offload Native processing from Mode, it is automatically set to DSP Mode. When the host CPU to dedicated DSP processing DSP Mode is automatically enabled on a track, the with HDX cards or Pro Tools | Carbon, you DSP Mode button turns dim green.

Chapter 52: DSP Mode 1161 DSP Mode enabled Native Mode DSP Mode Safe enabled DSP Mode Auto enabled Figure 1. DSP Mode button states Enabling DSP Mode Enable DSP Mode on tracks for low-latency mon- itoring during recording or input monitoring. With DSP Mode enabled:
• Protecting low latency is the highest priority.
• The track and its entire signal chain run on the DSP on HDX cards or Pro Tools | Carbon HDX DSP.
• Any Native plug-ins on the track that are not available in DSP format are bypassed.
• HEAT is not supported on audio tracks in DSP Mode. In the Mix window, HEAT is bypassed for all audio tracks in DSP Mode.
To enable DSP Mode on a track, do one of the following:
In the Mix or Edit window, click the DSP Mode  Track DSP Mode button enabled: in the Edit window button on the track so that it lights bright green.
(top) and in the Mix window (bottom) In the Mix or Edit window, right-click the DSP  Mode button on the track and select DSP Mode.
1162 Pro Tools Reference Guide Right-click the Track name in the Track List, or  Enabling DSP Mode Safe in the Edit or Mix window, and select DSP Mode. The DSP Mode button is not shown Use DSP Mode Safe to prevent DSP Mode from when the Track Height is set to Small, Mini, or being automatically enabled on a track. This is use- Micro in the Edit window. In this case, use this ful if you want to route any audio from a DSP method to enable (or disable) DSP Mode, or do Mode–enabled track with any plug-ins that are so in the Mix window.
only available in Native format. For example, you might want to send audio from a DSP Mode–en- abled audio track to a DSP Mode Safe–enabled Auxiliary Input track for processing with a reverb plug-in that is only available as a Native plug-in.
DSP Safe Mode can also be useful if you just need to avoid using DSP for plug-ins on a track for any reason.
To enable DSP Mode Safe on a track, do one of the following:
Right-click the Track name in the Track List, or  in the Edit or Mix window, and select DSP Mode Safe.
Right-click a track name to enable DSP Mode in the Edit window Select the Enable DSP Mode when Tracks with Hardware Inputs are Record-Armed/In- Enabling DSP Mode Safe on a track in the Mix window put-Enabled option in the Operation Prefer- ences (Setup > Preferences) to automatically In the Mix or Edit window, right-click the DSP  enable DSP Mode when record arming or en- Mode button on the track and select DSP Mode abling input monitoring tracks. See “Opera- Safe.
tion Preferences” on page 114.
In the Mix or Edit window, Command-click the  DSP Mode button on the track.

abled, or if a plug-in has been made inactive due to insufficient DSP resources. Similarly, with Dy- namics plug-ins, the Key Input selector turns dark orange to indicate that there are insufficient DSP resources.

DSP Mode automatically enabled DSP Mode, DSP plug-in inactive due to insufficient DSP resources DSP Mode-enabled, track output inactive due to insufficient resources DSP Mode, Key Input inactive due to insufficient DSP resources Chapter 52: DSP Mode 1165 Workflow Example: Recording in DSP Mode for Ultra-Low Latency Monitoring The following workflow example uses DSP Mode for recording a singer with DSP plug-in processing and low-latency monitoring.
This example session is made up of the following tracks (see the graphic on the next page):
• 3 mono audio tracks for vocals, guitar, and bass. Each track has EQ and dynamics plug-ins, and the vocal track also has a slap-back delay.
• 2 stereo audio tracks, one for electric piano and another for drum loops. Each track has EQ and dynamics plug-ins.
• 1 stereo Auxiliary Input track for reverb processing.
• 2 Master Fader tracks, one for control room monitoring and the other for the singer’s headphones. Each has a limiter on it.
• 1 mono Auxiliary Input track for the built-in talkback mic.
The following diagram shows the physical studio connections for this workflow example. While this work- flow example only reviews recording vocals in DSP Mode with a dedicated headphone mix, you can just as easily have independent headphone mixes for the rest of the band.
Figure 2. Physical studio connections (HDX Hybrid Engine with MTRX Studio shown) 1166 Pro Tools Reference Guide Adjust the Mix for Recording Start playback to set the levels and panning of the instruments to get the mix you want in the control room monitors. Set the send levels from each track to balance the headphone mix. Input-enable the vocal track and use the talkback mic to communicate with the singer and adjust the headphone mix to the singer’s lik- ing. Once the singer is happy with the headphone mix, you are ready to record some vocals.
Figure 3. Session with the band already recorded Chapter 52: DSP Mode 1167 Record with Ultra-Low Latency Monitoring Enable DSP Mode on the vocal track. Notice that all tracks that are part of the signal chain for the vocal track have DSP Mode enabled automatically: the reverb Auxiliary Input track and both Master Fader tracks. All plug-ins on these tracks switch from Native to DSP format (any Native only plug-ins on any of these tracks are automatically bypassed). Record-arm the vocal track, and when the singer is ready, start re- cording. The singer will hear themselves in the mix with virtually no latency.
Figure 4. Recording vocals in DSP Mode for ultra-low latency monitoring 1168 Pro Tools Reference Guide Use DSP Mode Safe to Record with Native Reverb Let’s assume that you have a Native-only reverb plug-in that you really like. You can still record vocals with virtually no latency in their monitor mix while still using your favorite Native-only reverb plug-in to process all of the audio tracks. Enable DSP Mode Safe on the reverb track and set the wet/dry mix on the reverb plug-in to 100% wet. Enable DSP Mode on the vocal track—DSP Mode is enabled for all tracks in the signal chain of the vocal track except for the reverb track. Record-arm the vocal track and when the singer is ready, start recording. There will be latency on the reverb track, but given the nature of reverb pro- cessing it won’t really be noticeable. The important thing is that the talent won’t hear any latency in the mix with their performance.
Figure 5. Session configured for recording vocals, but with Auxiliary Input track in DSP Mode Safe for Native reverb processing Chapter 52: DSP Mode 1169 Tips for Using DSP Mode To ensure optimal workflows in DSP Mode:
Use input and output gain controls on Native-only plug-in inserts to make up for any undesirable  changes in signal level when switching between Native Mode and DSP Mode.
Since DSP Mode does not reduce latency for tracks that are not part of a live signal path, avoid enabling  DSP Mode on tracks that are not part of the signal chain for recording and input monitoring. However, note that DSP Mode can be used to offload plug-in processing from the CPU when mixing without con- sequence.
To avoid unnecessarily enabling DSP Mode automatically on tracks that are fed by other channels of a  multi-channel bus, use dedicated mono bus paths instead of mono sub-paths of stereo busses when rout- ing mono audio signals with tracks in DSP Mode. You will need to specifically create mono bus paths in I/O Setup since the default bus paths will always be stereo with mono sub-paths.
Avoid routing unrelated signals to the channels of a multichannel bus. For example, avoid routing a gui-  tar signal to the left channel and a bass signal to the right channel of the same stereo bus.
1170 Pro Tools Reference Guide Chapter 53: Automation Pro Tools features dynamic automation of mixing Automation Overview controls on each of the track types. You can write automation moves and view them in real time Pro Tools provides many options for recording, re- during playback and recording in your session.
placing, and editing automation data.
You can also edit automation data with the same techniques you use to edit audio and MIDI data. The basic steps for automation recording are:
• Enable the automation type that you want to re- Automation Accuracy with cord (volume, pan, mute, send level, send pan, Control Surfaces send mute, or plug-in automation).
• Put the appropriate tracks in an automation writ- Control Surfaces D-Control, D-Command, C|24, ing mode (Write, Touch, or Latch, or a Trim and Command|8 control surfaces support the auto- mode).
mation features in Pro Tools. Pro Tools control surfaces provide 10-bit resolution, or 1,024 steps • If you are automating a plug-in, enable the of fader resolution. Pro Tools interpolates this in- individual plug-in controls to be automated.
put to 24-bit resolution on playback, resulting in • Automation Safe any plug-ins, outputs, or sends extremely accurate and smooth fader automation.
that have existing automation data that you want to protect from being overwritten.
For details on using control surfaces to create mix automation, see your control surface guide. • Begin playback to begin automation recording, and adjust controls as needed. Pro Tools remem- EUCON Avid control surfaces using EUCON, bers all moves performed on enabled controls.
such as Pro Tools | S6 or Artist Series controllers, provide 12-bit resolution fader and knob control. To edit automation once it has been recorded, you can:
MIDI Control Surfaces Most MIDI control sur- • Repeat the above steps to write new automation faces have 7-bit resolution, or 128 steps. Pro Tools over the previous data.
interpolates this input to 24-bit resolution on play- back, resulting in accurate and smooth fader auto- • Graphically edit the automation data in the Edit mation.
window.
• Cut, copy, paste, or delete automation data (cer- tain restrictions apply).

 Smoothing When you perform automation moves (including Trim moves) with a continuous control, Pro Tools records the move as a series of very small steps, re- sulting in a staircase pattern with many break- points. Smoothing intelligently resolves this stair- case pattern into a single, smooth ramp from one breakpoint to the next. (Smoothing does not apply to switched controls such as mutes or plug-in by- Mixing Preferences page, Automation passes.) With smoothing enabled, the resulting au- tomation data is often a more accurate representa- For detailed information on Pro Tools Auto- tion of actual automation moves.
mation preferences, see “Mixing Preferences” on page 122.
Chapter 53: Automation 1179 Thinning To enable or disable Automation Follows Edit, do one of the following:
Thinning automatically reduces the overall num- Select or deselect  Options > Automation Follows ber of breakpoints in an automation playlist (in- Edit.
cluding Trim playlists) in order to improve system performance. The amount of thinning applied is Click the Automation Follows Edit indicator in  determined by the Degree of Thinning setting in the Edit window toolbar. The indicator is high- the Automation Preferences page. When using lighted blue when enabled. When disabled, the high amounts of thinning, the resulting automation indicator is highlighted orange.
may differ noticeably from the original automation moves. Thinning only applies to audio tracks, and does not affect MIDI tracks. For instructions, see “Thinning Automation” on page 1196.
AutoMatch Time AutoMatch Time is the amount of time it takes for Automation Follows Edit indicator in the Edit window a fader to return (by ramping up or down) to the level of automation still on the track as the automa- Automation Safe tion pass ends. This time value is set in the Auto- mation Preferences page (see “Automation Prefer- Outputs, sends, and plug-ins can be placed in Au- ences” on page 1179). tomation Safe mode. In Automation Safe mode, any automation associated with an Output window AutoMatch is automatically applied to all Touch (such as track or send level, panning, or mute), or mode passes, and can be applied to Latch or Write plug-in on that track, is protected from being over- passes.
written while automating other items on that track.
The AutoMatch Time also determines the rate at which delta values written with the Trim will re- turn to 0 dB (delta value of zero).
The AutoMatch feature works with continuous controls (such as Volume or Pan) by ramping their values back to previously automated levels. There are some stepped controls (for example, the EQ Automation Safe enabled send type in the 1-band EQ plug-in) that provide more Automation Safe mode suspends automation re- than two discrete steps over their operational cording for the selected track output, send, or plug- range. AutoMatch has no effect on these controls.
in that is enabled. You can also suspend automa- tion recording and playback session-wide from the Automation Follows Edit Option Automation window. See “Enabling and Suspend- ing Automation” on page 1193.
When disabled, automation events are not affected by edits to audio or MIDI notes. When enabled, au- tomation events are affected by edits to audio or MIDI notes.
1180 Pro Tools Reference Guide Viewing Automation Pro Tools creates a separate playlist in the Edit window for each type of automation in a track, in- cluding a playlist for each type of Trim automa- tion. This data can be viewed and edited in the same way as audio and MIDI data. You can either change Track Views to show the automation you want, or you can reveal an Automation lane under the track regardless of its Track View.
You can easily toggle between different Track Displaying Trim automation playlists Views. See “Changing Track Views” on page 255.
Track Views You can view automation data by type by selecting the corresponding Track View.

You can edit automation and control- ler data in lanes exactly the same way that you do in different automation and controller Track Views.

1186 Pro Tools Reference Guide To automate a send level, mute or pan: To copy a track’s settings or automation to one of its sends:
1 In the Automation window, make sure the auto- 1 Select the tracks you want to edit by clicking on mation type is write-enabled (send level, send the track names to highlight them.
mute, send pan).
2 Choose Edit > Automation > Copy to Send.
2 In the Mix or Edit window, set the Automation mode for each track containing sends you want to automate. For each track, click the Automa- tion Mode selector and set the Automation mode. For the initial automation pass, choose Write mode.
3 If sends are not currently visible on your tracks, select View > Mix Window (or Edit Window) > Sends A–E (or Sends F–J).
4 To display send controls, do one of the follow- ing:
• Click the send to open the Output window for the send that you want to automate.
• Select View > Sends A–E (or Sends F–J) and Copy To Send dialog select one of the individual views (such as Send A). In the Copy to Send dialog, do any of the fol- lowing:
5 Start playback to begin writing automation.
• Select Current Value to copy the current set- 6 Move the controls you want to automate.
tings of the corresponding controls.
7 When you have finished, stop playback. • Select Automation to copy the entire automa- tion playlist for the corresponding controls.

on page 1179).

must enable individual plug-in controls for auto- mation.

Using Pencil Tool Shapes You can draw automation for audio as well as MIDI tracks. For example, use the Triangle pattern MIDI Data drawn with resolution of 10 ms to control continuous functions, or the Square pat- tern to control switched functions such as Mute or Bypass.
Since the Pencil tool draws these shapes using the current Grid value, you can use it to perform pan- ning in tempo with a music track, or on frame scene changes when working in post production.
For more information on MIDI continuous MIDI Data drawn with resolution of 100 ms controller data, see “Continuous Controller For better performance, consider selecting a Events” on page 793.
smaller value for MIDI controls that need higher resolution (such as MIDI volume), and a larger Resolution of MIDI Controller value for controls that may not require a high reso- Data lution (such as Pan).

Chapter 53: Automation 1207 Glide Automation To enable Trim mode:
Considerations In the Mix or Edit window, click the Automa-  tion Mode selector of the track where you want When Glide Automation is applied to automation to trim automation, and select Trim.
data, it behaves as follows:
• When a selection is made, automation break- points are written at the start and end points of the Glide Automation. The automation value at the start of the selection is the start point of the Glide Automation and the end of the selection is the end point of the Glide Automation. The Glide Automation that is created between the two is based on the length of the selection and the end value that is selected.
• If automation breakpoints follow the selection, they are not changed, but the value selected for Enabling Trim mode the end of the selection is written from the end point up until the next breakpoint. When a track’s Trim mode is enabled, its Volume and Send Level faders turn yellow, and its Auto- • If no automation breakpoints follow the selec- mation Mode selector is outlined in yellow. This tion, the value selected for the end of the selec- outline appears solid if the track is trim-enabled, tion is written to the end of the track.
and flashes whenever trimming is occurring on the • If no selection is made, a breakpoint is written to track’s volume or send levels.
the current location, and the value selected for the Glide Automation is written to the next Writing Trim Automation breakpoint.

(Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) Requirements for Standard Pro Tools lets you write current automation values Write Automation Commands from any insertion point forward (or backward) to the end (or beginning) of a track or Edit selection For automation data to be written for a specific pa- in a track, or to an entire track or Edit selection, rameter, the associated track must be in one of the while performing an automation pass.
following Automation modes and meet the follow- ing conditions:
Latch Mode The control for the parameter must be changed (touched) during the automation pass.
Touch Mode The control for the parameter must be changed (touched).
Write Mode All controls on that track must be in Write mode.
This command can be undone.

Chapter 53: Automation 1215 A mute event can be extended when the automa- Overwriting or Extending tion pass begins before an on or off event and stops Mute Automation before the end of the second event.
(Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) Pro Tools lets you overwrite or extend an existing mute event in real time, without changing the cur- rent mute state.
A mute event can be overwritten when the automa- Original mute automation tion pass begins before the first mute event and (mute off, mute on, mute off) ends after the second event. In this case, the current on/off state of the mute is maintained.
Mute off event extended (up to automation pass end) Original mute automation (mute off, mute on, mute off) Extending a mute automation event Mute Overwrite/Extend is Supported in Touch and Latch Mode, as follows:
Touch Mode Writes the current state as long as the Mute button is held.
Latch Mode Writes the current state until you stop Mute on event overwritten or AutoMatch out.

(Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) 3 Click the Preview button in the Automation Automation Preview mode lets you audition window. The button lights green to indicate that changes to a mix and compare them to existing au- Preview mode is active.
tomation values without committing them to the automation playlist.
When you put Pro Tools in Automation Preview mode, you can preview values for automation-en- abled controls on any track that is in a writable au- tomation mode.
While in Automation Preview mode, when you touch or move a control, it isolates the control, dis- engaging it from its automation playlist. The con- trol stops reading or writing automation, allowing you to freely audition changes.
When you have found a level or state that you want to use, you can then commit or punch the pre- viewed value to the automation playlist for the iso- lated controls.
If you punch during playback, the automation  Preview controls in the Automation window mode is set to Latch and writing begins at the pre- 4 To isolate a control, touch or move an automa- view value.
tion-enabled control.You can isolate controls If you punch while the transport is stopped, the  during playback or while the transport is automation mode is set to Latch and the control is stopped.
primed at the preview value.
It is not necessary for the Allow Latch Prime in Stop preference to be enabled for Preview to put a control into Latch Prime.

1228 Pro Tools Reference Guide Chapter 54: Mixdown Pro Tools lets you mix down by recording a mix to Printing effects to disk is the technique of perma- new audio tracks or by bouncing a mix to disk. nently adding real-time effects, such as EQ or re- verb, to an audio track by bussing and recording it Recording to Tracks This is the process of sub- to new tracks with the effects added. The original mixing and recording the submix to new audio audio is preserved, so you can return to the source tracks, as you would any other track input signals.
track at any time. This can be useful when you This method requires available tracks, voices, and have a limited number of tracks or effects devices.
bus paths to accommodate the submix and the new tracks. While recording to tracks, you can manu- AudioSuite plug-ins provide another option ally adjust mixer or other controls. for writing (or “printing”) a plug-in effect to disk. For more information, see Chapter 43, Record to new tracks if you want to adjust mixer “AudioSuite Processing.” controls during the mixdown.
Bounce Mix The Bounce Mix command lets you Using Dither write a final mix, create a new audio loop, print ef- fects, or consolidate any submix to new audio files Dither can significantly improve audio quality on disk in any of several possible audio file for- when reducing the bit depth of digital audio.
mats. Any available output or bus path can be se- lected as the Mix Source. Sample rate, bit depth, When to Use a Dither Plug-In and other conversion processes can be applied during or after the bounce. You should use a dither plug-in in any situation where you are reducing bit depth, for example, when mixing down to a 16-bit file with the Bounce Selecting Audio for Loops, Mix command, or when sending your final mix to Submixes, and Effects an external digital device that records at 16-bit res- olution.
Both Bounce Mix and recording to tracks operate on the current Timeline or Edit selection, if one ex- A dither plug-in is necessary even when mixing ists. This makes it easy to turn multitrack selec- down to 16-bit from a 16-bit session. Even though tions into mono, stereo, or multichannel loops. 16-bit sessions use 16-bit files, they are still pro- Submixes, stems, and other specialized types of cessed at higher internal bit depths. ID3 Tag Type The ID3 tag stores data about the en- coded audio file that is used by MP3 players to dis- MXF (OP-Atom) play information about the file. Pro Tools supports (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Only) three versions of this tag, in order to provide back- ward compatibility with older MP3 players:
While OP-Atom MXF files can contain either one • ID3 v1.0: Appears at the end of a streaming MP3 channel of audio or one track of video per file, file, so that tag information is only displayed af- Pro Tools can only export audio when bouncing to ter software streaming is finished. (OP-Atom). Also, to ensure compatibility MXF with Non-Linear Editors (such as Avid Media • ID3 v1.1: Same as version 1.0, but adds track Composer) when bouncing to (OP-Atom), MXF number information to the tag.
is automatically selected as the only Multiple Mono • ID3 v2.3: Appears at the front of a streaming available option for and File Type Pad To Frame MP3 file, so that tag information is displayed is enforced.
Boundary when streaming begins.
MOV While ID3 v2.3 is the current standard MP3 format, not all MP3 players support ID3 .mov files can include both video and multiple v2.3. (Windows Only) Genre Choose a genre for the file. This informa- Windows Media formatted files can contain audio, tion is displayed by many MP3 players, and can video, or script data stored in Windows Media For- appear in searchable catalogs and databases.
mat. A Windows Media Format file may have a .asf or .wmv file name extension.

your program material—use a meter on a Master 10 Click Bounce. Fader, or use an external mastering deck’s meters to help identify clipping. If you are mastering to hard disk, avoid mixing full-code audio signals to- Mastering gether at unity or “0” level, as this invariably causes clipping.
In the final mastering process, you might record di- rectly to disk, Blue-ray, DAT, DVD, CD, stereo Mastering and Error-Correcting mastering recorder, or a multitrack recorder (for Media surround mixes).
Random access media (such as hard disks, or USB Once you have created a master of your session, or flash drives) can produce a true digital copy of you can transfer it to a portable medium for dupli- your data, because every bit value is maintained.
cation. You can transfer the master file to CD or Sequential media (such as digital tape) use error DAT to be used as a master for pressing compact correction schemes to fix the occasional bad data discs.
that is received in a digital transfer. These correc- tions are deviations from the actual data, and with Mastering to a Digital Recorder successive reproductions, represent a subtle form of generation loss.
Although it is usually best to master sessions di- rectly to hard disk, Pro Tools also lets you master You can avoid this loss by creating and maintain- digitally, direct to any AES/EBU-equipped or ing masters on random-access digital media (such S/PDIF-equipped digital recorder such as a DAT as a hard drive) and transferring them to sequential deck.
digital media only as needed.

Changing the output format for a given track has several effects:
• The panner that appears in the track will change to reflect the new output format.
• It may be necessary for one or more pan related automation playlists to be created or deleted.
Because changing the output format has these ef- fects, a warning dialog appears whenever you change the output format of a track to a format of fewer channels. Specifically, this will occur when- ever automation playlists will have to be deleted.
Channel Width Filtering (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Only) Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio software let you fil- ter which multichannel formats appear in the Track Format selector in the New Tracks dialog and also Channel Show/Hide dialog for the Default Path and New Paths Format selec- 3 Click OK.

Multichannel output path This provides a type of parallel mixing. You can create a variety of mixes of differing formats all at the same time by routing your elements to multiple paths.

When to Use Assign Multichannel Outputs Assign 5.1 paths only to those tracks that need  to be panned to all six channels.
For example, a sound effects track with a jet fly- over should be assigned a 5.1 path to fly the sound from front-to-back. In a music mix, you can fly a solo instrument or make a synth pad swirl around the room as a special effect.
Elements that need to be heard in all speakers si- multaneously can also be assigned to the multi- channel output, whether or not they require active panning in the sound field.
Chapter 56: Multichannel Tracks and Signal Routing 1263 When to Use Sub-Paths To help simplify large sessions, use sub-paths to route static (or, stationary) elements directly to the out-  put channel or channels.
For example, film dialog is often mixed to the center channel to anchor this essential sound element to the picture. Instead of assigning a six-channel panner to dialog tracks and panning the tracks to the center speaker only, you can route the dialog track’s main output to a mono (Center) sub-path.
Tracks routed to subpaths Tracks routed to main Auxiliary Input routed to 5.0 5.1 surround path sub-path Effect bus routing Outputs to sub-paths Outputs to main 5.1 path for surround panning Using signal routing and sub-paths to mix in surround 1264 Pro Tools Reference Guide Extending Stereo Mixing Example Output Paths Conventions to Surround Mixing The following figure shows output paths defined in Stereo mixing sets the precedent for active and the I/O Setup dialog of an example session that static panning, and surround mixing can benefit was created on a HDX system with an HD OMNI from the same basic principles. audio interface.
Panning a sound back-and-forth between the left and right speakers is best used as a special effect.
In a typical music mix, the basic tracks are placed in the stereo sound field and remain there.
Surround mixes can become incoherent if too many elements are continuously moving, For spe- I/O Setup, example output paths cial effects, some tracks can be panned dynami- cally, bouncing between speakers or sweeping Example Bus Paths from one side to the other.
The following figure shows example bus paths.
An LCR format path (“Dialog”), a stereo format Example Paths and Signal path (“Music”) and a 5.1 format path (“FX”) have Routing for a Surround Mix been defined, each with several of their own sub- paths.
Signal routing is the key element in any Pro Tools surround session. Signal routing is configured and defined in the I/O Setup dialog.
The examples that follow show how Auxiliary In- puts, Master Faders, and other Pro Tools signal routing features can be used for stem mixes, sub- I/O Setup, example bus paths mixes, and similar project needs.
In these examples, music and effects are being mixed for a trailer, in 5.1 surround. Separate stem mixes (for music and for effects) are to be mas- tered to 8-track MDM.

Output Windows Output windows provide panning controls (called panners), as well as standard Pro Tools controls, for tracks of all mix formats (from 3-channel LCR through 8-channel 7.1).
For information on Output window standard con- trols, see “Standard Controls” on page 1273. For information on Output window panning controls, see “Surround Panner Controls” on page 1274.
Some of the features of Output windows include:
• X/Y (joystick-style) panning • 3-Knob panning • Full Divergence and Center Percentage control • AutoGlide • LFE feed • Multichannel meters • Pro Tools target window support Drag to pan in the Track Grid Multiple panners can be opened simultaneously, or The Pan Location cursor follows your movements a single window can display the current panner.
as long as you hold the mouse button. Movements are scaled so that, once you click to “grab” the Pan Location cursor, you do not need to limit your movements to the small Track Grid area.
1272 Pro Tools Reference Guide To open an Output window:
Standard Controls Click the Output Window button (the small  fader at the right edge of the Output selector for All Output windows provide standard Pro Tools a track in the Mix or Edit window (I/O View). controls for routing, path assignment and other track features. These controls are located at the top of all Output, Send, Insert, and Plug-In windows (see “Standard Selector Controls in Output Win- dows” on page 1108).
Show meters Path selector Target icon Track selector Output selector Automation Safe Panner Control section Track Fader, Solo, Mute, and Auto All Output windows provide track volume and mute controls. You can adjust and automate track volume, or mute, directly using these controls. The larger fader and Mute button are equivalent to those in the Pro Tools Mix window.
Opening a multichannel Output window For instructions on managing multiple Out- put windows, see “Output Windows for Tracks and Sends” on page 1106.
Chapter 57: Surround Panning and Mixing 1273 Surround Panner Controls When assigned to tracks or paths with four or more channels, the Output window provides an X/Y Grid for surround panning. The speakers associated with the panner’s multichannel format are displayed in their relative positions outside the Panner Grid. These speakers are also used as Snap Pan to Speaker controls.
This section identifies all the controls and features found in Pro Tools multichannel panners.
Click to show meters Snap Pan to Speaker icons LFE fader Pan Location cursor Track Automation.
Solo, and Mute X/Y Grid Panning Mode button Track fader and meter Position (knob panners) Divergence Center % Output window with a 7.1 format multichannel panner 1274 Pro Tools Reference Guide Links (or unlinks) the Front The X/Y Grid and Pan Location Front/Rear Linking and Rear left/right positions controls (they are Cursor linked by default). When linked, the F/R control The X/Y Grid is where multichannel panning in- pans the signal front to back always along the same formation is input and displayed. You can input left/right path. When unlinked, you can pan front pan information using X/Y mode, 3-Knob mode, to back diagonally according to independent Front or by entering numeric values in the Position data and Rear settings.
fields. You can also edit pan automation graphi- cally in the Edit window.
Multichannel panners default to X/Y mode. In X/Y mode, the track’s current pan position is repre- sented by a green dot. This dot is the Pan Location cursor, and its color indicates track automation sta- tus using standard Pro Tools colors: Front and Rear positions linked (left) versus Front and Rear positions unlinked (right) Green The track is in automation Read mode.
Center % Control Red The track is in automation Write, Touch, Center % controls how much of the signal is routed Latch, or Touch/Latch mode.
to the center speaker. This lets you mix a track to Yellow The track is in automation Off mode (or the front with a phantom center (0% Center value) Automation Suspend) mode. or to three-channel (LCR) when panning front.
Panning Mode Button Divergence Controls This button, located below the X/Y Grid, provides Divergence determines the width of the panned access to Surround panning modes (see “Panning signal with respect to neighboring speakers. (For Modes” on page 1276). more information, see the Pro Tools Sync & Sur- round Concepts Guide). There are three Diver- gence controls:

Chapter 57: Surround Panning and Mixing 1275 Snap Pan to Speaker Icons The Front/Rear Inverse pan control is unlinked by default.
The Snap Pan to Speaker icons let you force the panner to the speaker’s location. For example, Link clicking on the upper left speaker moves the Pan Front Inverse Location cursor to the upper left corner of the X/Y Grid. Divergence and Center Percentage Divergence controls The Divergence and Center Percentage controls range from 0 to 100, and can be automated. They are especially useful for ensuring audibility and coverage in large venues, and to increase clarity of Divergence display and controls voices.
Chapter 57: Surround Panning and Mixing 1279 By default, Pro Tools surround panners are 100% Divergence Editing Mode or fully divergent, meaning that a signal that is In Divergence Editing mode, you can adjust the di- panned completely to one speaker will only be au- vergence boundaries by dragging directly in the dible in that speaker.
Divergence Grid.

cursor is positioned next to a single speaker.

Center % (Percentage) In LCR, LCRS, 5.0, 5.1, 6.0, 6.1 7.0, 7.0 SDDS, 7.1, and 7.1 SDDS surround formats, the Center % Front controls whether there is a discrete center channel Divergence for the track or a phantom center channel.
Center Percentage Example Front Divergence at less than 100% for wider panning In film and video production, the center channel To adjust divergence:
often contains dialog. To enhance the clarity of di- Adjust the Front, Rear, and Front/Rear Diver-  alog, it is often beneficial to keep elements such as gence controls as necessary.
music out of the center speaker. By reducing the Center Percentage on music tracks, music panned to the front of the soundfield can be panned to only the left and right speakers, forcing them to have a variable phantom center image.

center.

Panners The LFE fader controls how much of the track is sent to LFE. LFE faders are only available when a track is assigned to a path whose format supports LFE (5.1, 6.1, or 7.1).
For general information about LFE, sub channels, and related topics, see the Pro Tools Sync & Surround Concepts Guide.

Compressor/Limiter To process the LFE channel in a multichannel plug-in:
Click to enable LFE Enable in supporting mul-  tichannel plug-ins. LFE Enable is highlighted when LFE processing is enabled, and unhigh- lighted when LFE processing is bypassed.
When LFE Enable is unavailable, try using the multi-mono version of the plug-in.

Chapter 58: Mixing to Dolby Atmos 1285 Installing and Configuring Dolby Atmos Software and Hardware To use Pro Tools with Dolby Atmos, follow the instructions provided by Dolby for installing and configuring your Dolby Atmos hardware (if any) and software. Pro Tools cannot provide essential Dolby Atmos features if the necessary Dolby Atmos software is not correctly installed, configured, and authorized on your Pro Tools system.
For complete information on installing, configuring, and using Dolby Atmos products, please refer to the appropriate documentation for your specific Dolby Atmos products published by Dolby.
Example Hardware-Based Dolby Atmos System Configuration A basic hardware-based Dolby Atmos system includes the following components:
• One or more Pro Tools | HDX systems for audio playback and panning control for sound objects.
• Optional dubbing (recording) Pro Tools | HDX system • One or two Avid HD MADI audio interfaces per Pro Tools system for digital audio streaming from Pro Tools to the Dolby Atmos RMU or rendering and mastering workstation (MADI optical or coaxial connections) • Dolby Atmos Rendering and Mastering Unit (RMU)—applies object panning and creates the Dolby Atmos master from your Pro Tools mix • Dolby Atmos Monitor application (installed and running on a Pro Tools system) • Dolby Atmos Renderer Web Client for configuring the Dolby Atmos RMU, setting up beds (speaker/room configurations) and objects (panning with metadata through speaker zones).
Control Surface such as Avid S6 (optional) [Ethernet] [DigiLink] [MADI] Pro Tools | HDX system 7.1.2 track (bed mix) bed object inputs Object source tracks 11–64 object inputs 65–128 Figure 1. ing the check box in its row in the Mapping to Output column.
Reasons mappings are important.
• Creates a relationship with the bus and the 4 Select the check box in its row in the Mapping to Renderer. Mapped buses are routed to the Renderer column. The output bus is automati- Renderer, and not just to a direct output. cally mapped to the matching 7.1.2 bed input to the Renderer.
• Ties pan data from a bus to an object.
• Determines what is bounced to an ADM Mapping Object Busses to BWF file (see “Export ADM files” on Renderer Input Paths page 1310).

1292 Pro Tools Reference Guide Only groups that are assigned to mapped beds and objects appear in the groups list. If a group (other than the default groups) exists in the Renderer, but that group is not used for an object or bed that Pro Tools is using, that group does not appear in the Manage Bed/Ob- jects Groups dialog in Pro Tools.
4 If Pro Tools is selected for managing groups, you can do any of the following:

• Click the name of a group in the list and type to rename it.
• Click the “X” to the right of the name of a Manage Bed/Object Groups dialog group in the list to delete it.

Chapter 58: Mixing to Dolby Atmos 1293 To assign a Bed or Object bus to a group: 5 Create a new 7.1.2 audio track.
1 Open the I/O Setup (Setup > I/O) and click the 6 Assign the 7.1.2 track Output to the 7.1.2 Bed Bus tab.
o3utput path (“Bed Monitor”).
2 In the Bed/Object Group column, click the se- 7 Assign the 7.1.2 track Input to the 7.1.2 internal lector for the bus you want to assign.
bus (“Bed Mix”).
8 Create as many new tracks as needed for source audio.
9 Assign the track Output for each source track to the 7.1.2 internal bus (“Bed Mix”).
10 Enable Input monitor on the 7.1.2 audio track.
11 Mix the audio on the source tracks to the 7.1.2 audio track using volume and pan automation.
Assigning a bus to a Bed/Object Group 3 Select the desired group name (such as FX). To remove a bus from a group, select no group.
Mixing Bed Material Mix bed material for a Dolby Atmos mix to 7.1.2.
Bus record the bed mix to a 7.1.2 audio track in preparation for playback and mixing to a Renderer.
To configure a Pro Tools session for mixing bed material:
1 In the Output page of the I/O Setup, create a 7.1.2 Output path assigned to the first ten phys- ical outputs of your audio interface (for moni- toring the mix with a 7.1.2 speaker configuration).
Example Bed mix 2 In the Bus page of the I/O setup, create a 7.1.2 bus and map it to the 7.1.2 output (“Bed Moni- tor”).
3 Then map that 7.1.2 output bus to the first 10 in- puts of the Renderer.
4 Create another 7.1.2 bus with the Auto-create sub-paths option enabled (“Bed Mix”).

Object view, Mix window Chapter 58: Mixing to Dolby Atmos 1297 Object Controls Object controls shown in the Edit and Mix window Object view mirror each other on a track-by-track basis. These controls let you assign audio, Auxil- iary Input, and Instrument tracks to object output paths, automate toggling of the track between the Bus and Object output, and set the control mode for object panning metadata.
Object Path selector Renderer Connection Status indicator Output Window button Object Control Mode button Bus/Object toggle Object controls Object Path Selector The Object Path Selector lets you assign the track to any available object output path (defined in the Bus page of the I/O Setup).
Selecting an object output path for a mono audio track Output Window Button The Output Window button in the Object view opens the Output Window for the track.
1298 Pro Tools Reference Guide Bus/Object Toggle The Bus/Object toggle switches the routing of track audio between the Output selected in the I/O view (Bus) and the selected object output path (Object).
• When toggled to Bus, the track behaves like a normal Pro Tools track. The only exception is that track panning is controlled by the 7.1.2 object panner regardless of the channel width of the selected Output.
• When toggled to Object, the selected Output in I/O view is completely bypassed and track audio follows the selected object output path to the Renderer. Also, all pan automation is translated to Dolby Atmos metadata and sent over the network to the Renderer. No panning automation is applied to the track audio in Pro Tools.
Bus/Object toggle can be automated (see “Object/Bus Toggle Automation” on page 1304).
Object Control Mode The Object Control Mode button lets you cycle between the three different Master Object Control modes:
Master, Record, and Off/Bypass. Click the Object Control Mode button to cycle through and select the Object Control mode.
Object pan metadata for a track is only included in an exported BWF ADM file if Object Control Mode is set to Master for that track.
Mode Description Purpose Master Object assigned, Set by default if no other tracks are already assigned to this sending metadata object, use this mode to control an object on the Renderer.
Record Object assigned, Use this mode in a re-recording workflow to record pan record mode automation sent from a Master panner on the network.
Off/bypass Object assigned, no Use this mode to mix multiple tracks of audio to the same metadata being sent object output path. In this workflow, one track set as Master controls the panning, while one or more other tracks with the same object output path assignment but set to Off/bypass feed audio to the object output path but without sending pan metadata.
Renderer Connection Status Indicator The Renderer Connection Status indicator appears in the title bar of the Object view to the right of the title in the Edit and window if Atmos is enabled in the Peripherals dialog, but not if Atmos is disabled. It is green if Pro Tools is successfully connected and communicating with the Renderer on the network. It is pale yellow if it is not connected or the network connection has failed.
Chapter 58: Mixing to Dolby Atmos 1299 7.0.2, 7.1.2, and Object Panner When a mono or stereo track is assigned to a 7.0.2 or 7.1.2 output bus, or to an object output path, it uses the cor- responding 7.0.2, 7.1.2, or Object panner.
Figure 2. Output window in standard (2D) view for a mono track assigned to a 7.1.2 bus (left) and an Object (right) Panner Controls and Indicators The following elements of the Pro Tools Output window are specific to the 7.0.2, 7.1.2, and Object pan- ners. The Output window for each provides the same controls, except that the Object panner additionally provides the Object Output Path selector and the Object Control Mode button, and the 7.0.2 panner ex- cludes the LFE fader.
1300 Pro Tools Reference Guide Theater Mode The Pro Tools Panning Mode selector provides the option, which is only available with the 7.0.2, Theater 7.1.2, and Object panners. When this mode is selected, the panner displays a 3D/Theater view.

Automation Option 1 Open the Output window for the track you want.

when mixing to Dolby Atmos (7.0.2 or 7.1.2).
• Right-click the Height Mode selector and se- When this option is enabled, the selected auto- lect (or deselect) Auto-Height Overrides height mode overrides any Height automation, Height Automation.
generating new height automation automatically.
This way you don’t have to re-write all new auto- mation when opening existing 5.1 or 7.1 sessions and re-mixing them for Dolby Atmos (7.0.2 or 7.1.2 panning).
The Auto-Height Overrides Height Automa- tion option defaults to on if the track already has a main output assignment prior to assign- ing an object output (assuming a re-use work- flow). This option defaults to off when trans- lating automation from the Dolby Atmos Panner plug-in and also when a track has an object output assigned but no main output as- signed.

Chapter 58: Mixing to Dolby Atmos 1313 Chapter 59: Ambisonics 1st Order Ambisonics correspond to physical mi- Pro Tools Support for crophone pickup patterns using an omni-direc- Ambisonics tional microphone in conjunction with figure-eight (Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio Software Only) capsules oriented along three spatial axes. Miking for 1st Order Ambisonics is a three-dimensional Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio software provides extension of common Mid/Side stereo (M/S) support for 1st-, 2nd-, and 3rd-Order Ambisonics miking techniques that adds additional difference channel formats (4-, 9-, and 16-channel formats re- channels for height and depth. This results in spectively). Import Ambisonics audio recordings, B-format audio (WXYZ), or 1st Order Ambison- record Ambisonics audio in Pro Tools using Am- ics, which consists of four discrete audio channels:
bisonics miking techniques, or use supported 3rd- W for sound pressure (omni-directional—the M in party AAX Ambisonics panning, and encoding M/S), X for the front-minus-back sound pressure and decoding plug-ins to create your own Ambi- gradient, Y for left-minus-right (the S in M/S), and sonics mixes from source audio tracks.
Z for up-minus-down.
1st Order Ambisonics is most commonly used for What is Ambisonics? horizontal-only surround because of positional er- rors and shading effects that result from adding a Ambisonics is a “full-sphere” surround sound fourth microphone. Higher-order Ambisonics pro- technique that is capable of representing sound vide greater positional resolution of sound sources sources that are stationary or in motion, and at vari- in the sound field. Higher-order difference signals able distances from above, below, and all around are derived from several spatially distributed (usu- the listener. More interestingly though, higher or- ally omni-directional) capsules using sophisticated der Ambisonics can represent sound sources that digital signal processing. Higher-order Ambison- seem to remain fixed in a sound field while the lis- ics can also be rendered from channel-based audio tener’s perspective changes with their head posi- recordings (such as mono and stereo) strictly using tion (as opposed to “head-locked” stereo mixes).
Ambisonics plug-in processing in Pro Tools.
Ambisonics audio mixes are used for 3D audio in virtual reality and gaming applications, as well as 360 video. However, the possibilities for Ambi- sonics are only just beginning to be explored com- mercially.
Chapter 59: Ambisonics 1315 Configuring the I/O Setup for Ambisonics Pro Tools lets you create 1st-, 2nd-, and 3rd-Order Ambisonics input, output, and bus paths. Typically, you will only need to create bus paths in Ambisonics formats for most Ambisonics mixing workflows. Create Ambisonics Input paths if you will be recording Ambisonics right into Pro Tools using Ambisonics miking techniques (using specially configured microphone arrays or specialized commercially available Ambi- sonics microphones). It is unlikely that you would ever need to create Ambisonics Output paths unless you are playing back Ambisonics from one Pro Tools system and recording it on another Pro Tools system or using another system to decode Ambisonics audio.
To create an Ambisonics bus path:
Choose Setup > I/O and click the Bus tab.
Click the New Path button.
Create the busses that you need with the desired Ambisonics format.
Selecting 1st Order Ambisonics as the format for a new bus path 4 Click Create.
5 Click OK to close the I/O Setup.
1316 Pro Tools Reference Guide Ambisonics Sub-Paths Any higher-order Ambisonics path can have lower-order Ambisonics sub-paths. For example, a 3rd-order Ambisonics path can have 2nd and 1st–order sub-paths, and a 2nd-order Ambisonics path can have a 1st- order sub-path. All supported orders of Ambisonics paths can have mono sub-paths. Since Ambisonics channels do not map directly to speaker channels, all other channel-width formats are disallowed as sub- paths for Ambisonics paths.
Selecting a 2nd Order Ambisonics sub-path for a 3rd-order Ambisonics path Channel Ordering for Ambisonics Audio Signal Paths Ambisonics formats use discrete channel orderings that are distinct from channel-based surround formats (such as 5.1). In Pro Tools, the individual channels of all Ambisonics formats are numbered from left to right in the I/O Setup starting with 00 (or W in B-format) and increase incrementally. For example, the in- dividual channels for 1st Order Ambisonics are 00, 01, 02, 03 (WXYZ); and for 2nd Order Ambisonics are 00, 01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 07, 08.
Figure 5. Ambisonics bus paths with standard channel orders in the I/O Setup The individual channels of Ambisonics formats do not correspond to speaker signal mappings like other surround formats (such as 5.1—L C R Ls Rs LFE). In most cases, use Ambisonics panning, encoder and decoder plug-ins to accurately mix and monitor Ambisonics mixes over headphones. However, even though Ambisonics formats do not directly map to common channel-based surround speaker configura- tions, various alternate speaker configurations can be used for the playback of Ambisonics mixes.
Do not use multi-mono plug-ins following any Ambisonics plug-ins. Likewise, any plug-in processing of source tracks should only be inserted before Ambisonics plug-ins.
Chapter 59: Ambisonics 1317 Ambisonics Mixes in Pro Tools Creating Ambisonics mixes in Pro Tools usually entails using Avid-qualified third-party AAX Ambison- ics panning plug-ins on source audio tracks and routing the output of those tracks to an Auxiliary Input track with an Ambisonics format–to-stereo plug-in for headphone monitoring. Ambisonics mixing work- flows and configurations vary depending on the source material, specific Ambisonics plug-ins used, and required delivery formats.
Figure 6. Chapter 59: Ambisonics 1319 Folding Down Ambisonics You can route ambisonics formats to other formats for monitoring, but not for conversion between formats. When routing between Ambisonics and other formats, the following conditions apply:
• Ambisonics routed to a channel-based format are summed to mono at –3 dB.
• Channel-based formats routed to ambisonics are summed to channel 0 at –3 dB.
• Ambisonics of a lower order routed to a higher order map to the first channels only.
• Ambisonics of higher order routed to a lower or- der map the first channels to what is available in the lower order format, but drop any channels above that.
1320 Pro Tools Reference Guide Part XI: Sync and Video Chapter 60: Working with Synchronization Synchronization allows one system to output time- Timecode Trigger Only code and the other devices to chase (follow) that This solution does not require a synchronization timecode. Pro Tools can synchronize with other peripheral, but is really only useful for short proj- devices using Timecode.
ects if both systems are extremely close in speed.
Even the best systems will rarely stay perfectly synchronized for longer than a couple of minutes.

Session Length System Delay Use this field to specify a limit for the length of the When Delay Compensation is enabled (Options > Delay Compensation), this displays the total hours). Used in conjunction with the Session Start amount of delay (in samples) applied by the sys- setting, the Session Length field lets you specify tem to compensate for delays incurred by plug-in the desired working range for the session when processing and mixer routing.
Pro Tools is controlled by an external transport (such as with MachineControl, MMC, or Satellite Link). If a timecode location is received outside of this range, Pro Tools will not respond. Pro Tools Chapter 60: Working with Synchronization 1327 responds only when a timecode location within Sync X Setup in the Session range is received. This prevents untended jumps to Setup Window the end of the timeline, thus providing intelligent handling of 24-hour timecode. (HDX and HD Native Systems with Sync X Only) Timecode The controls in the section of the Sync X Setup Session Setup window let you configure the Clock If your Clock Reference Source is set to Internal Reference, Video Reference, and Positional Refer- and Pro Tools is generating timecode, this displays ence settings for Sync X.

Resolve All Components of Your System 29.97 fps Non-Drop Rate Not Recognized When striping timecode, make sure that the time- 29.97 fps Non-Drop is a slightly slower version of code generator and the video record deck are re- 30 fps Non-Drop timecode. When 29.97 fps Non- solved to the same crystal reference. For example, Drop is used with color video, each video frame when striping 29.97 Drop Frame timecode onto a matches up with each Timecode frame without VTR, both the Timecode generator and the VTR having to use a drop-frame coding. This makes any should be resolved to the same “black burst” or frame number mathematics much simpler, since no house sync generator. During playback, the master frame numbers are dropped.
deck should be resolved to “black burst” or house sync.
Chapter 60: Working with Synchronization 1349 Some hardware and software devices do not recog- nize 29.97 Non-Drop as a separate frame rate. For example, any standard LTC/VITC-to MTC-con- verter does not explicitly recognize it. You must set the converter to expect 30 fps Non-Drop in- stead. In fact, many devices that read Timecode work acceptably by reading 29.97 Non-Drop if they are set to expect 30 fps Non-Drop.
Any Timecode reader that uses the timecode num- bers to make real-time calculations (as Pro Tools does when it tries to trigger and synchronize to Timecode) also needs to know that the frame for- mat is 29.97 and not 30 fps. Since Pro Tools al- lows this choice of frame rate this does not really pose a problem. The problem exists because many users cannot readily distinguish 29.97 from 30 fps.
Even though NTSC tapes only use 29.97, some production companies will distribute video work prints striped with 29.97 fps but mark them as “30 fps NTSC,” by which they actually mean 29.97 fps Non-Drop.
By the time you get the tape, you may have no idea what’s actually on it. Feeding 29.97 Non-Drop to Pro Tools when it is set for 30 fps Non-Drop will result in timing errors of about 1.8 frames per min- ute, causing audio playback to trigger out of sync.
Use a Consistent Clock Source When possible, media files should be played back using the same sync reference signal they were re- corded with. This assures the closest match be- tween record and playback sample rates.

Pro Tools can generate LTC while the Transport is online, but only when Posi- tional Reference is set to Serial Timecode.

Assemble mode should be used carefully because it arms all tracks on the target deck for recording— including the video track, timecode track and con- trol track. Pro Tools plays back audio only when the received command requires audio playback at 1X speed in Auto Edit Recording the forward direction. Reverse playback and vari- speed playback are not supported in Pro Tools. Punch-In/Punch-Out Recording supports punch- on-the-fly, with Pro Tools in QuickPunch mode. In this mode, tracks are armed before the record pass, and recording begins and ends when Punch-In and Punch-Out commands are received form the mas- ter machine.

tion audio recorder, is a device used by a produc- tion sound mixer during a film or video shoot to make a multichannel recording of multiple micro- Field Recorder and phone inputs recorded simultaneously.

should be encoded with start and end time stamps representing SMPTE timecode or linear timecode Field Recorder Terminology (also known as LTC). files and certain types of metadata entered on field • Information embedded in a media file. This may recorders.

bling a sequence.

a Broadcast WAV file (or BWF) while still main- taining a .WAV file extension. Broadcast WAV Keycode Each frame of film stock is embedded files can contain both BEXT and iXML chunks, or during manufacturing with a unique identifying a BEXT chunk by itself.
timecode position known as keycode.

cend beginning from a certain starting time of day.

some features that conform to standards used by Avid video editing applications and Pro Tools— Production Sound Mixer specifically, Description metadata is interpreted differently.

ent based on the following order:
• Telecine operator (film workflow only) • Avid OMF-wrapped media or MXF media • Avid video editor • Any iXML chunk metadata in a BWF • Pro Tools editor • Any BEXT chunk metadata in a BWF 1388 Pro Tools Reference Guide Production Terminology for Film Telecine and Telecine Videotape Workflows Only The Telecine is a machine used to transfer film The following terminology applies only to film footage and synchronized audio to videotape (re- workflows: ferred to as the dailies).
The telecine videotape contains the dailies. The Shootlist SMPTE timecode on the telecine videotape will be The shootlist is a document in which the camera different from the timecode recorded on location.
assistant records each scene and take relative to its keycode value on the film stock. It is later used by FLEx File the telecine operator to synchronize the audio and The FLEx file is a text file generated during a tele- video together.
cine session that relates film keycode, field re- corder timecode, and telecine videotape timecode.
Sound Log When the telecine videotape is batch digitized in The sound log is a document in which the produc- the Avid video editing application, the information tion sound mixer records each scene and take rela- in the FLEx file is used to assign the original (lo- tive to its beginning timecode position.
cation) timecode to the resulting audio and video source files.
Slate Operator and Clapboard Information The slate operator holds and labels the slate (also known as a clapboard) for each new scene and Supported Field Recorder take. At the start of each take, the slate operator Audio Files and Metadata claps the slate, which simultaneously captures the Pro Tools supports the following audio files re- following information:
corded by field recorders:
• The scene, take, and timecode information is • OMF-wrapped media captured visually on film.
• MXF • The sound of the slate clapping is captured by the field recorder, which should be using the • Broadcast WAV (BWF) files with the following same timecode as displayed on the slate. metadata:
• Both BEXT and iXML chunks The telecine operator uses all of the information • BEXT metadata but no iXML metadata above to synchronize used film stock to the re- corded audio in order to create the telecine video- tape.

Pro Tools lets you open and import OMF or AAF sequences that reference audio files with mixed sample rates or bit depths. Audio files will be converted to the highest sample rate and of the files being imported (for new ses- sions) or to the current sample rate (for exist- ing sessions). Files with mixed bit depths that are already at the target sample rate are im- ported without conversion.
Chapter 62: Working with Field Recorders in Pro Tools 1393 Importing Raw Production This misalignment results from the incompatibility Source Audio Directly into between frame-accurate time stamping in Avid Pro Tools video editing applications and sample-accurate time stamping in Pro Tools. When you attempt to If the AAF or OMF sequence you imported into link the two, the misalignment results.
Pro Tools did not include raw production source audio, you may import it into the session sepa- In order to repair this discrepancy, you may need to rately using media supplied by the production manually offset the raw production source audio sound mixer.
after importing it into Pro Tools. Another option is to use a utility like SynchroArts Titan to process You can import monophonic or polyphonic audio and align the source audio.
files from field recorders into Pro Tools using any of the following methods:

the Timeline) if all of the following are true:

• Criteria set in the Match Criteria dialog is met.
• The metadata embedded during shooting and re- cording has been preserved prior to import into Pro Tools.

The Select Areas to Search option lets you specify additional locations (such as alternate folders or Re-Indexing hard drives) for Pro Tools to search for matches.

Missing Search Folders Folders that you select as search areas can go miss- ing for a variety of reasons (such as deletion or the absence of a needed hard drive). If a folder is miss- ing, Pro Tools shows an error message when you open the Select Areas to Search dialog. The miss- Select Areas to Search dialog ing path name is displayed in the list in gray-col- ored italic text.

zeroes), enter T001 with two zeroes—not T01 with one zero or T0001 with three zeroes.

Tape name character for character. For ex- When the digitize is complete, use the Avid ample, if the metadata was T001 (with two video editing application to edit audio and zeroes), enter T001 with two zeroes—not T01 video. with one zero or T0001 with three zeroes.
When you are finished editing, export the edited Use the Avid video editing application to edit sequence as an AAF or OMF sequence so that it audio and video.
can be imported into Pro Tools.
When you are finished editing, export the edited sequence as an AAF or OMF sequence so that it can be imported into Pro Tools.

Video Tracks Video tracks let you add or import video to the Timeline, as follows:
• Pro Tools Studio lets you import one video track per session, with a limit of one video playlist and one MXF or QuickTime video file on that track.
• Pro Tools Ultimate lets you add multiple video Empty video track tracks to the Timeline, with multiple video play- lists and multiple video files per track.
• Pro Tools Ultimate lets you mix QuickTime and MXF video tracks in the Timeline (but not on the same video track).
Chapter 63: Working with Video in Pro Tools 1411 Multiple Video Tracks in the Main Video Track Timeline (Pro Tools Ultimate Only) The main video track is the video track that is cur- rently online, and will be played back with any au- You can add multiple video tracks to the Timeline, dio in the session.
but only one video track can be played back at a time. This track is known as the main video track.
When the main video track is offline, you can play back the audio in your session without playing Multiple Video Track Playlists back the video. When the video is offline, the Video Online button changes to gray so you can (Pro Tools Ultimate Only) tell at a glance whether your video is offline or Playlists provide an easy way of changing back simply playing black fill.
and forth between alternate versions of a video track. If no video track is currently online, the main video track is the video track that was last online.
You can perform all of the same operations with video playlists as you can with audio playlists, in- To select the main video track:
cluding:
Click the Video Online button of any track that  • Duplicating a playlist is offline (gray) to put it online (blue), making • Creating a playlist that track the main video track.

1412 Pro Tools Reference Guide To set the view for the video track: Blocks View On the video track, select Frames or Blocks  In Blocks View, video frames are replaced by col- from the View Options pop-up menu.
ored blocks.
Frames View In Frames View, video data is displayed as pictures in the video track. These pictures are computed based on the video image, and scale according to Video track in Blocks View your track zoom and height settings. They allow you to easily find a scene or sequence in the video Video Online Button track.
The Video Online button lets you select the main video track, switch that track online or offline, and see at a glance by color whether a video track is of- fline (gray) or online (blue).
Video track in Frames View Frames do not display properly if you change zoom settings during playback. Blank frames are drawn until playback is stopped. You should stop playback before changing zoom settings if you want to view frames.
If video pull up or pull down is enabled, frames do not change position.

Chapter 63: Working with Video in Pro Tools 1413 Video Dropped Frames Indicator Move the mouse cursor over the indicator to show a tooltip that reports the following information af- Pro Tools provides an indicator on video tracks to ter playback is stopped:

If you encounter • Dropped Frames: <sum of skipped, pipeline a warning about dropped frames during playback, dropped frames> the tooltip provides an issue report when you stop • Disk Read Times: <in ms> playback.
• Possible Reason(s) for Dropped Frames Possible reasons for dropped frames can include:
Disk may be too slow reported if the hard drive could not keep up with the video media during playback. Possible solutions include:
• Use a faster drive that can handle the band- width of the media.
• Use a local drive if the media resides on net- work storage.
Dropped Frames indicator red Previous playback report in tooltip • Use Bounce Mix to create a lower bandwidth MOV file to use in the session.
Dropped Frames indicator states • Split audio and video media onto separate Playback drives to remove bandwidth contention.

Video Engine Rate When a video track is locked, Pro Tools restricts The video engine rate represents the number of all editing operations involving the track (except video frames output by Pro Tools per second, and for locking and unlocking video clips).
is determined automatically by the frame rate of the first video file added to a session. of frames displayed per second in grids, rulers, and counters. You can set the session timecode rate manually with the Timecode Rate pop-up menu in the Session Setup window. Importing Video into Pro Tools Using Import Session Data You can use the Import Session Data command to import video from other Pro Tools sessions or from an AAF sequence. Any video files you im- port must have the same video frame rate as any video file already present in the session.
For information on importing session data, see “Importing Session Data” on page 443.

When you paste audio and video simultane- ously, this video frame re-alignment may re- sult in the audio not being positioned exactly where you placed it. A warning dialog displays when this occurs.
Using the Insert Silence Command to Insert Space in Video Tracks The command lets you make a selec- Insert Silence tion on a track (or tracks) and insert precisely that amount of space. You can also select across video and audio tracks, and use the Insert Silence com- mand to insert audio silence and space simultane- ously.

Chapter 63: Working with Video in Pro Tools 1435 Bouncing the Video Track to a QuickTime Movie (macOS Mojave and Below, and Windows with QuickTime Installed Only) When you have finished your final mix and syn- chronized your audio events to the movie, you can bounce the main video track and a mono, stereo, or greater-than-stereo (Pro Tools Ultimate and Stu- dio only) mixdown of your session to a new Quick- Time movie. Pro Tools Ultimate and Studio also let you bounce multiple stems to QuickTime.
You can choose from a variety of standard Quick- Time codecs, which are fully compatible with other applications that support QuickTime .mov files.
To bounce video and audio in the video track to a QuickTime movie:
Finalize your mix (or stems).

VBR typically takes longer than CBR en- DNxHD Provides the following compression coding, but it is preferred for most non-web options:
content delivery.
• DNxHD LB (lowest bandwidth) • DNxHD SQ Color Space and Bounce Mix • DNxHD HQ (highest bandwidth) When exporting video using Bounce Mix, transcoding to a new file uses the selected color DNxHR Provides the following compression space of the video track. When enabled, the Same options:
as Source option only exports files using the • DNxHR LB (lowest bandwidth) YCbCr 709 color space • DNxHR SQ If a color space is not supported for export, you • DNxHR HQ (highest bandwidth) are notified and YCbCr 709 is used instead.
Apple ProRes Provides the following compres- sion options:
• ProRes Proxy (lowest bandwidth) • ProRes LT • ProRes HQ (highest bandwidth) 1440 Pro Tools Reference Guide MOV Export with PCM or AAC Audio Pro Tools can export either PCM (uncompressed) or AAC (compressed) audio when bouncing to MOV.
When bouncing AAC audio, you can select either a constant bit rate (CBR) or variable bit rate (VBR) encoding modes for mono, stereo, 5.1, or 7.1 mix sources:
• CBR lets you select a specific bit rate for the file being exported. The available bit rates change depending on the sample rate of the export, and the audio mix source stem width. Use CBR for the most predicable file size and sound quality.
• VBR does not offer a bit rate setting. Instead, it lets you choose a quality setting. The higher the quality, the longer the export time and the larger the file size will be. Use VBR for the best qual- ity-to-size ratio.
AAC export supports 44.1 kHz, 48 kHz, 88.2 kHz, and 96 kHz sample rates, and a single stem of au- dio.
PCM audio supports all sample rates, bit depths, and channel widths.

Satellite Link requires all linked systems to be con- nected to an Ethernet Local Area Network. All sys- tems should be on the same subnet. If possible, they should also be on the same Ethernet switch, to minimize response time over the network.

lowing system components:
